members.summary: Fully chromaticized formula using altered dominant 7th chords throughout; the final formula in the book.

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Diatonic Voice Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Each voice in a chord moves up (or down) to the next note of the major scale bearing the same chord-tone name, producing the next diatonic chord in the scale.

references.topic: Building a chord scale by stepwise voice motion
references.quote_excerpt: MOVING EACH NOTE IN THE CHORD UP TO THE NEXT NOTE

traversal_rules.name: Cycle-of-4ths Root Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Each chord root is a diatonic fourth higher than the previous root; the cycle is reducible to two alternating descending chord scales.

references.quote_excerpt: each chord root is a 4th higher than the previous
two chord scales descending, while they alternate with each other

traversal_rules.name: String Transference — Moving to Higher Strings
traversal_rules.summary: Relocate a chord voicing to the next higher string group: retain shape, raise any note landing on the 2nd string by one fret, then drop the whole chord five frets.

references.topic: String transference — moving to higher strings
references.quote_excerpt: move all the notes in the chord over to the next higher group of strings

traversal_rules.name: String Transference — Moving to Lower Strings
traversal_rules.summary: Relocate a chord voicing to the next lower string group: retain shape, lower any note landing on the 3rd string by one fret, then raise the whole chord five frets.

references.topic: String transference — moving to lower strings
references.quote_excerpt: move all the notes in the chord over to the next lower group of strings

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Fingerboard Exploration via Key Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Every harmonic formula must be explored across the entire fingerboard from multiple angles; key changes prevent monotony.

references.quote_excerpt: exploring the entire fingerboard from many different angles
changing the key can be like a breath of fresh air
practice each cycle in all octaves on each string set

traversal_rules.name: V7 to I Resolution (Standing Cadential Rule)
traversal_rules.summary: V7 chords must periodically resolve to I to maintain functional-harmonic grounding; rule recurs across chapters 7, 9, 10, 11.

references.quote_excerpt: You should always try resolving the V to I
Remember to resolve the V7's to I's occasionally
Remember to try resolving the V7's to I, occasionally
Don't forget to occasionally try resolving the V7's to I's

traversal_rules.name: Bidirectional Chord Scale Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Every chord scale must be practiced descending as well as ascending.

references.quote_excerpt: Remember to go down in all your chord scales, too

traversal_rules.name: Cycle Start-Degree Flexibility
traversal_rules.summary: Diatonic cycles and chord-scale patterns may begin on any scale degree; the interval motion defines the pattern, not the starting point.

references.quote_excerpt: common for the cycle to begin on other chords than the I
patterns using chord scales can start on any degree of a scale

modification_rules.name: Omission of 5th or Root Allowed
modification_rules.summary: The 5th or root may be omitted from any voicing; ear-appeal and smooth voice leading take priority over theoretical completeness.

references.quote_excerpt: missing tones are either the 5th or the root
many chord forms leave out 5ths or roots

modification_rules.name: Ear-Governed Register / Transposition
modification_rules.summary: Register and transposition decisions are governed by ear judgment; muddy low voicings are to be avoided.

references.quote_excerpt: let your ears be the guide in determining how low

modification_rules.name: Chord Scale Decoration (Delay / Moving Lines / Team Concept)
modification_rules.summary: Chord scales may be decorated by delaying notes, by moving lines built from diatonic scale tones, or by Van Eps's team concept of alternating two interesting intervals.

references.quote_excerpt: decorate them... such as: 1) delaying notes
finding two interesting intervals in a chord and alternating them

references.topic: Decoration — moving lines from diatonic tones
references.quote_excerpt: Using moving lines built from other diatonic scale tones

modification_rules.name: Voicing Note Omission for Physical Playability
modification_rules.summary: Notes may be omitted or changed when guitar-specific physical constraints make the full voicing impractical.

references.quote_excerpt: leave a note out of a chord due to the physical problem

modification_rules.name: Cycle Combination to Avoid Overuse
modification_rules.summary: The cycle of 4ths should be combined with other cycle patterns rather than used in isolation.

references.quote_excerpt: cycle of 4ths is a beautiful harmonic tool, but it can be monotonous if overused

systems.name: Dominant Substitution Lattice System
systems.summary: The book's secondary system governs how minor-seventh chords are replaced with dominant-seventh chords and how those dominants are then altered. The foundational rule — stated in capitals — is that any m7-type chord may be replaced with a dominant 7th according to personal taste. Greene teaches a specific derivation mechanism: raise the 3rd of a ii7 shape by one fret to produce II7; when two 3rds are present, raise the lower-pitched one, typically yielding II7#9. The same mechanism applies to converting vi7 to VI7. The system permits iii7 in place of III7 and vi7 in place of VI7 throughout the altered-dominant formula. Altered dominants — voicings incorporating #9, b9, #5, or b5 — are introduced only after diatonic preparation. A boundary condition limits the 3rd-raising mechanism: it does not apply when a iim7/11 chord has its 11th in the soprano.

members.name: Minor Seventh Type Chord (m7)
members.summary: Diatonic minor seventh chords (iii7, vi7, ii7); the source class for dominant substitution.

members.name: Dominant Seventh Type Chord — Diatonic Preparation
members.summary: Unaltered dominant seventh chords used before introducing altered dominants; labeled uppercase (II7, III7, VI7, V7).

members.name: Altered Dominant Seventh Type Chord
members.summary: Dominant seventh chords with #9, b9, #5, or b5 extensions; the chromatic end of the substitution lattice.

members.name: ii as Substitute for IV (Homonym Equivalence)
members.summary: ii can substitute for IV because they share the same tones (e.g., F#m7 = A6).

traversal_rules.name: Diatonic Preparation Before Altered Dominant Introduction
traversal_rules.summary: Altered dominant chords should be introduced only after preparing the ear with diatonic 'major scaley' sounds.

references.quote_excerpt: ear will accept altered chords more easily if prepared with sounds of a more normal, diatonic
combining the consonant and dissonant types of progressions

modification_rules.name: Dominant-for-Minor Substitution (Universal Rule)
modification_rules.summary: Any m7-type chord may be replaced with a dominant 7th-type chord according to personal taste.

references.quote_excerpt: ANY m7 TYPE CHORD MAY BE REPLACED WITH A DOMINANT 7th TYPE CHORD
E7 is replacing the normal Em7

modification_rules.name: 3rd-Raising Conversion: ii7 to II7 (and II7#9)
modification_rules.summary: Raise the 3rd of a ii7 voicing by one fret to convert it to II7; when two 3rds are present, raise the lower one, typically producing II7#9.

references.topic: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 derivation method
references.quote_excerpt: raise the 3rd (one fret higher) in the ii7 types, thereby making them II7's
raising the 3rd that is the lower in pitch... II7#9 chord

modification_rules.name: Soprano Exception: iim7/11 with 11th in Soprano
modification_rules.summary: The standard 3rd-raising conversion does not apply when a iim7/11 chord has its 11th as the highest voice.

references.quote_excerpt: iim7/11 with its 11th in the soprano then none of these ideas will work

modification_rules.name: Diatonic-for-Chromatic Substitution: iii7 for III7, vi7 for VI7
modification_rules.summary: In III7 VI7 II7 V7, iii7 may be used in place of III7 types and vi7 in place of VI7 types.

references.quote_excerpt: try using iii7 types in place of III7 types, or vi7 types in place of VI7 types

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Color Tone Introduction (#9, b9, #5, b5)
modification_rules.summary: Altered dominant voicings incorporate at least one of the four characteristic color tones (#9, b9, #5, b5).

references.quote_excerpt: altered dominants are those with #9's, b9's, #5's, and b5's
VI7 sounds good with any altered tone when used in smooth connection

modification_rules.name: ii Substitutes for IV (Homonym/Overtone Basis)
modification_rules.summary: ii7 may substitute for IV because they share the same tones; acoustically grounded in the overtone series.

references.quote_excerpt: ii can substitute for IV because they are virtually the same chord
based on the tones of the major chord which is part of Nature's overtone series

systems.name: Fingerboard Geography and Homonym Naming System
systems.summary: The book's organizational meta-system. Greene partitions the fingerboard into five position areas used as reference anchors for all major and minor chord forms. Within each position, chord voicings are memorized by three methods — shape-based, interval-based, and root-anchored — with experienced players combining all three eclectically. The concept of chord homonyms is central: any given physical voicing may function as multiple chords depending on harmonic context. The governing rule is that context and common sense determine naming. Mastery requires knowing all useful homonym names for every chord form. This system is the prerequisite infrastructure that enables the other two systems.

members.name: Neck Area 1
members.summary: One of the five fingerboard zones used as a positional anchor.

members.name: Neck Area 2
members.summary: Second of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 3
members.summary: Third of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 4
members.summary: Fourth of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 5
members.summary: Fifth of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Homonym Chord Form
members.summary: A chord voicing whose name depends on harmonic context (e.g., Em6 / C#m7b5 / A9).

traversal_rules.name: Five-Position Area Navigation
traversal_rules.summary: The five neck areas serve as spatial reference anchors; locate any chord form by identifying which area its root falls in.

references.topic: Five neck areas for chord memorization
references.quote_excerpt: Divide the neck up into five main areas for any major or minor chord

modification_rules.name: Homonym Naming by Harmonic Context
modification_rules.summary: When a chord form can carry multiple names, harmonic context of the surrounding progression determines the correct name.

references.quote_excerpt: naming of homonyms will be determined by what is easiest or just plain common sense
working with the progression Em Am... going to be called Em6
if you are working with Em7 A7 D... it will be called A9

modification_rules.name: All Useful Homonym Names Must Be Known
modification_rules.summary: A player must learn every useful name for each chord form to avoid long-term practical difficulty.

references.quote_excerpt: know all the useful names for a chord form or you will just be making it hard

modification_rules.name: Eclectic Combination of Three Memorization Methods
modification_rules.summary: Experienced players combine shape-based, interval-based, and root-anchored memorization; no single method suffices.

references.quote_excerpt: Most players use a combination of all three methods outlined here
Many guitar players learn new chords quickly by relating them visually

works.title: Jazz Guitar Single Note Soloing, Vol. 1
works.summary: Greene's single-note soloing primer (originally Dale F. Zdenek, 1978; reissued by Alfred Music). Seven conceptual chapters move from fingerboard fundamentals through chord-scale pairing to articulation and altered-dominant vocabulary. The book is notation-heavy — dense with scale diagrams, exercises, and line studies — with prose concentrated in the introductory and conceptual chapters (3-5 carry the densest pedagogical text). The three derived systems treat chord-scale pairing as the central improvisational architecture, fingerboard position and register as the spatial-coloristic axis, and rhythmic / articulation control as the executable-quality gate. Greene's characteristic moves — visualization-as-prerequisite, prohibition-against-shortcut, tempo-gating-by-execution-quality — appear throughout and produce 8 _pending: kinds that don't map cleanly to the existing engine 12.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Jazz Guitar Single Note Soloing, Vol. 1. Alfred Music (reissue of Dale F. Zdenek), 1978.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-greene-single-note-soloing-vol-1; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/ted-greene/single-note-soloing-vol-1/.

systems.name: Chord-Scale Pairing System
systems.summary: Central system of the book. Every chord type belongs to a named group, and each group is paired with one or more scales. The major scale is the universal reference; all other scales are defined as transformations of it. A chord 'takes' a scale when that scale sounds good over it.

traversal_rules.name: Run-to-Dominant-Group Synchronization
traversal_rules.summary: Runs containing the 3rd suit Group 1 dominants; runs containing the 11th suit Group 2 dominants; both/neither work over either group.

references.quote_excerpt: if a run contains the 3rd, use it over any of the group-1 dominants

traversal_rules.name: Root-Based Scale Internalization
traversal_rules.summary: Dominant 7th and Minor 7th scales must be internalized from their own root; the relative-major transposition shortcut is prohibited.

references.quote_excerpt: Don't fall for the 'oh, dom. 7th scales, they're really just major scales up a 4th-trap'

references.topic: Warning: don't think m7 as relative major
references.quote_excerpt: don't think of the m7 scale in this way now

traversal_rules.name: Overtone Dominant Categorical Boundary
traversal_rules.summary: The Overtone Dominant scale is categorically excluded from the altered dominant family; its #11 is a natural overtone, not a chromatic alteration.

references.quote_excerpt: The Overtone Dominant scale is not considered to be an altered dominant scale

traversal_rules.name: Altered Dominant Package-Deal Positional Learning
traversal_rules.summary: All three altered dominant types are presented juxtaposed in the same fingerboard position so their color differences can be absorbed comparatively.

references.quote_excerpt: absorb them all in a kind of 'package deal', one position at a time

modification_rules.name: Scale-to-Chord Compatibility Rule
modification_rules.summary: Any chord containing some combination of (and only) the chord tones of a given scale can take that scale.

references.quote_excerpt: Any chord containing some combination of any of (but only) these chord tones, can take the Dominant 7th scale

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Tone Requirement
modification_rules.summary: A scale qualifies as an altered dominant only if it contains at least one of: b9, #9, b5, or #5(b13).

references.quote_excerpt: any dominant 7th scale with one or more of the following tones: b9, #9, b5 or #5
Any Dom. 7th type chord that contains any one or more of the four altered tones

modification_rules.name: Type 3 Altered Dominant Chord Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Over Type 3 altered dominant exercises, A7+ may substitute for A7#9+ and D49 may substitute for D6/9.

references.quote_excerpt: you may wish to try A7+ for A7#9+ and D49 for D6/9

modification_rules.name: #9 Substitution or Addition Rule
modification_rules.summary: The altered #9 may substitute for or be played alongside the natural 9 in altered dominant contexts.

references.quote_excerpt: INSTEAD OF OR IN ADDITION TO THE #9

modification_rules.name: Chord Form Visualization Prerequisite
modification_rules.summary: When a player cannot visualize scalar runs, return to the chord forms for that position before proceeding.

references.quote_excerpt: go back and learn the chord forms (group 4) for this position

modification_rules.name: Lydian Dominant Upper-Structure Triad (Polytonality)
modification_rules.summary: The 9, #11, and 13 of the Lydian Dominant scale form a major triad rooted a whole step above the chord root — the upper-structure triad of polytonal Lydian Dominant color.

references.quote_excerpt: the 9, #11 and 13 form a major triad whose root is a whole step above the root of the scale
This type of thinking and sound is commonly known as POLYTONALITY

systems.name: Fingerboard Position and Register System
systems.summary: Governs physical navigation. Position continuity keeps the player in a 5-6 fret area across chord changes. Fingerboard visualization is the primary internalization practice. Register is a compositional variable.

traversal_rules.name: Stay-in-Position Multi-Chord Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Know enough positions of each scale to remain within a 5-6 fret area across all chords in a progression.

references.quote_excerpt: stay in one little 5 or 6 fret area and play through the whole song

traversal_rules.name: Alternate Position Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Each set of runs should also be fingered in at least one alternate position.

references.quote_excerpt: Try fingering all these Em9 runs in the 5th position too

traversal_rules.name: Octave-Higher Same-Position Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Every pattern should be tried an octave higher within the same fingerboard position.

references.quote_excerpt: Also try this run an octave higher in the same position

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio String-Set Flexibility
traversal_rules.summary: Arpeggio tones may be voiced on alternate strings (e.g., 5th & 4th strings).

references.quote_excerpt: These notes may be played on other strings too (5th & 4th strings)

modification_rules.name: Register Governs Extension Color
modification_rules.summary: The perceptual weight or spice of b7 and 9 is determined by register.

references.quote_excerpt: b7 and 9 really start to come alive in this 'higher' register

modification_rules.name: Fingerboard Visualization as Primary Practice
modification_rules.summary: Visualizing the notes on the fingerboard is identified as the single most important technique for internalizing scales.

references.quote_excerpt: VISUALIZE the notes on the fingerboard... THIS IS VERY IMPORTANT

modification_rules.name: Type 3 Area-4A Structural Boundary
modification_rules.summary: No Type 3 altered dominant material exists for fingerboard Area 4A; an instrument-specific constraint.

references.quote_excerpt: There won't be any Type 3 - 4A.. it's just the way the instrument works out

systems.name: Rhythmic and Articulation System
systems.summary: Governs time and texture: the 8th note is the baseline jazz unit; swing feel transforms straight 8ths into triplet subdivisions; double-timing introduces 16ths; trills decorate rather than substitute; clean playing must precede tempo increases.

traversal_rules.name: 8th Note as Primary Jazz Unit
traversal_rules.summary: The eighth note is jazz's normal rhythmic unit at medium and fast tempos.

references.quote_excerpt: the normal unit of rhythm in jazz is the 8th note

traversal_rules.name: Both-Feels Practice Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Every 8th-note run in the book must be practiced in both swing feel and straight feel.

references.quote_excerpt: all the 8th notes in this book should be played as jazz 8ths and straight 8ths

traversal_rules.name: Clean Playing Before Tempo Increase
traversal_rules.summary: Tempo may only be increased after clean execution is achieved at the current tempo.

references.quote_excerpt: making sure you are playing cleanly at any tempo before moving on to a faster one

traversal_rules.name: Trill Placement on Strong Beat
traversal_rules.summary: The trill is more commonly placed on the strong part of the beat than the weak.

references.quote_excerpt: The trill is more commonly used on the strong part of the beat than the weak one

modification_rules.name: Trill as Decoration, Not Substitution
modification_rules.summary: A trill does not replace the decorated note; the underlying note is held and enhanced.

references.quote_excerpt: A is not really being replaced, but instead, is just being decorated

modification_rules.name: Hammer-On Scale-Tone Constraint
modification_rules.summary: At this stage, hammer-on targets must be drawn from the scale currently in use.

references.quote_excerpt: This higher note, for now, will be found in the scale you are working with at that moment

modification_rules.name: Double-Timing as Texture Escalation
modification_rules.summary: Double-timing (16th notes) is an advanced texture layer above baseline 8th-note texture.

references.quote_excerpt: Players who possess great technique often love to break into 16th notes at medium tempos

masters.name: Joe Pass
masters.biography: Bebop guitarist whose solo recordings (Virtuoso series, 1973-1983) reset expectations for unaccompanied jazz guitar. Combined walking bass lines with chord stabs and single-note runs from held chord positions.

principles.name: Walking bass plus chord stabs
principles.summary: Bass walks on every beat (chord tones, passing notes between chord tones, chord-extension notes, tritone approaches to the root); chord 'stabs' land on off-beats with 2-3 note voicings rather than full block chords. The texture blends 'walking bass, inner voices, and lead lines into a single texture.' Voicings must keep the bass string accessible.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Style
references.citation: Pass, Joe and Bill Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn Publishing, 1971.

references.source: jazz-guitar-licks.com — Joe Pass walking-bass transcription
references.citation: Documents the walking-bass mechanics: chord tones + passing notes + chord-extension notes + tritone approaches.

principles.name: Drop-2 and drop-3 chord-melody harmonization
principles.summary: Pass's typical chord-melody technique harmonizes the melody with drop-2 and drop-3 voicings. Drop-2 keeps melody on top of a 4-note voicing on adjacent strings; drop-3 spreads the voicing wider with a 'skip' in the middle. The melody is on the top string of the voicing; the chord tones fill in below.

references.source: drdavewalkerblog — Joe Pass Solo Jazz Guitar
references.citation: Documents Pass's typical chord-melody technique as drop-2 and drop-3 harmonization.

principles.name: Harmonic restraint — don't reharmonize every note
principles.summary: Pass's published advice: 'You don't need a chord for every note' — add color through alterations rather than constant reharmonization. On ii-V-I, he often bypasses the ii ('forget the II — focus on the V'). The aesthetic privileges voice-leading economy over harmonic density. 'You don't have to play bass, chord, melody all the time — dip in and out between all three.'

references.source: Sam Blakelock — What Was Joe Pass's Approach to Solo Jazz Guitar?
references.citation: Quotes Pass: 'You don't need a chord for every note,' 'forget the II,' 'dip in and out between all three.'

principles.name: Single-note runs from chord shapes
principles.summary: Improvise melodically by playing single notes drawn from the chord shape currently held. The held shape acts as a position anchor; soloing fingers move between the chord tones without requiring a separate scale framework. Underpins his single-note-to-chord transitions in solo arrangements.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Style
references.citation: Pass and Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn, 1971.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 4: 'With the Chord Forms acting as points of anchor or reference, the ability to create an improvised scale or melody will be unhampered by the struggle to find the notes.'

principles.name: Six Chord Forms as positional substrate
principles.summary: Pass's framework divides the fingerboard into six overlapping positional regions named for the chord shape that anchors each one — C, A, G, E, D, and Intermediate Forms — each spanning roughly five to six frets. Choosing a voicing is therefore also choosing a position: the player picks the Form whose chord shape lets the melody, bass, and inner voices sit inside one hand position. This is the spatial substrate that makes the walking-bass-plus-chord-stabs and runs-from-chord-shapes principles physically executable.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 4: 'Below are six diagrams which are usually learned by all guitarists when beginning to play in first position [...]. All scales are played vertically within each form whose object is to provide a framework in which to function.' The six forms enumerated are C, A, G, E, D (1st inversion of C), and Intermediate (top part of C).

principles.name: Three- to four-note voicings linked by voice-leading
principles.summary: For unaccompanied solo work Pass deliberately reduces voicings to three or four sounding notes and connects successive chords through common tones or stepwise inner-voice motion. Full six-string block chords are avoided as the default. The principle complements drop-2/drop-3 chord-melody — drop voicings supply the shape, this principle constrains the density and the linkage between adjacent chords. Engine signal: floor voicings at three sounding notes; rank candidates that share tones with the previous voicing higher.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 30, head note to 'Blues for Nina': 'All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords. And for better movement, voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them. Nobs contains several examples of this technique.' The 'EXAMPLES OF VOICE LEADING' panel on p. 31 demonstrates the principle with the sequence Dm9 → C13 → C9(b5) → C9(6)/C7 → Fmaj7.

principles.name: Dominant alteration and tritone substitution lattice
principles.summary: A written 'simple' dominant chord licenses an entire family of altered and extended substitutes — 9, 13, 9(6), 11, +9, b9, +5, b5 — plus the tritone substitute and its altered family. In the *Joe Pass Guitar Method* this is stated as an explicit rule for the whole 'Blues for Nina / Nobs / Alison / Grete' set: any of these alterations 'may be substituted for simple Dominants.' On Joe's Blues, plain G7 expands to G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9); E7 expands to E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or the tritone family Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13. Engine signal: when ranking voicings against a notated dominant, the altered/extended family and the tritone substitute should both be eligible candidates carrying Pass's style tag.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734). 'Joe's Blues' head note, p. 25, enumerates substitutes for G7 (G13/G9/G9(6)/G7+9/G7(b9)), Bm7 in bar 8 (F13/F9/F+9/Fm9, or B7/B9/B13), and E7 (E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or the tritone-substitute family Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13/Bb9). 'Blues for Nina' head note, p. 30: 'Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants.'

systems.name: Six Chord Forms (Position System)
systems.summary: Pass partitions the fretboard into six overlapping positional regions, each anchored on a chord shape: C, A, G, E, D, and Intermediate (top part of C Form). Each Form occupies ~5-6 frets, defines a scale-fingering window, and serves as the spatial reference for chord-melody and improvisation work. Position is identified by which Form anchors the hand; movement between Forms uses pivot-finger shifts.

members.name: C Form
members.summary: Anchor at C major shape; canonical pivot for Pass's chord-melody.

members.name: D Form
members.summary: First inversion of C Form.

members.name: Intermediate Form
members.summary: Top part of C Form; secondary.

traversal_rules.name: Pivot-finger shift to next Form
traversal_rules.summary: To move between Forms, use the 1st or 4th finger to play two consecutive notes; the second note lands the hand in the next Form.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: ch5-1, p. 19: 'one can shift to any string by using either the first finger or fourth finger to play two notes. This will place the hand on the next Chord Form.'

traversal_rules.name: Same-Form continuity preferred
traversal_rules.summary: While the musical context permits, stay in one Form across consecutive voicings to keep scale fingerings and melody-line landing positions consistent.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Implicit across pp. 4-21; explicit cross-ref ch8-3 p. 23: 'When playing a Dominant 7th Chord or Scale, one Chord Form must be kept in mind.'

preferences.name: Chord-form preference order
preferences.summary: Pass mentions A, E, C, D as the four core Forms; G and Intermediate are secondary. A and E come first in his enumeration.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 6: 'By mastering all the scales in the A — E — C/D Chord Forms, which are basically similar, the student will have covered the fingerboard quite thoroughly.'

systems.name: Three Harmonic Families (Major / Minor / Dominant)
systems.summary: Pass partitions all chord qualities into three families that share voicing-pool characteristics. Major covers maj6/maj7/maj9/maj13/6-9; Minor covers m6/m7/m9/m-maj7/m11; Dominant covers dom7/dom9/dom13 and ALL altered/extended dominants, with diminished and augmented as subspecies of Dominant. Within a family, voicing-level substitution is free (a written maj7 admits maj6, maj9, maj13, 6/9 with no harmonic re-analysis).

members.name: Major family
members.summary: Covers maj6, maj7, maj9, maj13, 6/9.

members.name: Minor family
members.summary: Covers m6, m7, m9, m-maj7, m11.

members.name: Dominant family
members.summary: Covers dom7/9/13 and all altered/extended dominants; diminished and augmented are subspecies.

traversal_rules.name: Family-coherent comping preferred
traversal_rules.summary: When traversing a chord progression, voicings drawn from the same family share recognition cues; mixing families is fine but is itself a compositional choice.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 16: 'the underlying chord sequence for this study should be analyzed, remembering that it can be broken down into three simple chord types: Major, Minor, and Dominant 7th (Aug./Dim.).'

modification_rules.name: Within-family substitution is free
modification_rules.summary: A written maj7 admits maj6, maj9, maj13, 6/9 as voicing-level substitutes — same family, same listener recognition.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 16 + cross-reference Complete Chord Melody Module 2 which credits this concept to Pass by name.

systems.name: Voicing Density and Voice Leading
systems.summary: Pass treats voicing-internal density and inter-voicing motion as coupled. Solo work runs on 3-4 sounding notes; walking-bass texture drops to 2-3-note stabs; 5-6-note block chords are the avoided default. Voice motion preferences are common-tone first, stepwise inner as fallback.

traversal_rules.name: Common-tone preferred, stepwise inner as fallback
traversal_rules.summary: Voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them; stepwise inner-voice motion is the fallback when no common tone is available.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30: 'voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them.' Worked example pp. 31-32 (Dm9 → C13 → C7(b5) → C9(6)/C7 → Fmaj7) demonstrates per-transition shared tones.

modification_rules.name: Density ceiling — 4 sounding notes in solo work
modification_rules.summary: Five- or six-note block chords are NOT Pass's default for solo work.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30: 'All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords.'

modification_rules.name: Density floor — 3 sounding notes in solo work
modification_rules.summary: Pass's solo arrangements use at least 3 sounding notes; bare 2-note voicings are walking-bass territory, not solo.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 same passage: 'three- or four-note chords' establishes 3 as the floor for solo contexts.

modification_rules.name: Walking-bass texture with 2-note chord stabs
modification_rules.summary: Bass walks every beat; chord stabs land off-beats with 2-3-note voicings rather than full block chords.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Joe's Blues worked example pp. 25-29; corroborated by PR #239 walking-bass-plus-chord-stabs principle (originally from Joe Pass Guitar Style 1971).

modification_rules.name: Drop-2 and Drop-3 are the default chord-melody shapes
modification_rules.summary: Method's pp. 31-32 worked examples are predominantly drop-2 shapes; drop-3 is the secondary chord-melody shape.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: pp. 31-32 worked examples; explicit drop-2 / drop-3 terminology elsewhere in Pass's secondary literature.

preferences.name: Voice-motion preference order
preferences.summary: Pass's explicit preference: common-tone first, then stepwise inner, then leap, parallel last (avoided).

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 same passage as R3.1.

systems.name: Harmonic Restraint (Three-Textures Cycling)
systems.summary: The question of whether to harmonize a given melody note at all. Pass cycles through three textures within one solo arrangement — harmonize, leave-as-single-note, dip-out-to-bass-only — weaving rather than maintaining a single texture. Color comes from chord alteration (System 5), not from harmonizing every note.

traversal_rules.name: Cycle between bass/chord/melody textures within an arrangement
traversal_rules.summary: The arrangement weaves between three textures rather than maintaining one. Per Pass's own framing: 'You don't have to play bass, chord, melody all the time — dip in and out between all three.'

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Secondary-source quote of Pass, corroborated by Method's improvisation chapters where single-note lines dominate page-count.

modification_rules.name: Don't harmonize every note (low NCT-harmonization appetite)
modification_rules.summary: Pass's restraint: 'You don't need a chord for every note.' Solo arrangements deliberately leave melodic NCT passages as bare single-note lines.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Sam Blakelock interview quoting Pass; corroborated by Method's improvisation chapters' page-count dominance of single-note lines over chord-density.

modification_rules.name: Bypass the ii in ii-V-I — focus on the V
modification_rules.summary: Pass's specific habit of leaving the ii bar as a single-note line and harmonizing only the V resolution. Functional shorthand: the V carries the ii's information.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Pass quote: 'Forget the II — focus on the V.' Secondary-source corroborated; Method's worked examples consistently apply this in ii-V passages.

preferences.name: Low NCT-harmonization appetite
preferences.summary: Lower than the bookshelf default; encodes Pass's restraint distinct from Greene's aggressive harmonization.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Same Blakelock-quoted passage as R4.2.

systems.name: Dominant Substitution Lattice
systems.summary: Pass's richest system and the engine-active one. Any written dominant chord admits an expansion-set of related qualities (dom9, dom13, dom7+9, dom7b9, dom7+5, dom7b5, dom7b13, dom-alt). Tritone-related pairs and diminished-symmetry quartets group roots; color tones (b9, +9, b5, +5) are baseline-not-optional even when 'playing the written changes'.

members.name: Tritone-related root pair (C7 ↔ F#7)
members.summary: Twelve canonical pairs covering all roots.

members.name: Diminished-symmetry root quartet
members.summary: {D7, F7, Ab7, B7}, {Db7, E7, G7, Bb7}, {C7, Eb7, Gb7, A7} — three quartets cover all twelve.

traversal_rules.name: Tritone substitution carries color alteration through
traversal_rules.summary: E7 admits Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13 — same alteration palette on the tritone-related root. The symmetric-pair member carries the same color tones.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 25 (ch9-2): 'Bm7 can be replaced by F13/F9/F+9/Fm9. Bm7 can be changed to a Dominant 7th (B7) or B9/B13.'

modification_rules.name: Tune-wide alteration permission
modification_rules.summary: Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants. This rule licenses substitution across the entire piece, not just one chord position.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 (ch10-4): 'Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants.'

modification_rules.name: Color tones (b9, +9, b5, +5) are baseline in dom7 lines, not optional
modification_rules.summary: Even when playing the written changes, Pass includes a color tone (b9, +9, b5, or +5) in his dom7 lines as the baseline expectation, not as an embellishment.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 9 (ch1-6): 'Against almost any Dominant 7th Chord, I will include the b9, +9, b5, or +5 in my lines.'

preferences.name: High substitution aggressiveness
preferences.summary: Pass's appetite for substitution is high — R5.3's 'throughout, may be substituted' + ch9-4 'Students must be able to choose freely from the alternatives' both signal aggressive expansion.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: pp. 25, 30; ch9-4 'choose freely from the alternatives'.

works.title: The Joe Pass Guitar Method
works.summary: Joe Pass guitar method published 1977 by Chappell. 34-page method organizing scalar and harmonic instruction around the Chord Forms (A, E, C, D, G, Intermediate) as fingerboard navigational anchors. Covers scale-to-chord pairing rules (diminished scales over Dom7; whole-tone scales over Dom7+5), dominant flexibility and tritone substitution, voicing economy (3-4 note reduction with stepwise or common-tone voice leading), and four composition studies (Joe's Blues, Blues for Nina, NOBS-Alison-Grete) as applied laboratories. Ear-verification — that learned material must pass through the ear before application — is the non-negotiable governor throughout.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co. (1977). 34 pages.
references.citation: Primary source extracted via #297 master-distillation pipeline; promoted under #347 batch-1.

systems.name: Chord Forms Navigation System
systems.summary: The central navigational system of the Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chord Forms (A, E, C, D, G, and Intermediate) serve as vertical anchors on the fingerboard, providing a framework within which scalar and melodic material becomes automatic. The system defines six Form members as position-regions, specifies how the player moves between them via pivot-finger shifts (1st or 4th finger), mandates practice across all Forms in both chromatic and Form order, and requires every scale-line exercise to begin and end with the parent chord voicing so that melody is always tethered to harmony. Mastery of A–E–C/D Forms is prioritized as the most efficient path to full fingerboard coverage. The ear-verification rule — that learned patterns must pass through the ear before application — is the non-negotiable governor of the entire system.

members.name: A Chord Form
members.summary: One of the three primary Forms covering the fingerboard thoroughly.

members.name: E Chord Form
members.summary: One of the three primary Forms; structurally similar to A Form.

members.name: C Chord Form
members.summary: Primary Form; related to D Form.

members.name: D Chord Form
members.summary: Structurally equivalent to C Form at a different register.

members.name: G Chord Form
members.summary: Contains note-choice decisions (B on d or g string).

members.name: Intermediate Chord Form
members.summary: Sixth Form; scale practice exception (no root-to-root requirement).

traversal_rules.name: Pivot-Finger Position Shift
traversal_rules.summary: Use the 1st or 4th finger to play two notes when shifting from one Chord Form position to the next.

references.quote_excerpt: shift to any string by using either the first finger or fourth finger to play two notes

traversal_rules.name: Form-Order and Chromatic-Order Practice Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Traverse all Forms either in chromatic key order within a single Form, or in Form order (C–A–G–E–Intermediate–D) for a given key, covering two octaves per scale.

references.quote_excerpt: practice in all Forms either chromatically or in Form order

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Frame Scale-Line Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Every scale-line exercise must begin and end with the parent chord voicing, anchoring melodic traversal to its harmonic origin.

references.quote_excerpt: chords on which these scale lines are based should be played at the beginning and ending of each exercise

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Half-Step Neck Extension
traversal_rules.summary: Extend chromatic patterns up the neck by half-steps, moving through successive Form positions.

references.quote_excerpt: starting with open E, then moving up by half-steps to extend the exercise

modification_rules.name: Scale Extrapolation Across All Forms
modification_rules.summary: Any scale given in abbreviated form must be repeated by the student in all six Forms with the same fingering.

references.quote_excerpt: These scales should be repeated in all Forms; the fingering is the same

modification_rules.name: A–E–C/D Form Priority Rule
modification_rules.summary: Prioritize mastery of A, E, and C/D Forms first; together they cover the fingerboard thoroughly.

references.quote_excerpt: By mastering all the scales in the A — E — C/D Chord Forms, which are basically similar, the student will have covered the fingerboard quite thoroughly

modification_rules.name: Ear-Verification Rule
modification_rules.summary: All learned material must pass through the ear before being applied; mechanical application without listening is prohibited.

references.quote_excerpt: the student is cautioned to use his ears

references.topic: Music from the Head, Not Fingers
references.quote_excerpt: music must come from the head, not simply from the fingers running up and down the neck

modification_rules.name: Dominant 7th Flat-Seventh Form Rule
modification_rules.summary: When improvising on a Dominant 7th, anchor to the Basic Chord Form and simply flat the seventh tone to reduce mistakes.

references.quote_excerpt: By constantly using and thinking in terms of the Basic Chord Forms and simply flatting the seventh tone

systems.name: Dominant Flexibility and Substitution System
systems.summary: This system governs how the player selects, alters, and substitutes dominant chords across the blues and jazz repertoire taught in the method. Its members are the dominant chord quality families: plain dominant 7th, extended dominants, altered dominants, augmented dominant, tritone substitute family, and compound altered dominants. Traversal rules describe scale-to-dominant pairings (diminished scales for dom7, whole-tone scales for dom7+5). Modification rules define interchangeability of extended and altered dominants with plain dominants, tritone substitution (E7 ↔ Bb9 family), and compound altered dominants for chromatic voice movement. Free ear-driven choice among available substitutions is the governing principle.

members.name: Plain Dominant 7th
members.summary: Base dominant chord; e.g. G7, C7, D7.

members.name: Extended Dominants
members.summary: Dominant chords with added 9/11/13/9(6); e.g. G9, G13, G11.

members.name: Altered Dominants
members.summary: Dominant chords with b5, +5, +9, b9; e.g. G7(+9), G7(b9).

members.name: Augmented Dominant (Dom7+5)
members.summary: Dominant chord exclusively paired with whole-tone scale.

members.name: Tritone Substitute Family
members.summary: Chords a tritone away from the target dominant; e.g. Bb9 family for E7.

members.name: Compound Altered Dominant
members.summary: Combined altered extensions for chromatic resolution; e.g. G7(+5/b9) to C9(6).

traversal_rules.name: Diminished Scale Exclusive Dom7 Pairing
traversal_rules.summary: The three diminished scales are played exclusively against Dominant 7th chords.

references.quote_excerpt: are all to be played against Dominant 7th Chords

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Tone Scale Dom7+5 Pairing
traversal_rules.summary: The two whole-tone scales fit exclusively with Dominant 7+5 chords.

references.quote_excerpt: There are but two Whole Tone Scales. They will fit with all Dominant 7 +5 Chords

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Derived Melodic Line Generation
traversal_rules.summary: Melodic patterns grow out of the chord at the start of each line; lines are chord-first, not scale-first.

references.quote_excerpt: melodic/scale patterns, in the following examples, grow out of the chords at the beginning of each line

modification_rules.name: Extended/Altered Dominant Free Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Any of +9, b9, 13, 9(6), 11, +5, b5 extensions may be freely substituted for a plain dominant chord.

references.quote_excerpt: +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/-+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution Rule
modification_rules.summary: E7 and the Bb9 family are interchangeable; tritone-equivalent dominants substitute freely.

references.quote_excerpt: E7 can be replaced with E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or Bb 9/Bb+9/Bb m9/Bb13/Bb9

modification_rules.name: Tonic Dominant Color Variant Palette
modification_rules.summary: For the tonic dominant (G7), any of G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9) may be used as color substitutions.

references.quote_excerpt: variants may be used in place of G7: G13, G9, G9(6), G7 +9, G7(b9)

modification_rules.name: Compound Altered Dominant Chromatic Resolution
modification_rules.summary: Compound altered dominants (G7+5/b9) resolve to extended tonics (C9(6)) for chromatic voice movement.

references.quote_excerpt: G7(+5/b9) —-C9(6) (c) Fmaj7 voice leading

modification_rules.name: Turnaround Bar Pivot Substitution
modification_rules.summary: In bar 8, Bm7 may be replaced with F-family dominants or converted to B7/B9/B13 in the second half of the bar.

references.quote_excerpt: In bar 8 Bm7 can be replaced by F13/F9/F +9/Fm9

modification_rules.name: Ear-Driven Harmonic Choice Verification
modification_rules.summary: Students freely choose from available alternatives and verify each improvised phrase against the chord they have chosen.

references.topic: Free choice in chording and improvisation
references.quote_excerpt: Students must be able to choose freely from the alternatives available

systems.name: Voicing Economy and Voice-Leading System
systems.summary: This system governs how chords are voiced and connected across a progression. Members are voicing-density categories (three-note, four-note, thick/rejected) and the harmonic-function chord types (tonic, subdominant, supertonic ii, dominant V, substituted). Two complementary rules govern movement: voicings must connect by stepwise voice motion or share a common tone, and all chord changes must be reduced to three or four notes. Demonstrated in NOBS (Ch. 10) and applied in Alison (Ch. 11) where F13, Bbm7, and Ebmaj7 substitute into the C7–F7–Bbmaj7–Eb backbone, and the ii–V–I with altered dominant extensions (Dm7–G7(13)/G7(+5)–Cmaj7) serves as the core template.

members.name: Three-Note Voicing
members.summary: Minimum density; preferred economy voicing.

members.name: Four-Note Voicing
members.summary: Maximum permitted density under the economy rule.

members.name: Thick/Dense Voicing (Rejected)
members.summary: Voicings exceeding four notes; explicitly prohibited.

members.name: Tonic Chord (I)
members.summary: Root-function chord; e.g. Cmaj7, Bbmaj7, C9(6).

members.name: Subdominant Chord (IV)
members.summary: Fourth-function chord; e.g. F7, Bbmaj7, Ebmaj7.

members.name: Supertonic Chord (ii)
members.summary: Second-function chord; e.g. Dm7, Dm9.

members.name: Dominant Chord (V)
members.summary: Fifth-function chord including altered forms.

members.name: Substituted Harmony
members.summary: Chord substituting for a functional root; e.g. F13 over C7.

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Motion or Common-Tone Connection
traversal_rules.summary: Voicings must either lead into one another through stepwise motion or share a common tone.

references.quote_excerpt: voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them

traversal_rules.name: ii–V–I Altered Dominant Voice-Leading Template
traversal_rules.summary: The ii–V–I with altered dominant extensions is the core voice-leading template.

references.quote_excerpt: Dm7 G7(13) G7(+5) Cmaj7 or Cm9 voice leading

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Voicing Substitution Over Backbone
traversal_rules.summary: Substituted voicings move over the underlying functional backbone (C7–F7–Bbmaj7–Eb).

references.quote_excerpt: (C7) (F7) (Bbmaj7) (Eb)

modification_rules.name: Four-Note Density Ceiling
modification_rules.summary: All chord changes must be reduced to three or four notes; no voicing may exceed this density.

references.quote_excerpt: All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Color-Tone Requirement for Voice Leading
modification_rules.summary: In the ii–V–I template, the dominant must carry at least one altered extension to generate chromatic voice motion.

masters.name: Martin Taylor
masters.biography: Scottish virtuoso whose solo fingerstyle approach extends the Django Reinhardt and Joe Pass traditions with greater fluidity in thumb-bass / chord / melody independence. Played a decade with Stéphane Grappelli; authored Solo Jazz Guitar (Mel Bay), Single Note Soloing (Mel Bay), the Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar DVD with Grossman's Guitar Workshop, and runs the Martin Taylor Guitar Academy with extensive online lessons. Cites Bill Evans and Art Tatum as keyboard influences for melody-bass separation.

principles.name: Alternating thumb-bass strokes
principles.summary: The right-hand thumb alternates between the 6th and 5th strings (or 6th/4th depending on chord) to maintain continuous bass support without interrupting melodic flow. Demonstrated in Taylor's '4 Bar 4 Shapes' etude: thumb keeps a walking pulse while the fingers play melody and chord stabs above. The thumb is mechanically independent of the fingers — neither waits for the other.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor (guitarist)
references.citation: Documents 'alternating thumb strokes between the 6th and 5th strings to maintain rhythmic drive, as demonstrated in etudes like 4 Bar 4 Shapes.'

principles.name: Melody-bass separation (Tatum/Evans-derived)
principles.summary: Clear textural separation between melody (top voice) and bass (thumb), with chord work as a third middle layer. Taylor cites Bill Evans and Art Tatum as keyboard influences for this approach — the goal is to sound like two players, not one. Distinguishes itself from Django by greater independence between layers rather than Django's parallel-voicings style.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'A clear melody bass separation and crisp chordal sophistication inspired by his keyboard heroes, Bill Evans and Art Tatum.'

principles.name: Internal dynamics across voices
principles.summary: Taylor's own term: 'internal dynamics' — don't treat every note the same. Bass gets a specific touch (often softer, percussive); melody comes out louder; chord stabs sit in the middle. Voicings are selected partly for whether they support this dynamic stratification — voicings that put bass on a louder open string aren't useful if the bass needs to recede.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'When playing fingerstyle guitar, he doesn't treat every note the same, giving them different sound textures and balance.'

principles.name: Natural and artificial harmonics in arrangement
principles.summary: Taylor incorporates both natural harmonics (open strings at fret-12 / fret-7 nodes) and artificial harmonics (right-hand harp-harmonic technique, related to Chet Atkins / Lenny Breau) as melodic colors. Voicings designed to terminate on or transition to a harmonic exploit the upper-string register that fretted notes can't access.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'Right-hand fingerstyle techniques, natural and artificial harmonics, walking bass lines, double thumbing bass lines, single note improvisation, playing melodies in and out of tempo.'

systems.name: Alternating-Thumb Bass (Patterned)
systems.summary: Right-hand thumb plays bass in alternating-string pattern (boom-chick-style, Reinhardt + folk-fingerstyle derived). Distinct from Breau's three-voice (Taylor's is more rhythmically regular) and Pass's walking-bass (Taylor's is patterned, not improvised).

members.name: Root + 5th alternation on adjacent low strings
Thumb walks in passing-tone contexts (borrows from Pass)

traversal_rules.name: Thumb alternates between root and 5th of the chord
traversal_rules.summary: Boom-chick pattern on adjacent low strings.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #224 alternating-thumb-bass principle.

modification_rules.name: Substitute alternating-thumb-bass for static-root bass in solo arrangements
modification_rules.summary: Default bass texture for Taylor-active solo contexts.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Beyond Chord Melody series.

systems.name: Strict Melody-Bass Separation
systems.summary: Tatum/Bill-Evans-derived pianistic split — melody on treble strings (fingers), bass on bass strings (thumb), chord stabs fill the middle. Melody and bass move independently in time AND direction; contrary motion common.

traversal_rules.name: Melody and bass move independently
traversal_rules.summary: Contrary motion is common.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Tatum/Bill-Evans-derived pianistic split.

modification_rules.name: Prefer voicings allowing simultaneous melody-on-top + bass-on-bottom with clear separation
modification_rules.summary: 3-voice or wider density.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Voice-separation constraint.

systems.name: Internal Voice Dynamics
systems.summary: Dynamic variation BETWEEN voices in the same voicing — bass louder, melody softer, chord stabs medium. Internal dynamic profile of a voicing is itself a compositional choice. Likely a playback affordance rather than a voicing selector.

modification_rules.name: Mark voicings with a dynamic_profile per voice
modification_rules.summary: Engine produces a chosen profile per voicing.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Lower v1 priority; playback affordance.

systems.name: Harmonics Vocabulary
systems.summary: Natural + artificial harmonics as melodic resource. Used for chord coloring and melodic statements at structural points (intros, codas, melodic peaks) — not just timbral decoration.

modification_rules.name: Substitute harmonic for fingered note where timbre suits
modification_rules.summary: Related to Breau's harp-harmonics — possibly subsumed under one harmonics-vocabulary payload.

references.source: plans/taylor-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Generalizes Breau's harp-harmonics use case.

masters.name: Wes Montgomery
masters.biography: Self-taught Indianapolis guitarist whose innovations after 1959 reshaped jazz guitar: thumb-attack right hand, parallel-octave melodic statements, and block-chord chorus climaxes built directly from melodic shape. Active recording career 1959-1968 (Riverside, Verve, A&M) with canonical statements on Smokin' at the Half Note (1965) and The Incredible Jazz Guitar of Wes Montgomery (1960). No instructional book of his own; documentation is via transcription collections and biographical studies.

principles.name: Three-tier chorus development
principles.summary: A solo arcs through three distinct textural tiers: single-note lines (statement), parallel octaves (intensification), block-chord harmonization (climax). Each tier carries the same melodic logic; the chord tier is melody-led, not chord-led — the top voice of the chord IS the melody, with chord tones filled in below. The arc is documented across nearly every extended Wes solo from 'West Coast Blues' onward.

references.source: The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio (Hal Leonard)
references.citation: Montgomery, Wes. The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio. Hal Leonard, posthumous transcriptions.

references.source: Adrian Ingram, Wes Montgomery (Centerstream)
references.citation: Ingram, Adrian. Wes Montgomery. Centerstream Publications, 2008. Biographical study with technique analysis.

references.source: Smokin' at the Half Note (1965)
references.citation: Recorded reference: 'Four on Six,' 'No Blues' — canonical three-tier choruses.

principles.name: Octave-passage construction
principles.summary: Parallel octaves played between non-adjacent strings — strings 6 and 4, 5 and 3, 4 and 2, or 3 and 1 depending on position. The thumb mutes the intervening string while striking both target strings. Octave passages are melodically motivated lines, not just doubled single-note lines: pitch contour and rhythm match the surrounding tier's logic.

references.source: Steve Khan, Pat Martino: The Nature of Guitar (analysis chapter on Wes)
references.citation: Khan, Steve. Analyses across multiple Wes solo transcriptions. The thumb-muting mechanism is documented technique.

principles.name: Block-chord drop-2 predominance
principles.summary: Wes's block-chord soloing uses drop-2 voicings as the primary vehicle — voicings on adjacent strings with the second-from-top voice dropped an octave. The melody lives on the top string of the voicing; chord tones fill in below. Voice-leading between adjacent block chords is typically smooth in the top voice with parallel inner motion.

references.source: Voicelid Jazz Guitar — Drop-2 + Upper Structures
references.citation: Documents drop-2 as 'the vehicle for these ideas' and notes Wes 'stuck with chords played mainly on adjacent strings and drop 2 chord shapes.'

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Wes Montgomery chord soloing
references.citation: Indexed across the 40-licks article and Wes Montgomery chord-solo lesson.

principles.name: Thumb-attack articulation
principles.summary: Right-hand technique uses the thumb only — no plectrum and no multi-finger fingerstyle. The thumb downstrokes single notes, brushes adjacent strings for octaves, and rakes across the chord for block voicings. The resulting tone is warmer and less defined than a pick attack; the constraint also influences voicing choice — block chords with the bass on the lower strings are thumb-friendly, while sweeps across all six strings are not.

references.source: Adrian Ingram, Wes Montgomery (Centerstream)
references.citation: Ingram chapter on right-hand technique — the calluses on Wes's thumb are documented in interviews.

principles.name: Upper-structure triad substitution
principles.summary: Over a ii7-V7 (or single dominant), Wes voices an upper-structure chord that omits the root: e.g., a DbMaj7 shape over Bbm7 (the 3-5-9-11), then the same DbMaj7 shape carries forward over the following Eb7 (becoming b7-9-sus4-13). Wes treats the entire ii-V interaction as a unified 'Db major field,' reducing cognitive load while generating sophisticated harmonic color. Lines and chord stabs both draw from this field — single-note phrases imply complex harmony.

references.source: Voicelid Jazz Guitar — Drop-2 + Upper Structures
references.citation: Documents Wes's chord-over-chord substitution: 'his lines imply complex harmony even when he plays single notes.'

references.source: Wikipedia — Wes Montgomery
references.citation: Documents 'superimposed triads and arpeggios as the main source for his soloing ideas.'

systems.name: Three-Tier Chorus Architecture
systems.summary: Wes's canonical solo arc spans multiple choruses with a one-directional textural climb: tier-1 single-notes → tier-2 octaves → tier-3 block chords. Each tier reuses the melodic shape established in tier-1.

traversal_rules.name: Climb is monotonic across choruses
traversal_rules.summary: Once a higher tier is reached, return to a lower tier is uncommon.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 three-tier-chorus-development principle.

modification_rules.name: Section-aggregate density escalation
modification_rules.summary: Each successive chorus uses denser texture (1 → 2 → 4 sounding notes).

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Tier-by-tier density progression.

systems.name: Octave-Doubled Melody
systems.summary: Single melody note becomes two notes an octave apart, played together. Thumb-led with lower note often slightly behind the upper. Octave passages move parallel (no internal voice-leading).

members.name: Octave on string set 5-3 (root on 5, octave on 3)
Chromatic octave approach to chord tone

traversal_rules.name: Octave passages move parallel
traversal_rules.summary: Both notes move together; no internal voice-leading.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 octave-passage-construction principle.

modification_rules.name: Substitute octave-pair for single note in tier-2 phrases
modification_rules.summary: Voicing pool restricts to 2-note octave voicings when tier-2 active.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Tier-2 voicing pool filter.

systems.name: Block Chord Drop-2
systems.summary: Third-tier texture: 4-note block chords using drop-2 voicings. Top voice carries the melody (chord-melody texture). Consecutive block chords tend to stay on one string set across a phrase.

traversal_rules.name: Consecutive block chords share string-set
traversal_rules.summary: Block-chord passages stay on one string set; move by position rather than transference.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Wes's tier-3 articulation across recordings.

modification_rules.name: Drop-2 is the default tier-3 voicing
modification_rules.summary: Engine ranks category 'drop-2' voicings higher than other 4-note shapes when tier-3 active.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 block-chord-drop-2-predominance principle.

systems.name: Thumb-Attack Articulation
systems.summary: Wes used his thumb (not a pick) as the primary attack, giving his sound its characteristic warm/round timbre. Every attack is a thumb downstroke. Limits maximum tempo; influences rhythmic feel.

traversal_rules.name: Downstroke-only with thumb
traversal_rules.summary: No alternate picking; every attack is a thumb downstroke.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 thumb-attack-articulation principle.

systems.name: Upper-Structure Triad Substitution Over Altered Dominants
systems.summary: Substitution over altered dominants via superimposed triads (e.g., Db major triad over C7 produces C7b9b13; D triad produces C7#11; Eb triad produces C7#9; F# triad gives a fully altered C7). The substitute is a TRIAD positioned above an implied dominant root — structurally distinct from chord-quality substitution.

members.name: Db triad over C7 → b9, b13
F# triad over C7 → fully altered

modification_rules.name: Expand candidate pool for dom7alt with upper-structure-triad voicings
modification_rules.summary: When written chord is dom7alt, candidate voicings include traditional altered-dominant voicings AND superimposed-triad voicings.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 upper-structure-triad-substitution principle.

masters.name: Lenny Breau
masters.biography: Canadian guitarist (1941-1984) who synthesized jazz, country, flamenco, folk, and classical idioms. Renowned for right-hand finger independence — bass, chord, and melody as three simultaneously moving voices — and for taking Chet Atkins's harp-harmonic technique to unprecedented levels. Later in his career played a custom 7-string with a high A tuned two octaves above the 5th string. Cited as a primary influence by Bill Frisell, Pat Metheny, and many others.

principles.name: Three independent voices on six strings
principles.summary: Bass, chord, and melody played simultaneously via fingerstyle, with right-hand finger independence treating each voice on its own timeline. Breau implied 'two guitarists playing together at times' (Guitar World) — left-hand fretting and right-hand articulation both required mechanical independence between thumb (bass) and fingers (chord + melody). The chord voice is often 2-3 notes (not a full block) to leave room for the bass and melody.

references.source: Premier Guitar — How Did Lenny Breau Do That?
references.citation: Documents the right-hand independence technique.

references.source: Guitar World — Lenny Breau: The Forgotten Guitar Genius
references.citation: 'Using fingers or pairs of fingers on his right hand independently of each other to juggle bass, melody and chords... He really did create the impression of two guitarists playing together, at times.'

principles.name: Harp-harmonics cascades (Atkins-derived)
principles.summary: Artificial-harmonic technique developed by Chet Atkins and brought to a higher level by Breau. Right hand: thumb or index finger touches a node 12 frets above the fretted position while another finger (typically ring) plucks the string, producing a harmonic at that fretted note's pitch. Cascading between fretted notes and harmonics creates harp-like textures with notes ringing across non-adjacent strings.

references.source: Guitar World — Dale Turner on Lenny Breau's harp harmonics
references.citation: Documents the technique's Atkins origin and Breau's extension: 'sheets of cascading harmonics to create complex harmonies and melodies.'

references.source: Premier Guitar — How Did Lenny Breau Do That?
references.citation: 'After mastering the harp-harmonic technique developed by Atkins, Breau took it to previously unimagined levels.'

principles.name: Implied harmony from two-note bass
principles.summary: Breau's signature device: imply a full chord from just two low-register notes (often root + 7th, or root + 3rd, or 3rd + 7th — the guide tones), leaving the upper strings free for melody. The voicing is minimal in the bass register but the implication is rich — the listener fills in the missing voices. Predates and underpins his three-voice approach when bass is held to two notes only.

references.source: Guitar World — Lenny Breau: The Forgotten Guitar Genius
references.citation: 'Lenny's ability to imply harmony with two low register notes while playing melody on the upper strings was unprecedented.'

principles.name: 7-string with high A (later career)
principles.summary: Late in his career, Breau played a custom 7-string with a high A tuned two octaves above the 5th string. The high A extends melody range above the 1st-string E without retuning, allowing piano-like simultaneous bass + chord + high melody — Breau's stated goal was to 'replicate on guitar a pianist's capacity for simultaneous linear and chordal development.' Distinct from Van Eps's 7-string (low A); Breau adds at the TOP, Van Eps at the bottom.

references.source: Wikipedia — Lenny Breau
references.citation: 'A custom seven-string guitar equipped with a high A tuned two octaves above the fifth string, which allowed him to replicate on guitar a pianist's capacity for simultaneous linear and chordal development.'

systems.name: Three-Voice Independence
systems.summary: Three simultaneously-moving lines on 6 strings — thumb plays bass independently while fingers play chord stabs and melody. Each voice has its own rhythmic identity. Distinct from Greene's SATB four-voice and Van Eps's outer/inner four-voice.

traversal_rules.name: Bass walks while chord pulses while melody sings
traversal_rules.summary: Each voice has its own rhythmic identity.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 three-voice-independence principle.

modification_rules.name: Prefer voicings that leave room for the three-voice texture
modification_rules.summary: Avoid voicings that conflict with the bass-line or block the melody string.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Three-voice texture constraint.

systems.name: Harp-Harmonics Cascades
systems.summary: Right hand plucks a string while right-hand index/middle taps a node 12 frets above, producing artificial harmonic in cascading patterns. Atkins-derived technique. Used for chord-melody where timbre suits (slow ballads, intros).

modification_rules.name: Substitute harmonics for plain notes where timbre suits
modification_rules.summary: Slow ballads and intros use harmonics as the primary attack.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 harp-harmonics principle, Atkins-derived.

systems.name: Implied Harmony via Bass
systems.summary: 2-note voicings (bass + melody only) where inner harmony is IMPLIED rather than played. Bass-led variant of Hall's space-doctrine. Bass note suggests harmonic root; melody fills out the implied chord-tone color.

traversal_rules.name: Bass note suggests harmonic root; melody fills chord-tone color
traversal_rules.summary: The listener completes the harmony.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Sparse-texture chord-melody.

modification_rules.name: Substitute 2-note bass-melody voicing for 4-note in sparse contexts
modification_rules.summary: Sparse-texture preference.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 implied-harmony-via-bass principle.

systems.name: 7-String High-A Melody Range
systems.summary: 7-string with HIGH A (A4, above standard e) — distinct from Van Eps's LOW A (below E). Adds a melody-range string above the standard sound box. Melody runs on high-A while standard 6-string territory handles bass + chord.

modification_rules.name: Substitute high-A melody line for melody-on-top in solo or accompanied-lead
modification_rules.summary: Tuning-specific affordance for the high-A configuration.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 7-string-high-a principle.

masters.name: Jim Hall
masters.biography: Influential jazz comper and soloist (1930-2013) whose voicings emphasize space, fourths, and conversational interaction. Studied composition and arrangement formally; recordings with Bill Evans, Sonny Rollins, Paul Desmond, and Art Farmer defined a quieter, more dialogic approach to jazz guitar accompaniment. Authored Exploring Jazz Guitar (Hal Leonard) and Jazz Guitar Environments (1994).

principles.name: Guide-tone (3 and 7) voicings
principles.summary: Hall's comping foundation: 3rd and 7th of the chord on the middle strings, often as two-note voicings or grace-noted into fuller shapes. Guide tones imply the harmony's quality (major/minor/dominant) and resolve smoothly between adjacent chords with minimal motion. Foundation for the rest of the Hall vocabulary — fuller voicings layer above the guide tones.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'Hall uses 3rd and 7ths (guide tone) voicings and octaves for comping.'

references.source: Guitar World — How Jim Hall revolutionized modern jazz guitar
references.citation: Documents the guide-tone approach as Hall's foundation.

principles.name: Stacked fourths and open voicings
principles.summary: Hall's signature 'fourths' sound: voicings built from stacked perfect fourths rather than triadic thirds — beautiful and harmonically ambiguous, doesn't commit to a single tonality. Often complemented with bass notes for grounding and counter-melodies on top. Hall avoids root-position triads; open voicings with wide intervals between adjacent voices keep the register wide.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: Documents the fourths voicings + open triads/inversions, avoiding 'root-position sound.'

references.source: Jim Hall: Exploring Jazz Guitar
references.citation: Hall, Jim. Exploring Jazz Guitar. Hal Leonard.

principles.name: Space and implied harmony
principles.summary: Hall's comping deliberately leaves space — fewer notes per voicing (often 2-3), strategic silences. The voicing's job is to imply the chord, not exhaustively spell it. This works as 'a second part in the arrangement rather than just filling harmonic space' (jazzguitar.be) — the comp is a melodic line as much as a harmonic support.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'His comping serves as a second part in the arrangement rather than just filling harmonic space.'

principles.name: Small motifs with melody on top of comp
principles.summary: Even in comping, Hall plays small melodic motifs on the top strings above the guide-tone middle voices. The result is a layered texture: harmonic support in the middle, conversational melody on top. Voicings are chosen to free up the top 1-2 strings for this motivic melody.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'Simple motifs, guide tone voicings in the middle strings with small melodies on the top strings.'

systems.name: Guide-Tone Comping (3-7 Voicings)
systems.summary: 2-note voicings of just the 3rd and 7th of the chord; rarely root. For dom7 = {3, b7}; for maj7 = {3, 7}; for m7 = {b3, b7}. Voice-leading by half-step (canonical 7→3 resolution on V7→I).

traversal_rules.name: Half-step 7→3 voice-leading on V→I
traversal_rules.summary: When chord changes, guide-tone voicing moves to the new chord's 3 and 7, often via half-step.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Canonical V7→I guide-tone resolution.

modification_rules.name: Substitute 3-7 voicing for any standard 4-note voicing in comping
modification_rules.summary: Rank 2-note voicings high under context: comping or ensemble.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 guide-tone-comping principle.

systems.name: Quartal and Open Voicings
systems.summary: Stacks of fourths (quartal) and open voicings with large intervals between voices (often a 10th between bass and next voice). Quartal voicings move in parallel; substitute for traditional drop-2/drop-3 in modal contexts.

members.name: Open voicing with 10th between bass and next voice

traversal_rules.name: Quartal voicings move in parallel
traversal_rules.summary: Same shape transposed, leveraging quartal harmony's interval-invariance.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Quartal harmony's defining property.

modification_rules.name: Substitute quartal voicing for drop-2/drop-3 in modal contexts
modification_rules.summary: Engine preference rank-order boosts quartal in modal contexts (m7, sus, modal-major-7).

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 quartal-voicing principle.

systems.name: Space Doctrine (Voice Less)
systems.summary: Voicings are NOT placed on every available beat. Hall's signature is the implied harmony in the gaps. Per-beat probability of voicing decreases under Hall-active.

modification_rules.name: Reduce voicing frequency below default
modification_rules.summary: Per-beat probability of voicing decreases. Hall voices ~50% of available beats vs Pass's ~80%+.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 implied-harmony principle.

systems.name: Motif and Melody
systems.summary: Short melodic statements (2-4 note motifs) with chord support below. Motivic development typical of Hall's compositional formal training.

members.name: Motif repeats with chord variation underneath

traversal_rules.name: Melody motif repeats with chord variation underneath
traversal_rules.summary: Engine signal: form-level motif tracker.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Hall's compositional pedigree.

modification_rules.name: Prefer voicings whose top voice contains the motivic pitch
modification_rules.summary: Melody-on-top constraint.
