references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Chord-melody convention applied strictly.

masters.name: Johnny Smith
masters.biography: Master of close-voiced chord-melody (1922-2013). His 1952 'Moonlight in Vermont' (Royal Roost) became a template for the genre — tight closed voicings that 'are a finger stretch on guitar' and 'pianistic' in feel. Authored the Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar (Mel Bay). His playing combines closed-position chord voicings with 'rapidly ascending lines... more diatonic than chromatically-based' (Django-like but diatonic).

principles.name: Tight closed voicings
principles.summary: Closely-voiced chord-melody — all four voices within an octave, often within a 4-fret span. Produces a unified vertical sound rather than a wide-spread texture. The voicings are 'a finger stretch on guitar' precisely because pianistic close-position spelling doesn't lay out under-the-hand on a guitar fretboard; Smith made them work through deliberate left-hand mechanics.

references.source: Guitar Player — Vinyl Treasures: Moonlight in Vermont
references.citation: 'Smith's version of Moonlight in Vermont was known because of its tight closed voicings, which are a finger stretch on guitar.'

references.source: Mel Bay's Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar
references.citation: Smith, Johnny. Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar. Mel Bay.

principles.name: Pianistic wide-stretch chord voicings
principles.summary: Smith's voicings cover 'a large section of real estate on the guitar neck' — his other signature alongside close voicings is the wide-stretch chord where adjacent voices are placed across non-adjacent strings or wider fret spans for piano-like sonority. The two approaches (tight closed AND wide stretch) coexist; arrangements move between them depending on which sonority the melody calls for.

references.source: Guitar Player — Moonlight in Vermont feature
references.citation: 'His challenging wide-stretch chord voicings are very pianistic... covering a large section of real estate on the guitar neck.'

principles.name: Diatonic single-note lines (Django-derived but diatonic)
principles.summary: Smith's single-note playing inherits Django Reinhardt's rapid-ascending-line aesthetic but stays more diatonically rooted, less chromatically-driven than bebop vocabulary. The diatonicism makes the lines blend smoothly into the close-voiced chord-melody context — chromatic interpolations would clash with the close diatonic stack.

references.source: Wikipedia — Johnny Smith
references.citation: 'Closed-position chord voicings and rapidly ascending lines (reminiscent of Django Reinhardt, but more diatonic than chromatically-based).'

systems.name: Close Voicing with Stretch Tolerance
systems.summary: 4-note voicings spanning ≤5 frets, all voices within an octave or close. The defining Smith texture. Stretch tolerance is HIGHER than most masters — close voicings that require larger finger stretches are accepted under Smith-active where they'd be filtered out for others.

traversal_rules.name: Consecutive close voicings move stepwise
traversal_rules.summary: Minimal hand motion; voicings nest within a 3-fret span across a phrase.

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Moonlight in Vermont (1952) reference recording.

modification_rules.name: Close voicing default over drop-2
modification_rules.summary: Contrast with Pass and Wes (drop-2-default).

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 close-voicing principle.

modification_rules.name: Higher finger-stretch tolerance than most masters
modification_rules.summary: Accept close voicings other masters would reject for finger reach.

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Per-master stretch ceiling.

systems.name: Pianistic Wide-Stretch Voicings
systems.summary: Voicings with internal intervals exceeding an octave. Smith's 'pianistic' voicings. Appear at melodic peaks and resolutions — register-conditional, not comping default.

modification_rules.name: Substitute wide-stretch when melody hits high register
modification_rules.summary: Register-conditional preference — when current register is high, wide-stretch preferred; otherwise close-voicing.

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Register-conditional preference, parallel to Wes's tier-conditional motion order.

systems.name: Diatonic Line Vocabulary
systems.summary: Single-note lines drawn from diatonic chord-scales. Stays IN the key. Django-influenced rhythmic feel. Chromatic approach is rare (unlike Pass).

traversal_rules.name: Lines target chord tones; chromatic approach rare
traversal_rules.summary: Diatonic-by-default.

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Smith's diatonic vocabulary.

modification_rules.name: Restrict line-pitch vocabulary to diatonic
modification_rules.summary: For solo and accompanied-lead contexts.

references.source: plans/smith-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Existing scale-pairing mechanism.

masters.name: Jody Fisher
masters.biography: Educator (1957-) whose Alfred Music series — Complete Jazz Guitar Method (Beginning / Intermediate / Mastering), Jazz Guitar Harmony, Chord Tone Soloing — systematized jazz guitar study for generations of self-taught players. Fisher's contribution is pedagogical structure rather than distinctive personal style: he codified the standard voicing vocabulary (drop-2, drop-3, extended harmonies, altered chords) into an ordered curriculum and provided 'hundreds of chord voicings and improvisation ideas' across the series.

principles.name: Pedagogical voicing taxonomy
principles.summary: Fisher categorizes voicings systematically for teaching: drop-2 (drop the second-from-top from a close-position stack), drop-3 (drop the third-from-top), abbreviated voicings, extended-harmony voicings (9ths/11ths/13ths), altered-chord formulas. The taxonomy isn't novel — it's the standard pedagogical framework — but Fisher's curriculum-builder organization is what makes it teachable to self-learners.

references.source: Jazz Guitar Harmony (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'Chord scales, abbreviated voicings, extended harmonies, altered chords, substitutions, ii-V-I progressions, the dominant cycle.'

references.source: Complete Jazz Guitar Method: Intermediate (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'ii-V-I progression, solo lines, altered chord formulas, and comping in various jazz feels.'

principles.name: Etude- and song-driven practice methodology
principles.summary: Fisher's pedagogical structure pairs every new concept (chord type, voicing family, substitution rule) with concrete etudes AND songs that exercise it. The principle for voicing-selection: a voicing is properly learned when it's been used in BOTH an abstract exercise (etude) and a musical context (song). Carries into the plugin domain as a preference for voicings that the user has explicit musical context for, not just position-tabular acquaintance.

references.source: Complete Jazz Guitar Method: Intermediate (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'Every new concept accompanied by etudes and songs for practice.'

systems.name: Pedagogical Organization (A-Lessons / B-Lessons)
systems.summary: Fisher's distinctive teaching structure: parallel A-lessons (chords/harmony) + B-lessons (improvisation) across his three volumes. Each chapter has a concept → etude → song-application. Documentation-only for v1 engine; may surface in a future practice-mode UI.

traversal_rules.name: A-lessons and B-lessons run in parallel
traversal_rules.summary: A1/B1, A2/B2, etc. are paired but independent tracks.

references.source: plans/fisher-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Fisher Vol 1-3 (Alfred Publishing) structure.

systems.name: Etude- and Song-Driven Practice
systems.summary: Fisher's commitment to demonstrating every concept with both an etude (mechanical) AND a full song (musical context). Composed tunes ('Noah's Groove', 'Ruby My Deerfly', 'Modelicious', 'Blue Tofu') are pedagogical artifacts as much as music.

traversal_rules.name: Etudes practiced before songs
traversal_rules.summary: Mechanical drilling precedes musical application.

references.source: plans/fisher-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Fisher Vol 1 'Practicing' appendix.

works.title: Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 1: Beginning
works.summary: The beginner-track entry in Fisher's Alfred Music method series. Seven progressive chapters install the operating system the rest of the series depends on: a major-scale-derived harmony engine (chord tones from scale degrees), fretboard navigation in a single position, the A-Lesson / B-Lesson dual-track pedagogical structure (technique vs. theory streams alternating), and a solo-architecture introduction anchored to motif-and-variation. Two systems were derived: the Major Scale Harmony and Fretboard Navigation System (the central generative engine) and the Solo Architecture and Motif Development System (the smaller compositional companion).

references.source: Fisher, Jody. Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 1: Beginning. Alfred Music, 1995.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-fisher-jazz-guitar-method-vol-1; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jody-fisher/jazz-guitar-method-vol-1-beginning/.

systems.name: Major Scale Harmony and Fretboard Navigation System
systems.summary: The central generative system of Fisher's method. Everything—chords, voicings, modes, and improvisation—derives from the major scale treated as a universal key. Members form a taxonomy running from the chromatic raw material through scale fingerings, triads, diatonic 7th chords, extended chords, and modal derivatives. Traversal rules govern how a player moves between fretboard positions and string sets; modification rules govern how chord voicings are built, extended, and legally thinned. The system is designed to be key-agnostic: all patterns are learned in Roman-numeral 'universal key' and transposed to any of the twelve keys via the cycle of 4ths/5ths.

members.name: Chromatic Scale (12 tones)
members.summary: The raw pitch universe from which all scales and intervals are measured.

members.name: Major Scale (W-W-H-W-W-W-H)
members.summary: The generative formula from which every chord, key, and mode in the method derives.

members.name: Major Scale Fingerings: Sixth-String Root (6/1, 6/2, 6/4)
members.summary: Three locked-position scale fingerings with roots on string 6, labeled by finger number.

members.name: Major Scale Fingerings: Fifth-String Root (5/1, 5/2, 5/4)
members.summary: Three locked-position scale fingerings with roots on string 5.

members.name: Triad — Root Position (R-3-5)
members.summary: Three-note chord voiced root, 3rd, 5th bottom-to-top across a three-string set.

members.name: Triad — First Inversion (3-5-R)
members.summary: Triad voiced 3rd, 5th, root bottom-to-top.

members.name: Triad — Second Inversion (5-R-3)
members.summary: Triad voiced 5th, root, 3rd bottom-to-top.

members.name: Diatonic 7th Chord (IMaj7, ii min7, iii min7, IVMaj7, V7, vi min7, vii min7b5)
members.summary: Seven chord qualities produced by stacking 3rds on each degree of the harmonized major scale.

members.name: Extended Chord (add 9, 11, 13)
members.summary: Chords using tones from the second octave of the major scale; named for their highest extension.

members.name: Modal Scale (Ionian through Locrian)
members.summary: Each of the seven rotations of the major scale, each corresponding to its diatonic chord degree.

members.name: String Set (four three-string groupings: 6-5-4, 5-4-3, 4-3-2, 3-2-1)
members.summary: The four overlapping three-string zones that organize all triad and chord-scale fingerings.

members.name: Fingerboard Position (six-fret span)
members.summary: A six-fret window covering fingers 1–4 plus two additional frets; one locked position covers eleven keys.

traversal_rules.name: Cycle of 4ths Chord Movement
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer moving chord roots a 4th upward (or 5th downward) as the primary harmonic motion pattern, following the cycle of 4ths/5ths.

references.quote_excerpt: One of the most common patterns in these styles is the movement of a 4th

traversal_rules.name: Scale-Wise Voice Motion Through Chord Scale
traversal_rules.summary: When moving from chord to chord within a diatonic progression, each voice moves by step (root→root, 3rd→3rd, etc.).

references.quote_excerpt: each voice moves in a scale-wise motion (from root to root, 3rd to 3rd, etc)

traversal_rules.name: Locked-Position Fingerboard Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Navigate the fingerboard using closed (no open string) scale and chord fingerings so that any pattern can be shifted to a new key without re-fingering.

references.quote_excerpt: Scales, arpeggios and licks are in locked position when they contain no open strings

traversal_rules.name: String-Set Horizontal Chord-Scale Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Move through diatonic chord scales by crossing string sets in a horizontal direction to learn all diatonic chords in closer proximity.

references.quote_excerpt: learn chord scales in a vertical direction, across the string sets
chord scales that follow are shown on various string sets

traversal_rules.name: Roman-Numeral Universal-Key Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Learn all progressions as Roman-numeral patterns (e.g., ii-V-I) so they can be instantly transposed to any of twelve keys by plugging into a new root.

references.quote_excerpt: Learn all of your songs in Roman numerals. Then you will have learned the material in a 'universal key'

traversal_rules.name: Mode-to-Diatonic-Chord Correspondence
traversal_rules.summary: Select the improvisation scale by matching each chord to its corresponding mode of the parent major scale (e.g., Dorian over ii min7).

references.quote_excerpt: Each mode of the major scale corresponds to the diatonic chord that shares the same root

traversal_rules.name: Diatonic / Modal Thinking Switch at Non-Diatonic Chord
traversal_rules.summary: Use the parent major scale over diatonic chords; switch to the specific mode when the progression reaches a non-diatonic chord.

references.quote_excerpt: when you get to a chord that is not natural to the key, switch to the modal approach

traversal_rules.name: Combine Locked-Position (Vertical) and Open-Position (Horizontal) Thinking
traversal_rules.summary: Avoid restricting improvisation to a single spatial mode; alternate between locked-position cross-string patterns and horizontal single-string movement.

references.quote_excerpt: when you use it to the exclusion of horizontal thinking it can severely limit your improvisational ideas

modification_rules.name: Omit Root, 5th, and/or 9th from Extended Voicings
modification_rules.summary: Roots, 5ths, and 9ths may be dropped in any combination to reduce voicing density without losing harmonic identity.

references.topic: which chord tones not to drop
references.quote_excerpt: Roots, 5ths and 9ths can be dropped in various combinations

modification_rules.name: Retain 3rd and 7th as Non-Omissible Color Tones
modification_rules.summary: The 3rd and 7th must be kept in any voicing because they are the tones that define chord quality (major, minor, dominant).

references.topic: which chord tones not to drop
references.quote_excerpt: it is generally not a good idea to drop 3rds and 7ths because these are the tones that define major, minor and dominant chords

modification_rules.name: Extend Chord by Adding Scale Tones Above Triad
modification_rules.summary: Construct any larger chord by beginning with the R-3-5 triad and adding successive scale degrees (6, 7, then 9, 11, 13 via second octave).

references.quote_excerpt: constructing larger chords is simply a matter of adding additional scale tones to the original triad
We get the extended notes (beyond the octave) by continuing the major scale up through a second octave

modification_rules.name: Name Chord by Highest Extension Present
modification_rules.summary: Label any extended chord by its highest present extension even when lower extensions are implied but unstated.

references.quote_excerpt: Chords are usually named for the highest extension present

modification_rules.name: Triad Inversion: Cycle Through Root / First / Second Inversion on String Sets
modification_rules.summary: Apply root (R-3-5), first (3-5-R), and second (5-R-3) inversion voicings across all four string sets to cover the full fingerboard.

references.quote_excerpt: When the notes are sounded from bottom to top in root, 3rd, 5th order, it is referred to as being in root position
divide the guitar into four string sets

modification_rules.name: Harmonize Each Scale Degree by Stacking 3rds
modification_rules.summary: Generate the full chord scale by stacking diatonic 3rds on each of the seven scale degrees, producing triads (three-high) or 7th chords (four-high).

references.quote_excerpt: we stack 3rds on top of each scale degree. If we stack them three notes high, we get triads. If we stack them four notes high, we get 7th chords

modification_rules.name: Scale-Choice Cardinality per Chord Quality
modification_rules.summary: Apply the fixed rule: Maj7 has two parent-scale choices, min7 has three, dom7 has one, min7b5 has one — constraining which scales are legal over each chord type.

references.quote_excerpt: Every Maj7 chord has two major scale choices… Every min7 chord has three… Every dominant 7th chord has one

systems.name: Solo Architecture and Motif Development System
systems.summary: A smaller compositional system governing how an improvised jazz solo is shaped over time. Members are the three formal sections of a solo (opening, body, conclusion) plus the motif as the atomic phrase unit. Traversal rules describe how to move through the solo's arc — from sparse opening through developing body to resolved conclusion. Modification rules describe how a stated motif is varied rhythmically or melodically, and how repetition is deployed to build and release tension. This system is explicitly taught in Chapters 6 and 7 and is distinct from the harmonic/fretboard navigation concerns of the central system.

members.name: Solo Opening
members.summary: Sparse beginning: easy-to-follow, startling, or silent; sets tone and listens to accompaniment.

members.name: Solo Body
members.summary: Develops themes initiated in the opening; longer lines, melodic patterns, register/dynamic changes.

members.name: Solo Conclusion
members.summary: Signals the end; may deploy 'flash' element; repetition used here for intensity before release.

members.name: Motif
members.summary: A short phrase (lick, spontaneous idea, or melodic pattern) that becomes the thematic unit driving the solo.

members.name: Tension Region
members.summary: A passage of repeated material that accumulates listener intensity.

members.name: Release Region
members.summary: The break in repetition that resolves accumulated tension.

traversal_rules.name: Three-Part Solo Arc: Opening → Body → Conclusion
traversal_rules.summary: Navigate the solo's formal structure by progressing from sparse opening through busier theme-development body to a conclusive ending that signals resolution.

references.quote_excerpt: The body usually gets a little busier to develop themes you have initiated… The conclusion lets your listeners know the solo is ending

traversal_rules.name: Tension/Release via Repetition and Its Breaking
traversal_rules.summary: Build tension by repeating a phrase across chord changes; resolve it by breaking the repetition at a strategic moment.

references.quote_excerpt: Breaking the repetition eases the tension and resolution is achieved

traversal_rules.name: Defer Peak Repetition to Solo Conclusion
traversal_rules.summary: Save the most intense repetitive phrase for the conclusion of the solo to maximize the payoff of built-up tension.

references.quote_excerpt: Repetition tends to build intensity. This is why some players save this technique for the conclusion of their solo

modification_rules.name: Motif Variation: Maintain Pitches, Change Rhythm
modification_rules.summary: Vary a stated motif by keeping its exact pitches while altering the rhythmic structure.

references.quote_excerpt: maintaining the pitches of the notes and changing the rhythmic structure

modification_rules.name: Motif Variation: Maintain Rhythm, Change Pitches
modification_rules.summary: Vary a stated motif by keeping its rhythmic figure while changing the pitches of the notes.

references.quote_excerpt: maintaining the rhythmic figure but changing the pitches of the notes

modification_rules.name: Limit Rhythmic Options to Isolate Melodic Hearing
modification_rules.summary: Constrain note values to a single denomination (whole, half, quarter, eighth) to break automatic finger patterns and develop melodic ear independently of rhythm.

references.quote_excerpt: limiting rhythmic options… will help you to break out of finger patterns that you automatically fall into

works.title: Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 2: Intermediate
works.summary: The intermediate-track continuation. Nine chapters extend the single-position vocabulary of Vol. 1 to the ii-V-I harmonic framework (the operative jazz harmonic cell), introduce lick-based improvisation vocabulary as the application of theory to phrase construction, and develop swing comping texture (chord-on-the-beat vs. on-the-and rhythmic placements). Three systems were derived: the ii-V7-I Harmonic Framework System, the Lick-Based Improvisation Vocabulary System, and the Swing Comping Texture System.

references.source: Fisher, Jody. Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 2: Intermediate. Alfred Music, 1995.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-fisher-jazz-guitar-method-vol-2; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jody-fisher/jazz-guitar-method-vol-2-intermediate/.

systems.name: ii-V7-I Harmonic Framework
systems.summary: The book's central load-bearing system organizes all harmonic and improvisational activity around the ii-V7-I progression. Members are the three chord-function roles (ii, V7, I) plus the altered/extended variants available at each slot. Traversal rules govern how voicings of each chord function are connected across the fingerboard. Modification rules govern what may be altered or omitted within each chord (3rds and 7ths are essential; roots and 5ths are expendable), how extensions are added to the V7, and how the rootless 7b9 symmetry at minor-third intervals can be exploited. The whole system is the substrate onto which all lick vocabulary, blues, rhythm changes, and turnaround material is subsequently grafted.

members.name: ii chord (minor function)
members.summary: Minor seventh chord a perfect fourth below the V7. Typically Dmin7 or Dmin9 in examples.

members.name: V7 chord (dominant function)
members.summary: Dominant seventh chord; primary site of alteration and extension (7b5, 7#5, 7b9, 9th, 11th, 13th).

members.name: I chord (tonic function)
members.summary: Major tonic chord that the progression resolves into and whose major scale governs diatonic improvisation.

members.name: Altered/Extended Chord Variant
members.summary: Any ii, V7, or I chord with raised or lowered 5th, 9th, or 11th. Still counts as ii-V7-I if roots are perfect-fourth apart and qualities remain minor/dominant/major.

traversal_rules.name: String-Set Shift for ii-V7-I
traversal_rules.summary: Voice the ii-V7-I progression on each of the three string sets (6-5-4-3, 5-4-3-2, 4-3-2-1) and shift across sets to cover the fingerboard.

references.quote_excerpt: divide the six strings of the guitar into three string sets
following ii-V7-I progressions move across the string sets

traversal_rules.name: Position-Naming Continuity (lowest-root naming)
traversal_rules.summary: Identify fingerings by the string and finger holding the lowest root (e.g., 6/2).

references.quote_excerpt: 6/2 means that the lowest root in the fingering is on the sixth string

traversal_rules.name: Target-Note Melodic Motion (spelling out the changes)
traversal_rules.summary: Begin each improvised phrase on a chord tone, preferring 3rds and 7ths, to articulate voice motion through the progression.

references.quote_excerpt: begin each phrase you play with a note of the chord
Improvisors tend to use 3rds and 7ths the most

traversal_rules.name: Maj7-from-b7 Arpeggio Over V7
traversal_rules.summary: Over V7, play a Major 7th arpeggio rooted on its flat-7 degree to voice upper extensions (9th, 11th, 13th).

references.quote_excerpt: play the notes of a major 7th arpeggio rooted on the b7 degree of the V7 chord
you don't have to start the major 7th arpeggio on the root

traversal_rules.name: Ascending Lines from Chord Tones
traversal_rules.summary: Play ascending scalar lines beginning on a chord tone, optionally alternating with descending lines for contour variety.

references.quote_excerpt: play ascending scale-wise patterns starting from chord tones
Alternating ascending and descending patterns will give your line a more interesting contour

modification_rules.name: 3rd and 7th Density Floor (Essential Tones)
modification_rules.summary: Any voiced chord must retain the 3rd and 7th; these tones define chord quality and are non-expendable.

references.quote_excerpt: two most essential notes in any chord are the 3rd and the 7th

modification_rules.name: Root and 5th Omission Allow
modification_rules.summary: Roots, 5ths, 9ths, and 11ths may be omitted; rootless voicings preferred when more extensions are needed or when a bass player covers the root.

references.quote_excerpt: It is common to omit roots, 5ths, 9ths and 11ths in certain situations
roots have been omitted from both the Dmin9 and G9 voicings

modification_rules.name: Alterable Tones Ceiling (5th/9th/11th Only)
modification_rules.summary: Only the 5th, 9th, and 11th may be raised or lowered; no other chord degrees can be altered.

references.quote_excerpt: only notes that can be altered in any chord are the 5th, 9th and the 11th

modification_rules.name: Diminished Triad Substitution Over V7
modification_rules.summary: Over V7, substitute a diminished triad from the 3rd, 5th, or 7th of that chord to produce altered extensions that resolve into I.

references.quote_excerpt: Play a diminished triad starting at the 3rd, 5th or 7th degree of the V7 chord

modification_rules.name: 7b9 Rootless Minor-Third Symmetry
modification_rules.summary: A rootless dominant 7b9 voicing moves at minor-third intervals around the fingerboard without changing chord quality.

references.quote_excerpt: move the same fingering around the fingerboard at intervals of a minor 3rd without changing the chord's quality

modification_rules.name: Upper Extensions as Color Tones (9th, 11th, 13th)
modification_rules.summary: On V7 chords, 9th/11th/13th extensions are required for jazz color; the Maj7-from-b7 technique is the primary delivery mechanism.

references.quote_excerpt: A is the 9th, C is the 11th and E is the 13th
Knowing lots of fingerings will allow you to play with greater taste and variety

modification_rules.name: Context-and-Ear Substitution Constraint
modification_rules.summary: Chord substitutions are valid only when they sound good in context; theoretical correctness alone does not justify a substitution.

references.quote_excerpt: context is everything. Just because a substitution is 'theoretically correct' doesn't mean it sounds good

modification_rules.name: Major-Scale Rule with Altered-Chord Exception
modification_rules.summary: Use the I-chord major scale over diatonic ii-V7-I; suspend this rule if any chord contains altered tones outside that scale.

references.quote_excerpt: use the major scale of the I chord
all chords with a raised or lowered 5th, 9th or 11th would make this rule inapplicable

systems.name: Lick-Based Improvisation Vocabulary System
systems.summary: Organizes the book's approach to improvisational vocabulary through chord-type-specific lick categories. Members are the four chord-type lick families plus blues and turnaround lick categories. Traversal rules govern how a soloist moves through a progression by deploying licks (neighbor-tone approach into target notes, melodic contour alternation, whole-neck combination). The central modification rule is the learn-transpose-apply cycle: every lick must be transposed to all twelve keys and immediately inserted into real solos. The system is explicitly framed as 'creative reorganization of learned vocabulary,' with licks as words and scales as alphabet.

members.name: Major-Type Chord Lick Family
members.summary: Licks applicable over major 7th, 6th, 9th, and 13th chords.

members.name: Minor-Type Chord Lick Family
members.summary: Licks applicable over minor seventh and related minor voicings.

members.name: Dominant-Type Chord Lick Family
members.summary: Licks applicable over unaltered dominant 7th, 9th, 11th, and 13th chords.

members.name: Half-Diminished (min7b5) Lick Family
members.summary: Licks for the min7b5 chord; introduced as ear-training for an unfamiliar sound.

members.name: Blues Scalar Tool Set
members.summary: Minor pentatonic, blues scale, and Mixolydian mode as the three primary soloing tools over I-IV-V7 blues progressions.

members.name: Turnaround Lick Family
members.summary: Melodic phrases designed to create smooth harmonic transitions at first endings and tune conclusions.

traversal_rules.name: Neighbor-Tone Approach into Target Notes
traversal_rules.summary: Approach each target chord tone with a half-step or whole-step neighbor tone above or below.

references.quote_excerpt: notes one half step below and above the chord tone

traversal_rules.name: Ascending/Descending Melodic Contour Alternation
traversal_rules.summary: Alternate ascending and descending lick patterns to create varied melodic contour.

references.quote_excerpt: Alternating ascending and descending patterns will give your line a more interesting contour

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Neck Scale Combination (Anti-Positional)
traversal_rules.summary: Combine scalar tools fluidly across the entire fingerboard rather than staying in a single position; supported by single-string practice discipline.

references.quote_excerpt: we usually combine the various tools and utilize the entire fingerboard
start learning all of your scales, modes and arpeggios up and down single strings

modification_rules.name: Learn-Transpose-Apply Cycle
modification_rules.summary: Every lick must be transposed to all twelve keys and immediately inserted into real solos.

references.quote_excerpt: Transpose them and start using them immediately in songs you are learning
learn, transpose and insert these into your solos as soon as possible
Memorize and transpose these and keep your ears open

modification_rules.name: Lick Extension Transfer (Related Voicings)
modification_rules.summary: Licks written for a base chord type transfer to related extensions (major 6th, 9th, 13th; dominant 9th, 11th, 13th) through ear verification.

references.quote_excerpt: work equally well with major 6th, major 9th and major 13th chords
Try using them over dominant 9th, 11th and 13th chords as well

modification_rules.name: Blues Scale Tool Selection (Pentatonic / Blues / Mixolydian)
modification_rules.summary: Over I-IV-V7 blues, choose among minor pentatonic, blues scale, and Mixolydian mode as interchangeable scalar color tools.

references.quote_excerpt: minor pentatonic scale, blues scale and Mixolydian mode fingerings

modification_rules.name: Hear-First Initiation Principle
modification_rules.summary: Every improvised idea must be heard mentally before executed; singing along with playing is the training mechanism.

references.quote_excerpt: the ideal is to first hear an idea in your head, and then execute it
Singing along with what you are playing is a good way to begin training yourself to hear first

systems.name: Swing Comping Texture System
systems.summary: A smaller but distinct system governing the guitarist's accompanying role. Members are the rhythmic-texture states available to the comping guitarist (down/up stroke, damped, sustained). The core aesthetic is that swing rhythm is implied rather than mechanically predictable—accents cycle unpredictably across the measure. A social-context modification rule forbids simultaneous free comping when a keyboard player is also comping.

members.name: Downbeat Downstroke
members.summary: Strum direction on downbeats: down stroke.

members.name: Upbeat Upstroke
members.summary: Strum direction on upbeats: up stroke.

members.name: Damped Chord
members.summary: Chord cut short via left-hand pressure release or right-hand palm/wrist damping.

members.name: Sustained Chord
members.summary: Chord allowed to ring through its natural decay.

traversal_rules.name: Swing Rhythm Implied Accent Cycle
traversal_rules.summary: Cycle accents unpredictably across parts of each measure rather than mechanically; maintain implied-not-stated rhythm.

references.quote_excerpt: the rhythm in a swing feel is implied. The beat is often suggested by the guitarist
strum down on the downbeats and to strum up on the upbeats

modification_rules.name: No Simultaneous Free Comping with Keyboard
modification_rules.summary: When a keyboard player is present, the guitarist and keyboardist must alternate comping roles rather than both comping freely.

references.quote_excerpt: If both the guitarist and the keyboard player comp freely at the same time the tune will become like a harmonic can of worms

modification_rules.name: Damp/Sustain Control During Rests
modification_rules.summary: Damp strings during notated rests using left-hand pressure release or right-hand palm damping to prevent unwanted sustain.

references.quote_excerpt: try to damp the strings so that the chords don't sustain during the rests

works.title: Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 3: Mastering Chord/Melody
works.summary: The advanced-track conclusion focusing on chord-melody style: simultaneous melody-and-harmony performance where the top voice carries the melody and the inner voices carry the harmony. Six chapters move from single-line technique reinforcement into the compositional craft of chord-melody arrangement, with explicit treatment of diad harmonization, quartal voicings, right-hand role-split (thumb / fingers for independent voices), and bassline-led reharmonization as compositional levers. Four systems were derived covering melodic-line construction, chord-melody arrangement, harmonic enrichment, and expressive priority decisions.

references.source: Fisher, Jody. Complete Jazz Guitar Method, Vol. 3: Mastering Chord/Melody. Alfred Music, 1996.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-fisher-jazz-guitar-method-vol-3; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jody-fisher/jazz-guitar-method-vol-3-mastering-chord-melody/.

systems.name: Right-Hand Technique System
systems.summary: This system governs the three primary right-hand techniques: pure fingerstyle, hybrid pick-with-fingers, and single-note alternating patterns. Fingerstyle enables selective chord voicing and simultaneous multi-voice harmony; hybrid allows easy switching but limits voicing to four notes; alternating patterns (i-m or p-m) suit melodic lines. The organizing principle is flexibility: mastering all techniques rather than specializing prepares the player for any musical situation.

traversal_rules.name: Master All Techniques for Flexibility
traversal_rules.summary: Develop proficiency in all techniques rather than specializing in one or two.

references.quote_excerpt: best to learn every technique instead of relying on just one or two

modification_rules.name: Fingerstyle Selective Voicing Control
modification_rules.summary: Pluck only the desired tones, avoiding muting and barring.

references.quote_excerpt: You pluck only the tones you want to hear

modification_rules.name: Fingerstyle Simultaneous Voice Sounding
modification_rules.summary: Fingerstyle uniquely enables all chord notes to sound simultaneously like a keyboard.

references.quote_excerpt: sound harmonies like a keyboard instrument—all the notes of a chord at the same moment

modification_rules.name: Hybrid Technique Four-Note Voicing Limit
modification_rules.summary: Hybrid pick-with-fingers limits harmonic density to four-note chords.

references.quote_excerpt: limited to playing four-note chords

systems.name: Harmonic Arrangement and Voice-Leading System
systems.summary: Core system for chord-melody arrangement: strategic voicing selection, voice-leading principles, and harmonic enrichment. Proceeds from melody understanding through key selection, chord voicing, and layered passing chords, substitutions, and alterations. The 3rd and 7th degrees define chord quality and anchor all voice-leading decisions; the system prizes stepwise motion, careful non-chord-tone handling, and judicious enrichment guided by context and taste.

traversal_rules.name: Melody-Driven Voicing Selection
traversal_rules.summary: Find chord voicings that place melody notes on top.

references.topic: Finding chord voicings with melody on top
references.quote_excerpt: find chord voicings that have the melody notes on top

traversal_rules.name: Melody Study Across All Octaves
traversal_rules.summary: Learn melody in single notes across every octave before harmonization.

references.quote_excerpt: Learn to play the melody in single notes first. Explore it in every octave

traversal_rules.name: Key Selection for Playability and Character
traversal_rules.summary: Explore different keys to improve fingerboard playability and exploit open strings.

references.quote_excerpt: the song might lie on the fingerboard better in a different key

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Voice Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Arrange voicings so voices travel in whole and half steps between changes.

references.quote_excerpt: voices actually travel in whole and half steps

traversal_rules.name: Passing Chord Smooth Transition
traversal_rules.summary: Insert passing chords between two primary chords to smooth voice-leading.

references.quote_excerpt: A passing chord is a chord added between two others to create a smoother transition

traversal_rules.name: Secondary Dominants as V7-I Generalization
traversal_rules.summary: Treat any chord as a temporary tonic and precede with its V7.

references.quote_excerpt: you may consider any chord a I chord and precede it by its own V7 chord

traversal_rules.name: ii-V7-I Progression for Key Definition
traversal_rules.summary: Use ii-V7-I to define a key via characteristic root, 3rd, 7th motion.

references.quote_excerpt: ii-V7-I progression defines or establishes a key

modification_rules.name: Octave Adjustment for Harmonic Expansion
modification_rules.summary: Raise the melody an octave to enable larger voicings.

references.quote_excerpt: raise the melody one octave in order to use larger chord voicings

modification_rules.name: Chord Enhancement Through Extension and Alteration
modification_rules.summary: Add extensions or alterations while maintaining family identity.

references.quote_excerpt: add extensions or alterations to the basic chord

modification_rules.name: Chord Family Interchangeability
modification_rules.summary: Within a chord family, members are functionally interchangeable.

references.quote_excerpt: all the chords are interchangeable with any other chord in the same column

modification_rules.name: Diads for Two-Note Harmonization
modification_rules.summary: Harmonize both chord and non-chord tones with diads (3rds and 6ths).

references.quote_excerpt: Diads, also known as double-stops, are two-note harmonizations

modification_rules.name: Non-Chord Tone Integration as Chord Extension
modification_rules.summary: Add melody-derived non-chord tones to the chord as extensions/alterations.

references.quote_excerpt: add a non-chord tone that is in the melody to the chord

modification_rules.name: 3rd and 7th as Voice-Leading Anchors
modification_rules.summary: Prioritize 3rd and 7th preservation—they define chord quality.

references.quote_excerpt: chords are defined by their 3rds and 7ths

modification_rules.name: Half-Step Dominant Passing Chords
modification_rules.summary: Use dominant chord a half step above/below to smooth transitions.

references.quote_excerpt: dominant chord with a root that is a half step higher, or lower

modification_rules.name: Diminished Chord as V7 Substitute
modification_rules.summary: Substitute diminished chord a half step below the target for V7.

references.quote_excerpt: diminished chord from one half step below the root is a common substitute for the V7

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution for Dominant Chords
modification_rules.summary: Replace V7 with dominant chord whose root is a tritone away.

references.quote_excerpt: replace the V7 chord with a dominant chord whose root is a diminished 5th

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution by Root Distance
modification_rules.summary: Approach any chord via substitute a tritone away from destination root.

references.quote_excerpt: approach any chord from another chord whose root is a tritone away

modification_rules.name: Backcycling as ii-V7-I Extension
modification_rules.summary: Extend ii-V7-I by repeating fourth-movement through the cycle.

references.quote_excerpt: Backcycling is a way to extend the ii-V7-I approach

modification_rules.name: Quartal Harmony Construction
modification_rules.summary: Build chords by stacking 4ths to create alternative harmonic color.

references.quote_excerpt: In quartal harmony, chords are constructed by stacking 4ths

modification_rules.name: Octave Doubling for Melodic Emphasis
modification_rules.summary: Double melody in octaves for stronger emphasis (Wes Montgomery, Benson).

references.quote_excerpt: Doubling melody notes in octaves will create a stronger effect

modification_rules.name: Context and Taste as Enhancement Guide
modification_rules.summary: Musical context and taste determine when and how much to enhance.

references.quote_excerpt: Context is everything. Taste should be your primary concern

systems.name: Bass Line and Two-Instrument Arrangement System
systems.summary: Two-part arrangement system where bass line and chord voicings sound like separate instruments. Voicings learned systematically in all twelve keys using minimal fingers; bass-line approaches applied across keys. Thumb handles bass, index/middle voice the triad; scale-tone connections and half-step approaches give walking-bass realism. Bass on every beat as anchor with chords as accents achieves the two-instrument illusion.

traversal_rules.name: Learn Voicings Systematically in All Twelve Keys
traversal_rules.summary: Master voicings in all twelve keys before applying bass-line approaches.

references.quote_excerpt: Learn the voicings in all twelve keys first

traversal_rules.name: Bass Line Connection via Scale Tones
traversal_rules.summary: Use chord-scale tones to connect triad voicings.

references.quote_excerpt: use the tones of the scale that the chord is built from to connect the various triad voicings

traversal_rules.name: Half-Step Chord Approaches
traversal_rules.summary: Approach chords from a half step above or below the lowest triad voice.

references.quote_excerpt: approached from the note a half step above or below the lowest voice in the triad

modification_rules.name: Minimal-Finger Voicing for Melody Freedom
modification_rules.summary: Use as few fingers as possible (often two) to free fingers for melody.

references.quote_excerpt: Finger these chords with as few fingers as possible

modification_rules.name: Right-Hand Thumb-Index-Middle Finger Assignment
modification_rules.summary: Thumb plays bass; index and middle voice the triad.

references.quote_excerpt: Your right thumb p should handle the bass notes while i and m should take care of the other two notes

modification_rules.name: Sustaining Overlapping Triad Notes
modification_rules.summary: Let two triad notes ring while hitting the next bass note.

references.quote_excerpt: let two notes of the triad ring as you hit the next bass note

modification_rules.name: Rhythmic Separation via Bass-Chord Delay
modification_rules.summary: Bass on downbeat, chord slightly after, within the same beat.

references.quote_excerpt: delaying the chord by playing the bass note on the downbeat and the chord slightly after

modification_rules.name: Bass Every Beat with Chords as Accents
modification_rules.summary: Bass on every beat as anchor; chords as punches/accents with realistic balance.

references.quote_excerpt: Put a bass note on every beat and have the chords appear as punches

systems.name: Voicing Symmetry and Octave Transposition System
systems.summary: System for discovering new voicings by moving familiar shapes through octaves and symmetrical transposition. Certain shapes retain function under whole-step movement (notably altered dominants). Includes ensemble techniques like trading fours and bassline-driven reharmonization. Underlying principle: artistic voice matters more than accumulation of voicings.

traversal_rules.name: Octave Exploration of Familiar Shapes
traversal_rules.summary: Discover voicings by moving voices of known shapes to higher/lower octaves.

references.quote_excerpt: moving the different voices around to higher and lower octaves

traversal_rules.name: Symmetrical Transposition with Function Retention
traversal_rules.summary: Move chord shapes by symmetrical intervals while retaining function.

references.quote_excerpt: chords shapes retain their function when moved around in certain symmetrical intervals

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Step Transposition for Dominant Alterations
traversal_rules.summary: Dominant voicings with 9ths and altered 5ths transpose by whole steps retaining function.

references.quote_excerpt: Chords with 9ths and altered 5ths may be moved around in whole steps and still retain their function

traversal_rules.name: Reharmonization via Bass Line Composition
traversal_rules.summary: Compose a new bass line to drive harmonic choices.

references.quote_excerpt: Reharmonize sections of the tune by composing a new bassline

modification_rules.name: Artistic Voice Over Theoretical Accumulation
modification_rules.summary: Prioritize artistic voice over voicing accumulation; usage matters more than quantity.

references.quote_excerpt: how you use what you know, rather than how much you know that counts

masters.name: Jens Larsen
masters.biography: Jens Larsen is a Danish jazz guitarist and educator active in the modern jazz pedagogical tradition. Through book-length works in the Fundamental Changes series and an extensive online presence, his teaching codifies a structured vocabulary of arpeggio substitutions, chromatic enclosures, and altered/diminished scale applications, organized around shared-tone analysis and voice-leading discipline. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Substitute arpeggios by shared-tone preservation, not by root
principles.summary: The improviser does not arpeggiate the root chord; instead they select the substitute arpeggio whose notes maximally overlap the underlying chord's guide tones (3rd and 7th) and color tones (9, 11, 13, altered extensions). The canonical example is G#m7b5 over Bb7alt: G# preserves F (the b7 of Bb), and the m7b5 arpeggio efficiently expresses the altered dominant's b9, #9, b13 sonority. The hierarchy is 3rd-rooted > 5th-rooted > 7th-rooted; each step trades shared-tones for color.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (Diatonic Substitution) establishes the framework; Chapter 4 (Altered Scale) develops the m7b5-on-3rd-of-altered-dominant case explicitly with the Bb7alt → G#m7b5 example on p.54.

principles.name: Chromatic enclosure as the default approach to chord tones
principles.summary: Target chord tones are approached not directly but via chromatic neighbors above and below, encircling the target before resolving onto it. This produces the 'bebop-line' surface texture but Larsen frames it pedagogically: the enclosure construction is taught as a shape (e.g. upper-chromatic + lower-chromatic + target) that can be transposed across chord tones, scale degrees, and harmonic functions. The shape generalizes; the specific notes follow from the underlying harmony.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 2 (Chromatic Enclosures) is the dedicated treatment; enclosures recur in Chapters 3-6 as a connective device across all subsequent material.

principles.name: Diminished symmetry generates upper-structure color over altered dominants
principles.summary: Four major triads embedded within each half-whole diminished scale (a minor-third-stack symmetry) become upper-structure devices for altered dominant tension. The line player selects among these triads to color the underlying altered dominant; the symmetry guarantees that each triad preserves the dominant's b9/#9/#11/13 sonority differently. Taught as a color palette, not a mechanical scale fragment.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 5 (Half-Whole Diminished Scale) develops the four-triad framework; Chapter 6's solo analysis demonstrates application in cadential contexts.

principles.name: Voice-leading via common tone, contrary motion, or encirclement
principles.summary: Every melodic move between phrases or chord changes must be justified by one of three voice-leading principles: a shared common tone held across the change, contrary motion (line moves opposite to the bass or to an implied second voice), or encirclement (line approaches the next target tone by chromatic neighbors). Large intervals must be balanced by stepwise resolution in the opposite direction. This is the criterion by which the book asks students to evaluate every melodic choice.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Counterpoint rule (large interval → stepwise opposite-direction resolution) appears across multiple chapters as a consistent connective tissue; Chapter 6 makes the voice-leading-as-criterion claim explicit through bar-by-bar solo analysis.

works.title: Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts
works.summary: Pedagogical text on improvisation as a unified harmonic and melodic system. Six chapters proceed cumulatively from diatonic substitution (Ch. 1), through chromatic enclosures (Ch. 2), harmonic minor on the V chord (Ch. 3), the altered scale (Ch. 4), and the half-whole diminished scale (Ch. 5), to a complete solo analysis integrating all prior material (Ch. 6). The book's central premise is that every arpeggio and scale choice must be grounded in shared tones with the underlying chord; voice-leading by common tone, contrary motion, or encirclement is the criterion by which the book asks students to evaluate every melodic choice.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes Music Publishing, 2018.
references.citation: Primary source for the four derived systems. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-larsen-modern-jazz-guitar-concepts; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jens-larsen/modern-jazz-guitar-concepts/.

systems.name: Harmonic Substitution Lattice
systems.summary: The book's central system: a graph of diatonic and non-diatonic arpeggios organized by their shared-tone relationships to underlying chord tones. Members are the substitution arpeggio types available over each chord quality. Traversal rules govern moves between arpeggios within a bar or across changes — primarily by common tone, guide-tone preservation, or root-interval relationship (3rd, 5th, 7th). Modification rules govern how a given arpeggio is altered: shell-voicing reduction, reframing pentatonics as extended arpeggios, scale rerooting for non-diatonic options, chord-tone omission, and avoidance of unintended sus4 colors. Established in Chapter 1, this system is the lens through which the entire book operates.

traversal_rules.name: Guide-Tone Arpeggio Selection
traversal_rules.summary: Select the substitute arpeggio whose notes contain the chord's defining 3rd and 7th intervals.

references.topic: Identifying guide tones of altered dominant
references.quote_excerpt: defining tones of a Bb7alt chord are the 3rd and 7th (F and Ab)

traversal_rules.name: Shared-Tone Arpeggio Move
traversal_rules.summary: Move to a substitute arpeggio by maximizing notes held in common with the underlying chord.

references.quote_excerpt: Fmaj7 and Am7 have three notes in common

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio Root Hierarchy (3rd > 5th > 7th)
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer arpeggio rooted on the chord's 3rd; treat 5th- and 7th-rooted options with increasing caution.

references.quote_excerpt: arpeggios from the higher notes (5th and 7th), you include fewer notes
playing a m7b5 chord on the 3rd of a dominant 7 chord

traversal_rules.name: 5th-of-II Arpeggio Targets 3rd of V
traversal_rules.summary: Arpeggiate from the 5th of the II chord to voice-lead toward the 3rd of the following V chord.

references.topic: 5th of II chord targets 3rd of V
references.quote_excerpt: Playing an arpeggio from the 5th of the II chord

traversal_rules.name: Tritone Substitute Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Replace an altered dominant arpeggio with the arpeggio of its tritone substitute, preserving guide tones.

references.quote_excerpt: E7 is the tritone substitute of Bb7

modification_rules.name: Shell Voicing Reduction
modification_rules.summary: Reduce any chord to root, 3rd, and 7th only to create a spare melodic skeleton.

references.quote_excerpt: shell voicing is a chord voicing that use only the root, 3rd and 7th

modification_rules.name: Pentatonic Reframe as Extended Arpeggio
modification_rules.summary: Reinterpret a pentatonic scale as a named extended arpeggio to make its harmonic function explicit.

references.quote_excerpt: notes of D Minor Pentatonic as though they are an arpeggio and they spell a Dm7add11

modification_rules.name: Non-Diatonic Arpeggio Construction by Scale Rerooting
modification_rules.summary: Derive non-diatonic arpeggios by reading scale tones starting from an alternate root degree.

references.quote_excerpt: construct this arpeggio with notes from the scale

modification_rules.name: Chord Tone Omission for Open Sound
modification_rules.summary: Omit a defining chord tone (e.g. the 3rd) to create an open, ambiguous line acceptable within a clear harmonic context.

references.topic: omitting chord tone for open sound
references.quote_excerpt: omitted the 3rd (Bb) of Gm7 which immediately makes the line more open sounding

modification_rules.name: Sus4 Tension Avoidance over Dominant
modification_rules.summary: Avoid the 11th (C over G7) when an unwanted sus4 color is undesirable; deploy only with intent.

references.quote_excerpt: Playing a C over a G7 chord will suggest a G7sus4 sound

systems.name: Chromatic Enclosure System
systems.summary: Governs how non-chord chromatic and diatonic passing tones are organized around target chord tones to produce characteristic bebop and hardbop melodic language. Members are enclosure construction types and resolution placement positions. Traversal rules specify where chromatic notes land in the rhythmic grid (off-beats) and how the improviser moves from one enclosure to the next target note. Modification rules govern when a diatonic substitute replaces a chromatic note and how beat-2 placement displaces resolution to beat 4 for a modern unresolved effect.

members.name: Diatonic Note Substitution for Missing Half-Step

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Notes on Off-Beats
traversal_rules.summary: Place all chromatic passing notes on off-beats, keeping strong chord tones on the beat.

references.quote_excerpt: chromatic notes are generally placed on the off-beats

traversal_rules.name: Enclosure Must Target Chord / Scale Tone
traversal_rules.summary: Every enclosure must resolve to a chord or scale tone; enclosures floating free of harmonic targets are forbidden.

references.quote_excerpt: main purpose is to target or gravitate towards scale / chord tones

traversal_rules.name: Beat-2 Enclosure Displacement
traversal_rules.summary: Place an enclosure on beat 2 so it resolves on beat 4, producing a Metheny-esque unresolved sound.

references.quote_excerpt: placed on beat 2, so that it will resolve on beat 4

traversal_rules.name: Legato Technique for Chromatic Softening
traversal_rules.summary: Use hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides so chromatic passing notes remain soft while scale tones are accented.

references.quote_excerpt: passing note is accented and the scale note is softer

modification_rules.name: Diatonic Gap Fill When Half-Step Absent
modification_rules.summary: When a chromatic half-step gap does not exist between two enclosure notes, substitute the diatonic note above.

references.topic: diatonic note substitution for chromatic gap
references.quote_excerpt: not possible to add a chromatic note in between, I added a G note

modification_rules.name: Four-Note Enclosure: Suspension vs. Tension-Pull
modification_rules.summary: Deploy a four-note enclosure either to create a suspended sound preceding a chord tone, or as a tension-and-pull device driving toward resolution.

references.quote_excerpt: precede a chord tone to create a suspended sound, or they can be used as a way of

systems.name: Diminished Symmetry Triad System
systems.summary: Exploits the half-whole diminished scale's minor-third symmetry to generate the four embedded major triads and deploy them as upper-structure melodic material over dominant 7th chords (notably the 13b9 voicing). Members are the four major triads and their inversions. Traversal rules govern common-tone voice-leading, encirclement, and positional cycling. Modification rules apply the minor-third symmetry axiom to transpose any shape by m3, select triad pairs for specific extensions, and bridge to the altered scale via the B major triad.

traversal_rules.name: Minor-Third Symmetry Rotation
traversal_rules.summary: Transpose any triad shape or arpeggio by minor thirds to access all four positions within the diminished scale.

references.quote_excerpt: Half Whole Diminished scale can be moved in minor 3rds

traversal_rules.name: Common-Tone Triad Chaining
traversal_rules.summary: Connect consecutive triads by linking shared top or bottom notes across the triad boundary.

references.quote_excerpt: linking the top two notes of the D triad to the lowest two notes of the Ab triad

traversal_rules.name: Encirclement as Triad-to-Triad Transition
traversal_rules.summary: Use the last two notes of one triad to encircle the first note of the next triad.

references.quote_excerpt: last two notes of one triad to encircle the first note of the next

traversal_rules.name: Positional Cycling Before Neck Cross
traversal_rules.summary: Cycle through all four triad inversions in a single neck position before crossing to the next position.

references.topic: Cycling Triads in Position Up the Neck
references.quote_excerpt: cycling through the triads in a single position before moving to the next position

modification_rules.name: Color-Tone Triad Pair Selection
modification_rules.summary: Select the Ab+D triad pair to express b9+#9+13 color over F7; use B+Ab pair for #11+b9+#9 color.

references.topic: Ab and D Triads Conveying F7 Extensions
references.quote_excerpt: Ab and D major triads conveys the sound of an F7

references.topic: B and Ab triads — b9 #9 #11 colour
references.quote_excerpt: B and Ab major triads … b9, #9 and #11

modification_rules.name: Root-Position Triad Omission for Colour
modification_rules.summary: Omit the F major triad (root upper-structure) because it adds no harmonic colour over F7.

references.quote_excerpt: don't often use the F major triad because it doesn't add any colour

modification_rules.name: Altered-Diminished Bridge via B Major Triad
modification_rules.summary: Use the B major triad to bridge from diminished to altered territory, since it is shared by both scales.

references.topic: B Major Triad Intervals Against F Root
references.quote_excerpt: B major triad is not unique to the diminished sound

systems.name: Melodic Line Construction System
systems.summary: A system for assembling complete jazz improvisation lines from named melodic gestures: ascending arpeggio + descending scale (the master's signature shape), Coltrane 1-2-3-5 and 1-b3-4-5 groupings, motif repetition with alteration, and the counterpoint rule that large intervals resolve stepwise in the opposite direction. Traversal rules specify gesture-to-gesture connections; modification rules govern rhythmic subdivision variation, scale-tone interpolation, and motivic alteration. This system wraps the harmonic substitution lattice and chromatic enclosure systems in a procedural envelope.

traversal_rules.name: Target-Note Approach
traversal_rules.summary: Organize the line by working backward from a specific chord tone to reach on the next strong beat.

references.quote_excerpt: both motif-based and target note ideas in your arsenal

traversal_rules.name: Motif Sequencing Across Chord Changes
traversal_rules.summary: Carry a rhythmic or intervallic motif across a chord change by restating it on the new harmony's arpeggio.

references.quote_excerpt: descending Bbmaj7 arpeggio motif is repeated using the descending E diminished arpeggio

traversal_rules.name: Counterpoint Large-Interval Stepwise Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: After a large ascending interval, resolve the line stepwise in the opposite (descending) direction.

references.topic: Counterpoint rule: large intervals resolve stepwise
references.quote_excerpt: large ascending intervals with a stepwise movement in the opposite direction
tension of the large ascending interval is resolved with step-wise motion

traversal_rules.name: Harmonic Minor Dominant Pull
traversal_rules.summary: Over the V chord, apply harmonic minor material to create a pull toward the next tonic chord.

references.quote_excerpt: harmonic minor on Bb7 to pull towards the Eb7

modification_rules.name: Melodic Repeat with Slight Alteration to Fit New Harmony
modification_rules.summary: Restate a melodic phrase on a new chord, changing only one or two notes to align with the new harmony.

references.quote_excerpt: repeats it over the Edim chord, altering it slightly to fit

modification_rules.name: Rhythmic Subdivision Embellishment
modification_rules.summary: Introduce triplet or sixteenth-note subdivisions within an eighth-note arpeggio line to vary phrasing and imply passing chords.

references.topic: Rhythmic variation with triplet passing notes
references.quote_excerpt: 8th note triplet is used for the chromatic passing notes

modification_rules.name: Scale-Tone Interpolation to Avoid Predictability
modification_rules.summary: Add scale tones between arpeggio notes while ensuring chord tones still land on the beat.

references.quote_excerpt: Embellishing an arpeggio by adding scale notes in between

modification_rules.name: Coltrane Pattern Minor Variant (1-b3-4-5)
modification_rules.summary: On minor chords, apply the 1-b3-4-5 variant of the Coltrane pattern to break up scalic lines.

references.topic: Chapter assignments: II V I lick writing
references.quote_excerpt: Coltrane patterns (1 2 35 on a major chord, 1 b3 4.5 on a minor chord)
Coltrane patterns are a useful way to break up scalic lines

masters.name: Jerry Bergonzi
masters.instrument: tenor saxophone (primary); pedagogical works widely applied across instruments including guitar
masters.biography: Jerry Bergonzi is an American jazz tenor saxophonist and educator whose *Inside Improvisation* series (Advance Music) is a foundational pedagogical corpus on melodic, harmonic, and rhythmic improvisation. Vol. 4 (Melodic Rhythms) treats rhythm as an independent compositional domain — included in batch-1 as a non-guitar source whose rhythmic systems transfer directly to guitar improvisation (line construction, comping, ensemble interaction). Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Displacement is the central generative technique for rhythmic vocabulary
principles.summary: A fixed group of consecutive notes (2-7 eighth notes, or their triplet/16th equivalents) is shifted systematically across all eight metrical entry points in a 4/4 bar. From a single source group, an expanding library of rhythmic patterns is generated without any new melodic material. The displacement logic transposes intact from eighth notes (Ch. 2-3) to triplets (Ch. 9-13) to sixteenths (Ch. 15); the same shift operation governs all three subdivision levels. This is the book's most reusable single idea.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998.
references.citation: Foundational across Chapters 1-15; recurs in Chapter 17 (the 22 one-bar rhythms from Ch. 1 reappear explicitly).

principles.name: Time zones and gears: switch metric feel as a compositional choice
principles.summary: The improviser holds multiple metric feels simultaneously as 'zones' (different time signatures or subdivisions felt internally) and 'gears' (different layers of rhythmic density), and switches between them as an expressive device rather than a mechanical bar-counting operation. Mastery is measured not by ability to count cross-meters but by the ability to feel them and move between them seamlessly during a solo.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998.
references.citation: Chapter 18 (In-the-Pocket), Chapter 19 (Time Zones), Chapter 20 (Gears), Chapter 29 (synthesis).

principles.name: Polyrhythmic superimposition with explicit resolution landmarks
principles.summary: Odd-meter phrases (5/4, 7/4, 9/4, 11/4) are played continuously over a 4/4 foundation, creating cross-rhythmic tension. Each superimposition has a precise resolution landmark: the 5/4-over-4/4 pattern realigns on the sixth bar; the 7/4-over-4/4 cycle completes in eight bars. The landmarks are the self-check the player uses to verify that the superimposition has been executed correctly across its full cycle.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998.
references.citation: Chapter 15 explicitly states the 5/4 and 7/4 resolution landmarks; superimposition framework recurs throughout Chapters 9-16.

principles.name: Four-stage internalization loop: drill → randomize → superimpose → apply
principles.summary: Every piece of rhythmic vocabulary is taken through the same four-stage learning loop: (1) structured position-by-position drilling, (2) randomization to convert rote knowledge into internalized flexibility, (3) metric superimposition layering the internalized pattern over a conflicting time signature, and (4) tune application over actual harmonic progressions. Skipping stages produces mechanical execution without expressive availability; the loop is what converts technique into feel.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998.
references.citation: Method-level protocol stated explicitly in the book-level distillation and applied consistently across every chapter's exercises.

principles.name: Composition is the evidence of rhythmic internalization
principles.summary: The book treats composition — writing new scale-and-intervallic sequences in a chosen time zone, modal sequences over backing tracks — as the demonstrable proof that rhythmic vocabulary has been internalized. Drilling without compositional application leaves the material unowned; composing forces the player to use the vocabulary as a fluent compositional element rather than recall it as an exercise.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998.
references.citation: Chapter 21 (compose your own scale-and-intervallic sequences in a time zone); Chapter 28 (twelve modal sequences for composition over backing tracks).

works.title: Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms
works.instrument_scope: saxophone-pedagogy applicable to any instrument incl. 6-string guitar
works.summary: Systematic pedagogical text on rhythm as an independent compositional domain. Twenty-nine chapters in three phases: foundational rhythmic awareness via note-group displacement (Ch. 1-8), polyrhythmic independence through triplet vocabulary and metric superimposition (Ch. 9-16), synthesis into expressive fluency via gear-switching, time zones, and rhythmic grammar (Ch. 17-29). Central technique is *displacement*: shifting a fixed consecutive-note group across all eight metrical entry points. Central method is the four-stage learning loop (structured drill → randomization → metric superimposition → tune application). Composition is treated as the evidence of internalization.

references.source: Bergonzi, Jerry. Inside Improvisation Series Volume 4: Melodic Rhythms. Advance Music, 1998. 117 pages.
references.citation: Primary source for the four derived systems. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-bergonzi-melodic-rhythms-vol-4; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jerry-bergonzi/melodic-rhythms-vol-4/.

systems.name: Consecutive Note Displacement System
systems.summary: Central system: take a fixed group of consecutive eighth notes (2-7 notes, or triplet equivalents) and systematically shift the group's starting position across all eight metrical entry points in the bar. Members are the group sizes; traversal rules govern movement between entry points; modification rules govern harmonic anticipation, omission, hemioval mirroring.

traversal_rules.name: Orderly Position-by-Position Drilling
traversal_rules.summary: Practice every entry point in order (1 through 8) before randomizing.

references.quote_excerpt: displace the grouping by starting on eight different places
practice playing five consecutive eighth notes beginning on these eight

traversal_rules.name: Randomization After Drilling
traversal_rules.summary: After mastering ordered positions, mix starting points unpredictably.

references.quote_excerpt: Start five consecutive eighth notes on different beats randomly
Try starting six consecutive eighth notes from different beats randomly
Try starting seven consecutive eighth notes from different beats randomly

traversal_rules.name: Half-Beat Displacement Shift
traversal_rules.summary: Half-beat displacement (1/2, 1 1/2, 2 1/2) produces perceptually distinct rhythmic patterns.

references.quote_excerpt: displacing a rhythm 1/2 of a beat creates entirely different sounding rhythms

traversal_rules.name: Metric Superimposition Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Layer an internalized group over a conflicting time signature (3/4, 5/4, 7/4, 9/4, 11/4 over 4/4).

references.quote_excerpt: This is a 3/4 pattern which takes three bars of 4/4 to work out
Here is a 7/4 pattern over 4/4
This is an 11/4 pattern over 4/4
the pattern should hit one on the sixth bar
takes seven bars to work out so that on the eighth bar you are starting on beat one again

traversal_rules.name: Integrate Superimposition Back Into Random Starts
traversal_rules.summary: After polymetric drills, return to random-start exercise applying time-signature devices.

references.quote_excerpt: After practicing #10-14 go back and try #9 again using some of these time signature devices
After practicing #10-14 go back and try #9 again using some of these time signature devices

traversal_rules.name: Hemioval Mirror-Offset Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Generate 5/4 mirror by restating on beat 2-and; 7/4 mirror by restating on beat 3-and.

references.quote_excerpt: By starting the same rhythm on beat 2 and that was started on beat 1 you get a 5/4 mirror
By starting the same rhythm on beat 3 and as beat 1, you get a 7/4 mirror rhythm

modification_rules.name: Harmony Anticipation at Late Entry Positions
modification_rules.summary: At late entry positions, anticipate the next chord by approaching its notes, stating it directly, or letting the final eighth note sound the next chord.

references.quote_excerpt: For #6, #7, and #8, the harmony can be anticipated
On this rhythm the last eighth note would sound the next chord
For #5, #6, and #7, the eighth notes before crossing the bar line can either anticipate the next chord
these eighth notes can anticipate the next chord, or they can be approach notes

modification_rules.name: Triplet-Gear Truncation Rule
modification_rules.summary: Fit an eight-note phrase into triplet subdivision by leaving off either the first four or the last four notes.

references.quote_excerpt: leave off the first four or the last four notes
For triplets try leaving off the first four notes

modification_rules.name: Selective Triplet Subdivision Silencing
modification_rules.summary: Sound only four of six available triplet subdivisions across two consecutive triplet groups.

references.quote_excerpt: only four of the six notes are sounded

modification_rules.name: Eighth-Note to Sixteenth-Note Doubling Conversion
modification_rules.summary: Convert any consecutive eighth-note exercise into sixteenth-note exercise by doubling up; resulting 2/4 phrase repeats twice to fill 4/4 bar.

references.quote_excerpt: convert them to sixteenth-note exercises by doubling up
repeat the 2/4 phrase twice to make a 4/4 bar

systems.name: Time Zone / Gear-Switching System
systems.summary: Five rhythmic subdivisions as interchangeable gears: quarter-note triplet, eighth-note, triplet, sixteenth-note, sextuplet. Any line can be recast in any gear; traversal rules specify shifting; modification rules reshape lines to fit a new gear.

traversal_rules.name: Isolate, Master, Combine Gear Sequence
traversal_rules.summary: Practice one zone exclusively, master it, then add the next, then move freely between zones.

references.quote_excerpt: stick to one time zone. Master that one and then go through and master the next

traversal_rules.name: Spontaneous Random Gear-Switching
traversal_rules.summary: Switch between feels randomly during improvisation rather than programming gear changes.

references.quote_excerpt: try using this technique randomly and spontaneously

traversal_rules.name: Alternating Triplet / Eighth-Note Pocket Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Play triplet rhythms then switch to eighth notes on the same tune to internalize the pocket through felt contrast.

references.quote_excerpt: play these triplet rhythms on Tune 8 and then play eighth notes to see if you feel a different pocket

traversal_rules.name: Felt-Pulse Collapse (Feeling in 1, 2, or 4)
traversal_rules.summary: While tempo stays constant, internally collapse the felt beat unit to reshape phrase contour and escape the eighth-note trap.

references.quote_excerpt: feeling the tune in four. Next play that tune in two

references.topic: Fast Tempo in Two or One
references.quote_excerpt: Fast tempo tunes are easier to play when you are feeling the tune in two
try feeling the music in two or one

modification_rules.name: Eighth-Note / Triplet Coexistence Principle
modification_rules.summary: Eighth notes coexist with the triplet feel via micro-timing (laying back, lobbing); they do not become literal triplets.

references.quote_excerpt: your eighth notes should be able to coexist with the triplet feel
laying back on the eighth notes or putting some type of lob on the notes

modification_rules.name: Expansion and Contraction of Note Groups
modification_rules.summary: Reinterpret a fixed group of notes across different beat spans, from one beat through four beats, or 1 1/2 beats.

references.quote_excerpt: Take a group of notes and try playing them over two beats
Expand and try playing these five notes over three beats, and then over four beats

modification_rules.name: Groove Internalization via Repetition at Slow Tempo
modification_rules.summary: Set metronome 50-60 on the half note and repeat a line until triplets feel natural with eighth-note phrasing.

references.quote_excerpt: repeating the line until you are in the groove of triplets
Set your metronome between 50 and 60 for the half note

systems.name: Polyrhythmic Construction System
systems.summary: Additive, subtractive, and displacement-based construction of polyrhythmic ratios (5:4, 9:4, 10:4, 12:4) and tuplet forms (quintuplet, septuplet, nonuplet), plus hemiola and scale-sequence rhythmization.

traversal_rules.name: Additive Polyrhythm Construction
traversal_rules.summary: Build a ratio by adding notes to an eighth-note line.

references.quote_excerpt: add two notes at the end of the original line to make nine notes per bar

traversal_rules.name: Subtractive Polyrhythm Construction
traversal_rules.summary: Build a ratio by removing notes from an eighth-note line.

Subtract four notes from the original line

traversal_rules.name: Offset-by-Triplet Displacement
traversal_rules.summary: Generate a variation by offsetting a polyrhythmic line by one eighth-note triplet.

references.quote_excerpt: This line could also be offset by an eighth-note triplet

traversal_rules.name: Bar-Line Displacement for Polyrhythmic Independence
traversal_rules.summary: Start polyrhythmic exercises one beat early or late so the pattern crosses the 4/4 bar line.

references.quote_excerpt: moving all of the exercises over and starting them one beat early

traversal_rules.name: Hemiola Phrase Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Play 3/4 rhythmic phrases continuously over 4/4; cycle completes after four bars of 3/4.

beginning phrases from various beats within the bar

traversal_rules.name: Tuplet Metric-Context Selection
traversal_rules.summary: Septuplets and nonuplets may be played over two, three, or four beats depending on context.

references.quote_excerpt: septuplet rhythms over four beats, three beats, or two beats
play them over two, three, or four beats

modification_rules.name: Note Omission to Generate Odd Groupings
modification_rules.summary: Leaving out one note from two groups of four creates groups of seven.

references.quote_excerpt: Leaving out one note in two groups of four creates groups of seven
