modification_rules.name: Modal Transposition of Scale Sequence
modification_rules.summary: Play pattern starting from every scale degree over a sus4 chord to remove avoid-note constraints.

references.quote_excerpt: play the pattern starting from every degree of the scale

modification_rules.name: Directional and Entry-Point Variation
modification_rules.summary: Vary a sequence by ascending/descending within it, or skipping to any scale degree as entry.

systems.name: Rhythmic Grammar and Phrasing Constraint System
systems.summary: Six explicit phrase-placement constraints from Chapter 29 functioning as compositional grammar for jazz improvisation: beat-avoidance, density variation, anticipation offsets, late-resolution offsets, silence as choice, eighth-note trap avoidance.

members.name: Silence and Endings as Active Choices
Eighth-Note Trap Avoidance via Quarter-Note Playing

traversal_rules.name: Beat-One Phrase Boundary Constraint
traversal_rules.summary: Never begin or end a phrase on beat one.

traversal_rules.name: Chord Anticipation Grammar Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Practice anticipating chord arrivals at offsets from 1/2 to 3 beats.

traversal_rules.summary: Resolve to chord tones late by 1 to 4 beats.

modification_rules.name: Eighth-Note Density Alternation

modification_rules.name: Silence and Endings as Compositional Choices
modification_rules.summary: Rests are active compositional events; knowing when to end a solo invites the next idea.

modification_rules.name: Accent-Framework Fill Rule
modification_rules.summary: Begin with accents only, then fill in around them.

masters.name: Greg O'Rourke
masters.biography: Greg O'Rourke is a jazz guitar educator behind JazzGuitar.Be, an online jazz guitar pedagogy platform. *Complete Chord Melody* codifies a procedural teach-yourself method for solo chord-melody arrangement, organized around a four-step recipe and a painting-by-numbers voicing-lookup system. Status is pedagogue (P-bucket per #345): real teaching influence, not a master voice. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Four-step chord-melody recipe
principles.summary: O'Rourke's central operating system. Step 1: pick a suitable tune (ballad-not-bebop selection rule — slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier). Step 2: transpose into a workable key (find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets — too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3: learn the melody on the top 2-3 strings (horizontal rather than positional fretting; occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are sanctioned). Step 4: voice chords below the melody on the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes (the guitar's quick decay makes a chord change imply sustain).

references.source: O'Rourke, Greg. Complete Chord Melody. JazzGuitar.Be.
references.citation: Chapter 1 states the four-step recipe; Chapters 2-7 attach lookup tables, constraints, and escape hatches to each step.

principles.name: Painting-by-numbers voicing selection (interval-on-top dictionary lookup)
principles.summary: The load-bearing organizing system. Voicings are selected by dictionary lookup keyed on three inputs: melody string, chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord. A dictionary shape is transposed into the tune's key by sliding it until its top note lands on the melody fret. The closed chord-tone interval vocabulary is fixed: 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT (passing-tone) as the explicit escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes. The 'golden rule of interval analysis' — intervals relate to the current chord, not the overall key — is elevated to a load-bearing prohibition.

references.source: O'Rourke, Greg. Complete Chord Melody. JazzGuitar.Be.
references.citation: Chapter 4 develops the painting-by-numbers rule and the transpose-by-sliding-shape mechanic; Chapter 3 states the golden rule of interval analysis.

principles.name: Three Harmonic Families taxonomy + chord-collection construct
principles.summary: Vocabulary is organized into three harmonic families (major, minor, dominant) and made indexable through chord collections. The collection construct groups chord voicings that share function and range, so the painting-by-numbers lookup can resolve a melody-note-plus-chord-type query to a single voicing shape efficiently. Tritone substitution and related substitution rules attach to the dominant family as named special cases.

references.source: O'Rourke, Greg. Complete Chord Melody. JazzGuitar.Be.
references.citation: Chapter 2 establishes the Three Harmonic Families; Chapters 5-7 develop the chord-collection construct and named substitution rules.

principles.name: Restraint as the aesthetic axiom — subtlety beats density
principles.summary: Restraint runs through every chapter as O'Rourke's most consistent prescriptive voice. Chapter 1: 'don't harmonize every note.' Chapter 4: 'less is more' and 'playability over ambition.' Chapter 7: subtlety beats density; substitutions are for occasional color, not for filling space. Three remediation strategies for awkward passages — cut notes, substitute diads or octaves, or remove the difficult voicing entirely (the author's most-used escape valve) — express the same axiom as concrete moves. The pedagogical posture is permissive about omitting voicings, hostile to over-harmonization.

references.source: O'Rourke, Greg. Complete Chord Melody. JazzGuitar.Be.
references.citation: Stated explicitly in Chapters 1, 4, and 7; remediation strategies in Chapter 4.

works.title: Complete Chord Melody
works.summary: A recipe-first, top-down teach-yourself method for chord-melody guitar. The load-bearing thesis is a four-step procedure (pick tune, transpose for range, melody on top strings, voicings below) made mechanical by a painting-by-numbers lookup keyed on melody string × chord type × melody-note-interval-to-chord. Restraint is the consistent prescriptive voice across all chapters: don't harmonize every note, playability over ambition, subtlety beats density. Three remediation strategies (cut notes, substitute diads/octaves, remove the voicing entirely) function as explicit escape hatches.

references.source: O'Rourke, Greg. Complete Chord Melody. JazzGuitar.Be.
references.citation: Chapter-loop run 2026-05-23T23-23-45-orourke-complete-chord-melody; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/greg-orourke/complete-chord-melody/.

systems.name: Chord-Melody Paint-by-Numbers Method (Four-Step Recipe)
systems.summary: The book's central thesis and operating system: a four-step chord-melody recipe that drives every later chapter as either a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of its four steps. Step 1 picks a suitable tune (ballads, not bebop, since slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier). Step 2 transposes the tune into a workable key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets, avoiding the two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 learns the melody on the top 2-3 strings, imposing a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine. Step 4 voices chords below the melody using the load-bearing painting-by-numbers rule: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord, applied to the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes, since the guitar's quick decay lets a chord change imply sustain. The interval-analysis linchpin is governed by the explicit golden rule (intervals relate to the current chord, not the overall key) backed by the major-scale-of-the-current-chord's-root reference and a closed chord-tone vocabulary (1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT). Dictionary lookup is keyed on melody string + chord type + interval, and a dictionary shape is transposed by sliding it until its top note lands on the melody fret.

members.name: Step 2: Transpose Into a Workable Key

members.name: Step 3: Learn the Melody on the Top 2-3 Strings

members.name: Step 4: Voice Chords Below the Melody
members.summary: Painting-by-numbers voicing selection: top note of the voicing equals the melody note's interval relative to the underlying chord.

traversal_rules.summary: Transpose into a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets to avoid thin (too high) or dark/muddy (too low) results.

traversal_rules.name: Transpose Dictionary Shape by Sliding to Melody Fret
traversal_rules.summary: A dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape along the neck until its top note lands on the target melody fret.

modification_rules.name: Painting-by-Numbers Voicing Selection Rule
modification_rules.summary: Pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord; the voicing may be slightly adapted as long as the melody note ends up on top.

modification_rules.name: Golden Rule: Intervals Relate to Current Chord, Not Key
modification_rules.summary: The most common beginner mistake is labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord; the reference is always the major scale of the current chord's root.

references.quote_excerpt: labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord

modification_rules.name: Don't Harmonize Every Melody Note
modification_rules.summary: Place chords selectively on long-held notes and at chord changes, exploiting the guitar's quick decay so that a chord change implies sustain rather than holding the note out.

modification_rules.name: PT (Passing-Tone) Label as Escape Hatch
modification_rules.summary: Unharmonized chromatic melody notes get the PT label rather than a chord-tone interval, and a melody note that anticipates the next chord is labeled against that next chord.

modification_rules.name: Three Remediation Strategies for Awkward Passages
modification_rules.summary: When a strict painting-by-numbers output is unplayable, cut notes out of the chord, substitute parallel octaves or diads, or remove the difficult voicing entirely (the author's most-used escape valve).

modification_rules.name: Playability Over Ambition (Less Is More)
modification_rules.summary: Easier voicings flow musically better; chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately.

modification_rules.name: Sanctioned Rhythmic Adjustment of Melody for Fingering
modification_rules.summary: Rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable to facilitate fretboard jumps, framed as a sanctioned rule rather than a compromise.

systems.name: Three Harmonic Families Taxonomy
systems.summary: Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy partitions the chord universe into three harmonic families - Major, Minor, and Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families. The sub-family distinction is what drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The chapter also formalizes the chord-construction principles that back the taxonomy: a chord symbol is an attempt to describe a sound, chords are built by stacking thirds, and 9/11/13 extensions are explained as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th. A final terminological rule - surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do - foreshadows the substitution machinery of Chapter 7.

systems.name: Chord Collection Library (ii-V-I Dictionary Keyed by String Set and Melody Note)
systems.summary: The central indexing unit of the method: a chord collection is a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note. The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I because that single progression covers all three harmonic families, and collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint. Chapter 8 consolidates the collections into a four-way split by quality and string set: Major ii-V-I on string set 4321 and on string set 5432, and Minor ii-V-i on the same two string sets with b5 and b9 alterations characteristic of the minor cadence. A separate section catalogues altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource, and a solo-guitar voicing section backs Module 4 with 6th-string-root shell voicings plus a catalog of extensions stacked on those shells.

systems.name: Voicing Construction Rules (Omittable Notes, Shells, Density)
systems.summary: The load-bearing instrument constraint of the method: the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list and a shell-voicing primitive. Chapter 2 establishes the omittable-notes priority - 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. Chapter 5 makes the shell voicing (root, 3rd, and 7th only) the primitive for solo-guitar arranging, derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first, with the 6th as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer color and a general rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. Bass-on-5th/6th-strings prominence is the load-bearing difference from trio playing, with bass-too-close-to-upper-harmony as the diagnostic for thin arrangements and a key-selection preference for A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass.

masters.name: Nelson Faria
masters.instrument: 6-string nylon-string guitar (primary); 7-string and electric also documented in catalog
masters.biography: Nelson Faria is a Brazilian jazz guitarist, composer, and educator whose *The Brazilian Guitar Book* (1995, Sher Music Co.) is the canonical English-language performance methodology for Brazilian guitar styles. His pedagogical approach centers body-first internalization (clap-and-foot drills) and a strict thumb-finger texture split that codifies five regional rhythmic vocabularies (Samba, Bossa Nova, Choro, Frevo, Baião) as deployable repertoires switched at structural moments. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Two-layer texture: thumb on-beat bass, fingers 1-3 syncopated upper voices
principles.summary: Faria's load-bearing technical architecture across all five Brazilian styles. The right hand is permanently divided into two independent rhythmic streams: the thumb anchors on-beat bass notes (alternating root and fifth, with the fifth preferably voiced below the root), while fingers 1, 2, and 3 carry the syncopated upper voices. The split is introduced in Chapter 1 (Samba) and reaffirmed identically in Chapters 2 (Bossa Nova), 4 (Frevo), and 5 (Baião), with an adapted form in Chapter 3 (Choro) simulating pandeiro. A sub-layer covers contexts where the bass moves syncopated and independently (thumb continues to handle bass) versus block-style situations (index finger takes the bass role).

references.source: Faria, Nelson. The Brazilian Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 1 establishes the split; Chapters 2, 4, 5 reaffirm; Chapter 3 adapts for pandeiro simulation in Choro.

principles.name: Kinesthetic pre-internalization — clap the upper voices, foot-tap the bass, before touching the instrument
principles.summary: Faria's most characteristic pedagogical move: before executing any rhythmic figure on the guitar, the student claps the upper-voice rhythm with their hands and taps the lower-voice rhythm with their foot. The off-instrument exercise appears explicitly in Chapters 1, 2, 3, and 5 as a methodological constant — making it a cross-style rule, not a Samba-specific drill. The intent is to install the two-layer independence in the body before the fingers are asked to execute it. The book is a *performance methodology*, not a theoretical survey: it trains the body as much as the mind.

references.source: Faria, Nelson. The Brazilian Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 1 p. 25; Chapter 2 p. 62; Chapter 3 p. 88; Chapter 5 p. 121.

principles.name: Each style reduces to a core rhythmic cell internalized before voicings
principles.summary: Every one of the five styles is taught as a foundational rhythmic cell that the student internalizes — kinesthetically, then on the instrument — before any harmonic embellishment is introduced. Samba's syncopation cliché is declared 'the single most critical figure a student must internalize,' setting the pattern: each subsequent style (Bossa Nova, Choro, Frevo, Baião) is presented as its own cell with its own characteristic syncopation, and the student's first contact with the style is the cell, not the chord progression. The harmonic language across all five styles is deliberately kept simple so the pedagogical weight falls on rhythm and texture.

references.source: Faria, Nelson. The Brazilian Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (Samba syncopation cliché as foundational); Chapters 2-5 (one cell per style); book-level distillation on the unified four-stage method.

principles.name: Numbered variations form a deployable repertoire; switch variation at structurally meaningful moments
principles.summary: Each style's core cell spawns a numbered set of variations derived from the cell through systematic transformation (rhythmic displacement, metric recontextualization, register shift, articulation density reduction, etc.). The variations are not interchangeable ornaments — they are a deployable repertoire that the performer switches at structurally meaningful moments (section boundaries, form modulations, energy changes). This converts what could be rote repetition into a structural-intention exercise: the student internalizes the cell + the variation catalog + the rule that variation choice is a formal decision.

references.source: Faria, Nelson. The Brazilian Guitar Book.
references.citation: Variation Deployment system documented across all 5 style chapters; book-level distillation on 'deployable repertoire switched at structurally meaningful moments.'

works.title: The Brazilian Guitar Book
works.summary: A systematic, performance-oriented survey of five core Brazilian guitar styles — Samba, Bossa Nova, Choro, Frevo, and Baião — arranged as self-contained parts the student works through in sequence. The book is a performance methodology, not a theoretical survey: it trains the body as much as the mind. The technical signature running across every chapter is the strict separation of the right hand into two independent rhythmic streams — thumb on-beat bass (alternating root and fifth), fingers 1-3 on syncopated upper voices — with off-instrument clapping pre-training as a cross-style methodological constant.

references.source: Faria, Nelson. The Brazilian Guitar Book. Sher Music Co., 1995.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-faria-brazilian-guitar-book; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/nelson-faria/brazilian-guitar-book/.

systems.name: Two-Layer Thumb-Finger Texture System
systems.summary: The load-bearing technical architecture of the entire book: the guitar is divided into two independent rhythmic streams at all times. The thumb anchors bass notes on the beat (root/fifth alternation, fifth preferably below root); fingers 1, 2, and 3 handle the syncopated upper voices. This division of labor is introduced in Chapter 1 for Samba, reaffirmed identically in Chapters 2, 4, and 5, and adapted for Choro's pandeiro simulation in Chapter 3. The members are the two voice layers; the traversal rules govern how the thumb assigns to bass strings and how fingers assign to upper voices; the modification rules govern alterations to articulation, register, and density within each layer.

members.name: Thumb Bass Layer
members.summary: Right-hand thumb playing on-beat bass notes, alternating root and fifth of the chord.

members.name: Finger Upper-Voice Layer
members.summary: Right-hand fingers 1, 2, and 3 playing syncopated upper voices of the chord.

members.name: Syncopated Independent Bass Sub-Layer
members.summary: Variant of the thumb layer where the bass line moves syncopated and independently, still assigned to the thumb (or index in block-style contexts).

traversal_rules.name: Thumb On-Beat Bass Assignment
traversal_rules.summary: The thumb is always assigned to the bass note landing on the beat; fingers 1-3 are always assigned to the syncopated upper voices.

systems.name: Variation Deployment System
systems.summary: Across all five Brazilian styles taught in the book, numbered rhythmic variations are not merely technical exercises but a deployable performance vocabulary. The members are the numbered variation sets for each style (Samba 1–17, Bossa Nova 1–4, Choro, Frevo 1–5, Baião 1–5). The traversal rules govern when and how to move between variations within a performance: any variation can sustain through an entire song, or variations can be mixed mid-song, with section endings and cadenzas being the idiomatic switch points. This system turns the variation inventory into a compositional grammar for rhythmic variety.

systems.name: Style-Specific Rhythmic Cell System
systems.summary: Each of the five Brazilian styles is organized around a single foundational rhythmic cell from which all variations are derived. For Samba the cell is the syncopation cliché; for Bossa Nova it is the clave (which must remain directionally fixed throughout a performance); for Choro it is the pandeiro simulation pattern; for Frevo it is the syncopated march figure with upbeat accent; for Baião it is the pedal-tone counterpoint. The members are these five style cells. The traversal rules govern how cells constrain variation generation and how the clave direction must hold. This system is the per-style grammar layer sitting above the universal two-layer texture.

members.name: Samba Syncopation Cliché
members.summary: The irreducible rhythmic figure found in almost every samba melody.

members.name: Bossa Nova Clave
members.summary: The rhythmic cell that is the root of all bossa nova patterns; direction must be fixed.

members.name: Choro Pandeiro Pattern
members.summary: Guitar patterns that simulate the pandeiro rhythm.

members.name: Frevo Syncopated March Cell
members.summary: Syncopated march figure with melodic accent on the upbeat.

members.name: Baião Pedal-Tone Cell
members.summary: Syncopated pedal-tone bass against on-beat upper voices; mixolydian or lydian b7.

traversal_rules.summary: Baião texture requires the bass to play a syncopated pedal tone while upper voices play on the beat.

references.quote_excerpt: syncopated pedal tone against the upper voices of the chord, played on the beat
harmonies are quite simple with much use of pedal tones in the bass

modification_rules.name: Batucada Syncopation Mixing
modification_rules.summary: The samba cell can be extended to a four-measure cycle that mixes syncopated and non-syncopated rhythms to produce the batucada style.

references.quote_excerpt: pattern mixes syncopated and non-syncopated rhythms, generating a batucada style pattern

modification_rules.name: Register Voice-Switching for Agogô Simulation
modification_rules.summary: The upper-voice layer can be modified by switching between low and high register positions to simulate the two-pitch agogô.

references.quote_excerpt: to simulate the agogô you can play the chord changes, switching from low to high voices

modification_rules.name: Berimbau Open-String Device
modification_rules.summary: The baião cell can be modified by using an open string as a drone to simulate the berimbau timbre.

references.quote_excerpt: using a G open string simulating a berimbau pattern

modification_rules.name: Modal Context Assignment for Baião
modification_rules.summary: Baião patterns are assigned to mixolydian or lydian b7 modal contexts.

references.quote_excerpt: baiao melodies are usually in the mixolydian or lydian b7 modes
practice these patterns and variations using a static seventh chord

systems.name: Choro Form and Harmony System
systems.summary: Choro is the only style in the book with a prescribed macro-formal structure that governs harmonic navigation: a three-part A-B-C form in which Part C modulates to the V or IV key center. The harmonic vocabulary is also prescribed: triads, seventh chords, and inverted diminished chords, with bass-line movement treated as structurally primary. This system is smaller and more style-specific than the others—it operates above the texture and variation layers and governs the large-scale harmonic trajectory of a choro performance.

members.name: Choro Section A
members.summary: First part of the three-part choro form.

members.name: Choro Section B
members.summary: Second part of the three-part choro form.

members.name: Choro Section C
members.summary: Third part; modulates to the V or IV chord key center.

members.name: Triad and Seventh Chord
members.summary: Core harmonic vocabulary of choro accompaniment.

members.name: Inverted Diminished Chord
members.summary: Diminished chords typically voiced in inverted positions.

traversal_rules.name: Section C Modulation to V or IV Key Center
traversal_rules.summary: When navigating to Section C of the choro form, the harmony modulates to the key center of the V or IV chord.

references.quote_excerpt: choro form is frequently divided in 3 parts — A, B and C — with a modulation to the V or IV

modification_rules.name: Diminished Chord Inversion Preference
modification_rules.summary: Diminished chords in choro are preferentially voiced in inverted positions rather than root position.

references.quote_excerpt: triads, seventh and diminished chords are common, usually in an inverted position

masters.name: Pat Martino
masters.instrument: 6-string electric guitar (primary); steel-string, semi-hollow archtop most associated with his sound
masters.biography: Pat Martino (1944-2021) was an American jazz guitarist and composer whose 1989 method *Linear Expressions* (Hal Leonard) codifies his lifelong harmonic-structure-first approach to improvisation. Martino's work centers the conviction that harmonic structure dictates melodic choice — that every line form on the neck is determined by the chord inversion the player is voicing under it, not by free melodic invention. His system reduces the full complexity of jazz harmony to five 'activity' forms drawn from the relative-minor relationship plus a small set of interval-formula substitutions that handle every altered or complex chord. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Harmonic structure dictates melodic choice — improvisation is not free invention
principles.summary: The single governing principle of Linear Expressions. Improvisation is not free melodic invention; the chord encountered selects the line form played. Every melodic decision in the system is downstream of harmonic identification. The book-level distillation explicitly characterizes the pedagogical arc as moving 'from the local to the global,' and the master's fundamental unit is the 'activity' — a melodic line form improvised over a harmonic structure, not freely chosen. Recurs across all four phases as the meta-rule the system serves.

references.source: Martino, Pat. Linear Expressions. Hal Leonard, 1989.
references.citation: Book-level distillation (Statement Overview); foundational across Chapters 1-4.

principles.name: Five-activity positional mapping: chord inversion selects line form
principles.summary: The neck is partitioned into five numbered 'activity' line forms derived from the G minor / Bb major relative relationship. Each of the four chord inversions maps to exactly one numbered activity: Inversion Form 1 → Activities 1 and 5; Form 2 → Activity 2; Form 3 → Activity 3; Form 4 → Activity 4. Activities 1 and 5 share Chord Inversion Form 1 and bookend one octave, making the system cyclically repeatable up and down the neck without limit. Key is determined by position alone, so the entire mapping transposes to all twelve keys by shifting position.

references.source: Martino, Pat. Linear Expressions. Hal Leonard, 1989.
references.citation: Chapter 1 establishes the five activities and inversion-selects-activity mapping (p. 10, p. 15); Chapter 2 extends across twelve keys.

principles.name: Harmonic reduction: any complex chord reduces to a simple minor via interval-formula substitution
principles.summary: Unfamiliar, altered, and extended chords are inversions or incomplete versions of familiar diatonic structures, and a small set of interval-formula substitution rules reduces them to a simple minor form the activities system already handles. Five operative reductions: same-quality/same-name interchange; m7b5 → minor a minor third up; dom9 → minor a perfect fifth up; altered-dom → minor a half-step up; augmented triad → minor-maj7 a major sixth up (three enharmonic roots). Plus a scale-choice preference for altered dominants (melodic minor over harmonic minor because only melodic minor contains the b7). This reductionist logic converts the full complexity of jazz harmony into the closed activities system.

references.source: Martino, Pat. Linear Expressions. Hal Leonard, 1989.
references.citation: Chapter 3 (Phase III) introduces equivalence table; Chapter 4 (Phase IV) provides the five interval-formula rules at pp. 55-57.

principles.name: Position determines key — the system transposes by shifting position alone
principles.summary: Key is determined by position alone; the entire activities system transposes to all twelve keys by shifting position on the neck. The first activity starting in the third position is G minor; starting in the fifth position is A minor. Phase II (Chapter 2) extends this vertically across every minor key with no rule changes — the chapter consists entirely of fingerboard diagrams applying the Chapter 1 rule across all twelve minor keys in a single fingerboard sector. Coverage is extended; the operative rule is unchanged.

references.source: Martino, Pat. Linear Expressions. Hal Leonard, 1989.
references.citation: Chapter 1 p. 11 ('The first activity starting in the third position is the key of G minor. The first activity starting in the fifth position is the key of A minor.'); Chapter 2 (Phase II) extends vertically across twelve keys.

works.title: Linear Expressions
works.summary: A four-phase method for jazz guitar improvisation organized around the single governing principle that harmonic structure dictates melodic choice. The neck is partitioned into five numbered 'activity' line forms derived from the G minor / Bb major relative relationship; each activity maps to a specific chord inversion, so the chord encountered selects the line form played. Phase I establishes horizontal coverage within one key; Phase II extends vertically across all twelve keys; Phase III integrates both axes through ten line studies arranged in five ascending pairs with an explicit student-completion directive on the final pair; Phase IV closes the system with a small set of interval-formula substitutions that reduce any altered or complex chord to a simple minor form the activities system handles. The system is cyclically repeatable because activities 1 and 5 share inversion form 1 and bookend an octave.

references.source: Martino, Pat. Linear Expressions. Hal Leonard, 1989.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-martino-linear-expressions; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/pat-martino/linear-expressions/.

systems.name: Chord-to-Activity Positional Mapping System
systems.summary: The central system of Linear Expressions. The neck is divided into five positional scale forms (activities) derived from the relative major/minor relationship (G minor / Bb major). Each chord inversion maps to exactly one numbered activity form, making the chord encountered the operative selector for the melodic line form to be played. Activities 1 and 5 share an inversion and bookend one octave, making the system cyclically repeatable. Key is determined solely by position on the neck — the same activity shape played higher or lower yields a different key — so the entire system transposes without changing its internal logic. Phase I covers one key horizontally; Phase II extends vertical coverage to all twelve keys; the same mapping rule governs both phases without modification.

members.name: Activity Form 1
members.summary: Starts in third position (G minor), spans frets 2-6, outlines G minor scale. Shares chord inversion with Activity 5.

members.name: Activity Form 2
members.summary: Lies in fifth position, spans frets 5-8. Outlines a Bbmaj7 arpeggio starting from the major 7th degree (A note).

members.name: Activity Form 3
members.summary: Primarily in seventh position with a single out-of-position extension (Bb on second string).

members.name: Activity Form 4
members.summary: Fourth positional form covering the upper neck region in its sector.

members.name: Activity Form 5
members.summary: Completes full one-octave fingerboard coverage. Begins on same sixth-string note (G) as Activity 1. Shares chord inversion (Form 1) with Activity 1.

members.name: Chord Inversion Form 1
members.summary: Corresponds to Activities 1 and 5.

members.name: Chord Inversion Form 2
members.summary: Corresponds exclusively to Activity Form 2.

members.name: Chord Inversion Form 3
members.summary: Corresponds exclusively to Activity Form 3.

members.name: Chord Inversion Form 4
members.summary: Corresponds exclusively to Activity Form 4.

traversal_rules.name: Chord Inversion Selects Activity Form
traversal_rules.summary: The chord inversion encountered determines which numbered activity form is played — the inversion is the selector, not melodic preference or position alone.

references.quote_excerpt: Each chord form will dictate the activity to be played
Form1 Forms 1&5 / Form 2 Form 2 / Form3 Form 3 / Form4 Form4

traversal_rules.name: Horizontal Ascending Position Continuity
traversal_rules.summary: Activities are traversed in ascending positional order up the fingerboard until all twelve frets (one octave) are covered before cycling back.

references.quote_excerpt: the five scale patterns move horizontally up the fingerboard, until all twelve frets (one octave) are covered
The next logical form to follow, would be the first form of activity one octave higher

traversal_rules.name: Position Determines Key (Transposition by Shift)
traversal_rules.summary: Changing position while keeping the same activity form shape changes the key; the form itself is position-independent.

engine_payload.note: position shift = key change, form invariant

references.quote_excerpt: The first activity starting in the third position is the key of G minor. The first activity starting in the fifth position is the key of A minor
Locate the four chord inversions and scales. Play the appropriate activity (line form), according to the chord inversion

traversal_rules.name: Cyclic Octave Wraparound at Activity 5
traversal_rules.summary: When Activity 5 is reached, the system wraps to Activity 1 one octave higher.

references.quote_excerpt: both the fifth and first activities start with the same note on the sixth string … The first and fifth activities share the same chord inversion

modification_rules.name: Positional Scale Coverage (Non-Root-Based Form)
modification_rules.summary: Activity forms display every accessible scale tone in position rather than starting and ending on the root.

engine_payload.note: scale forms are position-complete, not root-anchored

references.quote_excerpt: The scale forms illustrated herein do not necessarily start and end on the root of the scale. They instead display every accessible scale tone in position

systems.name: Harmonic Substitution and Reduction System
systems.summary: This system converts any complex or altered chord into a simple minor form the activities system can handle. Five operative rules: same-name/quality interchange; major-family to relative or secondary relative minor; m7b5 to minor via minor third above; dominant ninth to minor via perfect fifth above; altered dominants to minor via half-step above. Augmented triads yield three minor-maj7 substitutions (one per enharmonic root name). Scale choice for altered dominants prefers melodic minor over harmonic minor because only melodic minor contains the b7.

traversal_rules.name: Same-Name/Quality Chord Interchange
traversal_rules.summary: Chords of the same name and quality are freely interchangeable (Cmaj=Cmaj7=Cmaj9; C7=C9=C13).

references.quote_excerpt: Substitute chords of the same name and quality … Cmaj = Cmaj7 = Cmaj9 … C7 = C9 = C13

modification_rules.name: Relative Minor Reduction Rule
modification_rules.summary: Replace any major-family chord (maj6, maj7, maj9) with its relative minor.

references.quote_excerpt: Cmaj6 = Am7 (Relative minor) … Cmaj7 = Am9 (Relative minor)
complex chord changes can often be reduced to or substituted by more simplistic minor chord forms

modification_rules.name: Secondary Relative Minor Fallback Rule
modification_rules.summary: When primary relative minor clashes with bass note, substitute III chord of major key.

references.quote_excerpt: Cmaj9 = Em7 (Secondary relative minor)
we go to the secondary relative minor of Db major … namely, F minor

modification_rules.name: m7b5 → Minor via Minor Third Above Root
modification_rules.summary: Replace m7b5 with minor rooted a minor third above; m7b5 is incomplete dominant 9th and inversionally equivalent to m6.

references.quote_excerpt: From the root of a minor seventh flat five (m7b5) chord, go up a minor third and play minor
Minor seventh flat five (m7b5) chords are incomplete dominant ninth chords
the Bm7b5 chord is a Dm6 chord in its third inversion

modification_rules.name: Dominant Ninth → Minor via Perfect Fifth Above Root
modification_rules.summary: Replace dominant 9th with minor rooted a perfect fifth above.

references.topic: dominant ninth to minor conversion rule
references.quote_excerpt: go up a perfect fifth from the root of the 9th chord and play minor. i.e.: G9 … up a P5 = Dm6

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant → Minor One Half-Step Above Root
modification_rules.summary: Replace altered dominant with minor rooted one half-step above the dominant root.

references.quote_excerpt: go up a step from the root of the dominant chord and play minor. EXAMPLE: For Gb7 altered, go up a step and play G minor

modification_rules.name: Augmented Triad → Minor-Maj7 via Major Sixth Above Root
modification_rules.summary: Over augmented triad, go up a major 6th and play melodic or harmonic minor; three enharmonic root names yield three options.

references.quote_excerpt: Over an augmented triad, go up a major 6th from the root and play either melodic or harmonic minor

references.topic: augmented triad as incomplete minor maj7
references.quote_excerpt: the augmented triad is an incomplete minor (Ma7) chord
Each note of the triad can be called the root and can name the triad

modification_rules.name: Melodic Minor Preference Over Altered Dominant
modification_rules.summary: Prefer melodic minor over harmonic minor over altered dominant because only melodic minor contains the b7.

references.topic: melodic vs harmonic minor over altered dominant
references.quote_excerpt: only the melodic minor contains the b7, which is the 'hot' note of a dominant 7th chord

systems.name: Line Study Composition and Fingerboard Integration System
systems.summary: Phase III organizes practice and composition through ten line studies arranged in five ascending pairs (A and B), each pair anchored to a numbered activity form in G minor. Chord progressions are held fixed by letter (A vs B), so harmonic context is invariant while position ascends. Studies carry continuation arrows for cyclical use; the final pair 5A/5B is left incomplete for student composition. A five-step workflow governs application to jazz standards.

traversal_rules.name: Ascending Pair Progression Up the Fingerboard
traversal_rules.summary: Each successive pair of line studies moves higher on the fingerboard.

references.quote_excerpt: Starting with line studies 2A and 2B, the line studies will ascend up the fingerboard with each successive set of line studies

traversal_rules.name: Fixed Chord Progression by Letter Designation
traversal_rules.summary: Chord progression is determined by letter A or B, not position; harmonic context is invariant across ascending studies within each letter group.

references.quote_excerpt: Line studies 1A, 2A, 3A, 4A and 5A will share the same chord progression

traversal_rules.name: Cyclic Continuation Arrow
traversal_rules.summary: Continuation arrow at end of each line study signals the study can cycle back to its starting chord for open-ended improvisation use.

references.quote_excerpt: you will notice an arrow. This merely suggests that the line study can continue onward, starting again on a G minor chord

modification_rules.name: Student Composition Directive (Studies 5A/5B)
modification_rules.summary: Studies 5A/5B are intentionally left incomplete, requiring the student to generate original line content using the same forms and progressions.

references.quote_excerpt: studies 5A and 5B have been started and left for you to complete

modification_rules.name: Five-Step Substitution-to-Composition Workflow
modification_rules.summary: Five-step workflow: study original form, learn voicings and substitutions, memorize through analysis, play sample solos, compose original solos — applied to jazz standards.

references.quote_excerpt: 1) Study the song in its original form … 5) compose your own solo over the changes
apply the principles learned in this work to more common forms of progressions and songs

masters.name: Ted Dunbar
masters.instrument: 6-string electric guitar; theory/composition framework applies to all instruments
masters.biography: Ted Dunbar (1937-1998) was an American jazz guitarist, composer, and influential educator who taught at Rutgers University. Acknowledged in his own published works, his circle of mentors and collaborators included David Baker and George Russell — and his theoretical work sits alongside Russell's Lydian Chromatic Concept as a foundational American jazz-theory tradition. *A System of Tonal Convergence* (1975, self-published) codifies his harmonic framework: the tonal gravity law as a single founding axiom from which a complete improvisational and compositional method extends. Dunbar's characteristic move is to treat the entire chromatic scale as always available, governed by five awareness factors rather than fixed prescriptive rules. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Tonal gravity law: every chromatic pitch bears cadential pull to any active key
principles.summary: The founding axiom of Dunbar's method, named explicitly in Chapter 1. Every pitch in the chromatic scale exerts a cadential pull toward any active tonal center. From this single law the rest of the method extends: chromaticism is not 'outside the key' but a structured pull toward it, and convergence is the formal mechanism by which chromatic motion arrives at the target. The dominant seventh chord — with its inherent tritone — is the primary convergence vehicle; the Circle of Gravities (Chapter 8) maps tritone relationships as a navigational layer over the law.

references.source: Dunbar, Ted. A System of Tonal Convergence for Improvisors, Composers and Arrangers. 1975.
references.citation: Chapter 1 establishes the law (p. 4); Chapter 8 introduces the Circle of Gravities as the tritone-relationship map (p. 66-70).

principles.name: 24-scale convergence engine: target tonality bombarded from all harmonic directions
principles.summary: Dunbar's technical apparatus: a fixed inventory of twenty-four scales, organized by harmonic color (major, minor, augmented, dominant, diminished), that collectively surround any target tonal center and can approach it from any harmonic direction. Practitioners do not select a single scale and apply it; they rotate multiple scales in varying orders over a single target chord — especially the dominant seventh — deploying convergent approaches in turnbacks, cadences, and cycles. The engine was conceived at the II-V cadence, which Dunbar identifies as the seed of the entire system. Every scale's last note is a whole or half step from the root, 3rd, or 5th of the target (the Universal Last-Note Resolution Rule).

references.source: Dunbar, Ted. A System of Tonal Convergence. 1975.
references.citation: Chapter 2 builds the technical core (Scale Justification Sheets + universal voice-leading rule, p. 22); Chapter 3 introduces individual scales (p. 30); Chapter 4 demonstrates convergence via mode + tetrachord (p. 50); Chapters 5-7 present in pure notation.

principles.name: No wrong notes — five governing factors license full chromatic availability
principles.summary: Dunbar's 'no wrong notes' philosophy: the entire chromatic scale is available to the improvisor at all times, governed not by fixed prescriptive rules but by five awareness factors — key center, Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, Resolution, and the artist's confidence. This is not permissiveness; it is rigorous conditional availability, where each note's correctness is determined by the operative awareness factors at the moment of execution. Mistakes are reframed as 'new harmonic fields' (the Mistake-as-Generative-Event rule, Chapter 4) — an error becomes a generative event that the player navigates back to the target via the same convergence framework that governed the original line.

references.source: Dunbar, Ted. A System of Tonal Convergence. 1975.
references.citation: Chapter 3 (no-wrong-notes principle + five factors, p. 31); Chapter 4 (mistake as generative event, p. 50).

principles.name: Consciousness over prescription: the system increases awareness, not rules
principles.summary: Dunbar's pedagogical meta-principle: the System of Tonal Convergence is designed to increase the player's harmonic awareness, not to dictate procedure. The 24-Scale Engine, the Circle of Gravities, the Tonal Gravity Law, the five governing factors — all are mechanisms for cultivating perception. Practitioners do not learn a sequence of mechanical rules; they internalize a perceptual framework within which any chromatic move can be justified, evaluated, and connected to the target. Ear priming via cadences and sound projection (Chapter 2) are the preparatory practices that make this cultivated awareness operational.

references.source: Dunbar, Ted. A System of Tonal Convergence. 1975.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (governing metaphor); Chapter 2 (ear priming via cadences, p. 18; sound projection technique, p. 20); Chapter 4 (consciousness-over-prescription as explicit modification rule).

works.title: A System of Tonal Convergence for Improvisors, Composers and Arrangers
works.summary: Dunbar's foundational harmonic-theory text constructing a complete framework for organizing the chromatic scale into directed motion toward any tonal center. The central claim — stated in Chapter 1 and elaborated across Chapters 2-8 — is the tonal gravity law: every chromatic pitch bears a cadential relationship to any active key center. From that single axiom the book builds outward: a fixed inventory of 24 scales organized by harmonic color (major, minor, augmented, dominant, diminished) that 'bombard' a target tonality from all harmonic directions, visualized as a 360° compass with the key center at its hub. The dominant seventh chord — with its inherent tritone — is the system's primary convergence vehicle. Two systems constitute the method: the 24-Scale Convergence Engine (technical apparatus) and the Tonal Gravity Framework (perceptual / philosophical infrastructure), introduced together with the Circle of Gravities in Chapter 8 as a navigational map of tritone relationships.

references.source: Dunbar, Ted. A System of Tonal Convergence for Improvisors, Composers and Arrangers. Self-published, East Brunswick NJ, 1975.
references.citation: Primary source. systems.name: The 24-Scale Convergence Engine
systems.summary: The central system of the book: a fixed set of 24 scales (organized by harmonic color — major, minor, augmented, dominant, diminished) that surround any target tonal center like a 360-degree compass and 'bombard' it with directed harmonic motion. Each scale is justified by a universal voice-leading rule ensuring its final note lands within a whole or half step of the root, third, or fifth of the target tonality, making every scale a valid convergence vehicle. The system was conceived at the II-V cadence and treats the dominant seventh chord — with its tritone — as the most load-bearing chord type. Practitioners rotate multiple scales in varying orders over a single target chord, deploying them in turnbacks, cadences, and cycles. The governing philosophy is that harmonic fluency arises from understanding tonal gravity rather than from avoiding dissonance.

members.name: Major Color Scales
members.summary: Scales with major harmonic character, including Scale #16 (major with raised 4th / Lydian-like construction).

members.name: Minor Color Scales
members.summary: Scales with minor harmonic character, including Dorian mode (Scale #20) and G Jazz Melodic Minor.

members.name: Dominant Color Scales
members.summary: Scales with dominant seventh harmonic character; central to convergence on the V chord.

members.name: Diminished Color Scales
members.summary: Two diminished scale types: H-W (Scale #4) and W-H (Scale #5), each offering distinct approach colors.

members.name: Augmented Color Scales
members.summary: Scales with augmented harmonic character, completing the five-color spectrum.

members.name: Scale #11 - Descending Tritone Scale
members.summary: The author's personal scale containing an E-Bb tritone; descending tritone-based construction.

members.name: Scale #15 - Half-Diminished / Half-Whole-Tone
members.summary: Bipartite scale: first half yields diminished color, second half whole-tone; E natural functions as leading tone resolving to F.

members.name: Scale #16 - Major with Raised 4th
members.summary: Lydian-like construction beginning on the 7th of the V chord of F major; raised 4th is the major 3rd of the V chord.

members.name: Target Tonal Center
members.summary: The key center at the middle of the 360-degree compass; the gravitational destination all 24 scales converge upon.

members.name: Dominant Seventh Chord
members.summary: The most flexible chord type in the system, hosting the tritone and variants including 7b9, 7#5, 7#9; pivot of convergence at the II-V cadence.

traversal_rules.name: Universal Last-Note Resolution Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Every scale ends with its final note a whole or half step away from a chord tone of the target.
