systems.name: Tonal Gravity Framework
systems.summary: Theoretical sub-system establishing the philosophical and perceptual conditions under which the 24-scale engine operates. Every chromatic pitch has a cadential relationship to any key center (the 'tonal gravity law'), and fluency comes from the practitioner's conscious awareness of five governing factors: key center, Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, Resolution, and the artist's confidence. This framework licenses the 'no wrong notes' principle and positions the Circle of Gravities as the navigational tool for chord-on-chord motion. Priming the ear with cadences and projecting oneself aurally to the target tonality are the key preparatory practices. Mistakes are reframed as generative events; consciousness replaces prescriptive rules as the governing principle.

members.name: Key Center
members.summary: The tonal destination at the center of the 360-degree system.

members.name: Vantage Point
members.summary: The chromatic starting position relative to the key center.

members.name: Interval Tonics
members.summary: Pitch relationships defining cadential pull toward the key center.

members.name: Resolution
members.summary: The directed arrival at the tonal destination.

members.name: Artist's Confidence
members.summary: Internalized readiness enabling spontaneous chromatic deployment.

members.name: Circle of Gravities
members.summary: Systematic map of tritone relationships used as navigational tool in Ch. 8.

traversal_rules.name: Ear Priming via Cadences
traversal_rules.summary: Prime the ear with cadences into the target tonality before each scale.

references.quote_excerpt: brainwash your ear with the tonality ... with several cadences into the key

traversal_rules.name: Sound Projection Technique
traversal_rules.summary: Mentally project sound-attention to the target tonality before arrival.

references.quote_excerpt: project yourself sound wise to it already having your attention (sound attention) on it

traversal_rules.name: Tonal Gravity Law - Chromatic Cadential Pull
traversal_rules.summary: Every chromatic pitch exerts cadential pull toward any active key center.

references.quote_excerpt: tonal gravity law is created once you start to play music (in a key)
converginal relationship in a cadential motion sense to this key center

modification_rules.name: No Wrong Notes - Full Chromatic Availability
modification_rules.summary: Given the five governing factors, the entire chromatic scale is at the player's disposal.

references.quote_excerpt: no wrong notes when factors such as fixation on a key center, Vantage Point, Interval Tonics, Resolution are considered

modification_rules.name: Mistake as Generative Event
modification_rules.summary: Errors are reframed as new harmonic fields to be spontaneously filled.

references.quote_excerpt: A mistake is a new field, a new octave.

modification_rules.name: Consciousness Over Prescription
modification_rules.summary: The system increases awareness rather than dictating procedure.

references.quote_excerpt: system does not dictate to the student what to do other than increase his or her consciousness

masters.name: Brent Vaartstra
masters.instrument: 6-string guitar; theory framework applies to all instruments
masters.biography: Brent Vaartstra is a contemporary American jazz guitarist, educator, and the founder of Learn Jazz Standards (LearnJazzStandards.com) and the Learn Jazz Standards podcast. *The Jazz Standards Playbook* (2019) is his ten-standards method for self-taught jazz improvisation, centered on an ear-first acquisition protocol he calls LIST (Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer) and a relational, function-driven approach to harmonic analysis. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Ear-first LIST acquisition: Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer (in order; instrument away during Listen)
principles.summary: Vaartstra's procedural method for learning any standard. The four sequential stages — Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer — function as ordered prerequisites: the instrument stays away during Listen; undivided attention governs Internalize; the ability to sing the melody alone serves as the pass/fail gate before Transfer; and within Transfer, melody is learned before chords. Sheet music's role is to verify what the ear has already acquired, never to bootstrap the learning. Chapter 1 establishes the entire protocol; every subsequent chapter assumes it as the acquisition pathway.

references.source: Vaartstra, Brent. The Jazz Standards Playbook. Learn Jazz Standards LLC, 2019.
references.citation: Chapter 1 establishes the LIST process, the strict sequencing, and sheet-music-as-verification rule.

principles.name: Guide tones (3rd, 7th) are the melodic spine; 7th-to-3rd resolves stepwise across changes
principles.summary: In functional harmony, the 3rd and 7th of each chord define its quality and carry its function. The 7th of each chord resolves by step to the 3rd of the next, producing smooth melodic connection across ii-V-I and cycle-of-fourths progressions. This guide-tone voice leading is Vaartstra's primary real-time decision-making tool: when navigating fast changes, prioritize the guide-tone line rather than full arpeggios or scales. Minimal-change voice leading (adjust only the 3rds and 7ths that carry quality; hold other tones constant) is the governing simplicity principle for harmonically dense passages.

references.source: Vaartstra, Brent. The Jazz Standards Playbook. Learn Jazz Standards LLC, 2019.
references.citation: Chapter 2 (guide tones defined as melodic spine of improvisation); Chapter 7 (reinforced in modal context); Chapter 8 (minimal-change voice leading as simplicity principle).

principles.name: Chord function is relational, never absolute: function comes from neighbors, not the chord alone
principles.summary: A chord's function — what it is doing in the music — is determined by the chords before and after it, never by the chord considered in isolation. The same Dm7 may be ii of C, vi of F, or iii of Bb depending on context. This relational stance is the foundational analytical tool of the entire method: it governs substitution analysis (backdoor dominants, tritone subs, secondary dominants are all understood by where they sit relative to the resolution), and it dictates the practice protocol (analyze tunes by the function each chord performs, not by spelling each chord in absolute terms).

references.source: Vaartstra, Brent. The Jazz Standards Playbook. Learn Jazz Standards LLC, 2019.
references.citation: Chapter 4 introduces the relational stance; Chapter 5 elaborates through secondary dominant tonicization; Chapters 8-11 apply it through tritone, backdoor, and hybrid ii-V-I substitutions.

principles.name: In modal contexts, modes are pitch collections — not linear scales to run
principles.summary: Functional harmony lives by guide-tone voice leading; modal harmony does not. In modal contexts (Dorian over So What, etc.), the mode functions as a pitch collection rather than as a linear scale — guide tones are unnecessary because the underlying chord doesn't move. The aesthetic standard is Miles Davis's space-and-expand phrasing: play a phrase, leave space, then expand, avoiding continuous note-running in favor of sparse intentional statements. Chromatic approach notes are permitted as expressive exceptions that do not disrupt modal identity.

references.source: Vaartstra, Brent. The Jazz Standards Playbook. Learn Jazz Standards LLC, 2019.
references.citation: Chapter 6 reframes scales as pitch collections; Chapter 7 establishes Dorian over So What + Miles Davis space-and-expand as the modal aesthetic standard; Chapter 9 adds the Whole-Half Diminished Scale.

works.title: The Jazz Standards Playbook
works.instrument_scope: all instruments (lead-sheet / theory text; examples in concert pitch and bass clef editions)
works.summary: Vaartstra's ten-standards method for jazz improvisation, organized around an explicitly cumulative architecture: each tune from *Autumn Leaves* through *Stella by Starlight* introduces harmonic, melodic, and practice principles that compound across chapters. The defining characteristic is an ear-first orientation — sheet music is a verification tool rather than a primary source, and effortful aural retrieval is the engine of durable musical memory. Four interlocking systems are derived: the LIST process (Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer) as procedural acquisition protocol; functional harmony navigation built outward from the ii-V-I cornerstone; modal harmony improvisation anchored in Dorian and the Miles Davis aesthetic; and a concrete improvisation practice toolkit. The ten standards are the scaffolding; the product is a transferable improvisational grammar.

references.source: Vaartstra, Brent. The Jazz Standards Playbook. Learn Jazz Standards LLC, 2019.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-29T06-51-17-vaartstra-jazz-standards-playbook; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/brent-vaartstra/jazz-standards-playbook/. PDF salvaged from a #360 mismap originally filed under Brent Greenan; OCR was correct, only the master_id was wrong.

systems.name: Functional Harmony Navigation System
systems.summary: This system governs how a jazz improviser moves through functional harmonic progressions built from the Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords. Its members are the chord quality categories (major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished, diminished 7) organized by their Roman numeral positions. Traversal rules specify how to move melodically between chord members using voice leading — primarily the stepwise resolution of guide tones (3rds and 7ths) from chord to chord, especially the 7th-to-3rd resolution across ii-V-I progressions. Modification rules describe how any chord member may be altered: diatonic minor chords may be converted to dominant 7ths, V chords may be replaced by tritone substitutes or backdoor dominants (bVII7), diminished 7 chords may substitute for rootless dominant b9 chords, and secondary dominant ii-V pairs may expand any diatonic chord into a momentary tonicization. The system's analytical stance is relational: every chord's function depends on the chords before and after it, never in isolation.

members.name: Major 7 Chord (Imaj7, IVmaj7)
members.summary: Tonic and subdominant function; formula Root-3-5-7; stable arrival points in ii-V-I cadences.

members.name: Dominant 7 Chord (V7, secondary dominants, backdoor bVII7)
members.summary: Primary tension chord; formula Root-3-5-b7; drives resolution; diatonic minor chords may be converted to dominant 7ths.

members.name: Minor 7 Chord (ii, iii, vi)
members.summary: Pre-dominant and tonic minor function; formula Root-b3-5-b7; serves as the ii in ii-V-I and as tonicization targets.

members.name: Half-Diminished Chord (iiø7)
members.summary: Minor ii chord in minor key ii-V-i progressions; formula Root-b3-b5-b7.

members.name: Diminished 7 Chord (passing dim7, rootless dominant b9)
members.summary: Functions as rootless dominant b9 substitute or chromatic passing chord; formula Root-b3-b5-bb7.

members.name: ii-V-I Progression Unit
members.summary: The cornerstone chord progression of jazz; the fundamental cell over which all traversal and modification rules operate.

traversal_rules.name: Guide-Tone Voice Leading (7th resolves to 3rd)
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords in a ii-V-I or cycle-of-fourths progression, the 7th of each chord resolves by step to the 3rd of the next chord, producing smooth melodic connection.

references.quote_excerpt: the 7th of each chord resolves to the 3rd of the next chord
Voice leading is the smooth melodic movement of notes from one chord to the next

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio Connection via Nearest Chord Tone
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chord arpeggios, enter the next chord on whichever chord tone is closest to the last note played, not necessarily the root.

references.topic: chord tone arpeggio voice leading rule
references.quote_excerpt: move to the nearest chord tone of the next chord
connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord

traversal_rules.name: Cycle-of-Fourths Harmonic Movement
traversal_rules.summary: Chords and key centers progress by descending fifths / ascending fourths as the default motion of jazz harmony, governing both analysis and improvisation planning.

references.quote_excerpt: it's a perfect study of cycling 4ths, which is an important movement in jazz repertoire
This vi chord starts a long cycle of 4ths all the way through the Dbmaj7

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Connection at Key-Center Boundaries
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between rapidly modulating key centers (e.g., cycle of major thirds), prefer entering each new key center by half step or whole step at bar boundaries to maintain melodic flow.

references.topic: Connecting key centers by half/whole step
references.quote_excerpt: I do my best to enter into each new key center by half steps or whole steps

traversal_rules.name: Relational Chord Function Analysis
traversal_rules.summary: Always interpret a chord's function by examining both the chord before and after it; harmonic analysis is contextual, not absolute.

references.quote_excerpt: To understand the function of a chord, you must look at both the chord that comes before it and after it
the most important thing to pay attention to is where the chords are going

modification_rules.name: Diatonic Minor to Dominant 7 Conversion
modification_rules.summary: Any diatonic minor chord may be converted to a dominant 7 chord; this is a standard jazz harmonic practice that expands the pool of resolving dominants.

references.quote_excerpt: jazz musicians are constantly turning minor chords into dominant 7 chords
it is entirely normal and acceptable to turn minor chords in the diatonic series into dominant 7th chords

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution
modification_rules.summary: A dominant 7 chord may be replaced by the dominant 7 chord whose root lies a tritone interval away, sharing guide tones (3rd and 7th) with the original.

references.quote_excerpt: a tritone substitution, meaning, a chord is being substituted by another chord with a root a tritone interval away

modification_rules.name: Backdoor Dominant Substitution (bVII7 for V7)
modification_rules.summary: The V7 chord may be replaced by bVII7 (approaching the tonic by whole step), which shares many notes with an altered V7 and can be expanded to a full backdoor ii-V.

references.quote_excerpt: A backdoor dominant is a dominant 7th chord that substitutes the V7 chord for a bVII7 chord approaching the I chord by a whole step
the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7

modification_rules.name: Diminished 7 as Rootless Dominant b9 Substitute
modification_rules.summary: A diminished 7 chord may substitute for a dominant 7(b9) chord by functioning as its rootless voicing; all pitch classes are shared except the bass root.

references.quote_excerpt: You can think of the F#dim7 as a rootless D7(b9)
The F#dim7 chord is a substitute for the dominant VI7 chord

modification_rules.name: Secondary Dominant Tonicization (ii-V Expansion)
modification_rules.summary: Any diatonic chord may be preceded by its secondary dominant (V7/x) or a full ii-V pair, temporarily tonicizing that chord as a new tonic.

references.quote_excerpt: A secondary dominant is when a dominant 7 chord acts as a V chord of a diatonic chord other than the tonic
it's just as easy to add a ii chord in front of it to create a ii-V chord progression

modification_rules.name: Hybrid ii-V-I (Minor ii-V Resolving to Major I)
modification_rules.summary: The ii and V chords may be borrowed from the parallel minor key while resolving to the major I chord, creating an expressive surprise.

references.quote_excerpt: A hybrid ii-V-I is when a minor ii-V-i is mixed with a major ii-V-I. The ii and V are borrowed from the minor key, but they resolve to the major I
it gives you the sense that it will resolve to a minor chord, but then unexpectedly, it resolves to a major chord

modification_rules.name: Minimal-Change Voice Leading (Only Alter 3rds and 7ths)
modification_rules.summary: When navigating harmonically dense or shifting passages, adjust only the notes that define chord quality — the 3rds and 7ths — keeping all other melodic tones constant.

references.quote_excerpt: I try to adjust the notes minimally, only to accommodate the changes necessary from one chord to another
What are the defining notes of each chord? In other words, which notes differ from the chord before it

modification_rules.name: Extension Layer Permission (9ths, 11ths, 13ths)
modification_rules.summary: Any 7th chord may be extended with 9th, 11th, or 13th tones; these upper extensions are available as color tones in improvisation.

references.quote_excerpt: Utilizing the 9th, 11th and 13th can have a powerful effect

modification_rules.name: Chromatic Passing Tone Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Non-diatonic notes may be inserted between two diatonic notes by half-step to connect them; these are labeled chromatic passing tones and do not represent structural chord tones.

references.quote_excerpt: A chromatic passing tone is a non-diatonic note that connects two diatonic notes in a passage, usually by half steps
the note in question does not represent a basic chord tone, extension or alteration, but is simply chromatically approaching a diatonic note

systems.name: Modal Harmony Improvisation System
systems.summary: This system governs improvisation in modal harmonic contexts — pieces where chord movement is minimal or static and modes (rather than functional ii-V-I progressions) define the pitch language. Its members are the seven modes of the major scale, used as named pitch collections over sustained key centers. The central traversal rule is that modal arpeggios connect via the nearest chord tone rather than always starting on the root, and adjacent modes link through half- or whole-step motion. The defining modification rule is that guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required in modal contexts — the mode itself is the resource pool, and idiomatic modal playing means using modes as note choices rather than running them as scales. The Miles Davis approach to So What (sparse, space-conscious, phrase-then-expand, Dorian with occasional chromatic approach notes) is modeled as the aesthetic standard for this system.

members.name: Ionian (Major) — Mode 1
members.summary: Parent scale; W-W-H-W-W-W-H.

members.name: Dorian — Mode 2
members.summary: Primary mode for modal jazz; W-H-W-W-W-H-W; scale tone formula 1-2-b3-4-5-6-b7.

members.name: Phrygian — Mode 3
members.summary: H-W-W-W-H-W-W; darkest diatonic mode.

members.name: Lydian — Mode 4
members.summary: W-W-W-H-W-W-H; raised 4th gives bright, floating quality.

members.name: Mixolydian — Mode 5
members.summary: Major scale with b7; formula 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7; primary scale for dominant 7 chords.

members.name: Aeolian (Natural Minor) — Mode 6
members.summary: W-H-W-W-H-W-W; natural minor scale.

members.name: Locrian — Mode 7
members.summary: H-W-W-H-W-W-W; built on the leading tone; basis for half-diminished chord.

members.name: Whole-Half Diminished Scale
members.summary: Eight-note scale; W-H-W-H-W-H-W; used over diminished 7 and dominant b9 chords.

traversal_rules.name: Modal Arpeggio: Enter on Nearest Chord Tone
traversal_rules.summary: When connecting arpeggios across modal chord changes, begin the next arpeggio on whichever chord tone is closest to the last note played, not necessarily the root.

references.quote_excerpt: connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord tone of the proceeding chord

traversal_rules.name: Modal Pattern Exercise in 3rds with Half/Whole-Step Mode Links
traversal_rules.summary: Adjacent modes are connected through 3rd-based patterns that transition to the next mode by either a half step or whole step, internalizing mode relationships across the parent scale.

references.quote_excerpt: the pattern moves in 3rds and connects to the next mode in either a half step or a whole step

traversal_rules.name: Space-and-Expand Phrasing (Miles Davis Model)
traversal_rules.summary: In modal contexts, play a phrase, leave space, then expand upon that phrase — avoiding continuous note-running in favor of sparse, intentional melodic statements.

references.quote_excerpt: He plays a phrase, leaves some space, and then expands upon that phrase

modification_rules.name: Guide Tone Omission in Modal Contexts
modification_rules.summary: In modal harmony with minimal chord movement, guide tones (3rds and 7ths) are not required as connective tissue; the mode as pitch collection replaces the guide-tone framework.

references.topic: guide tones omitted for modal tunes
references.quote_excerpt: there is no need to identify the guide tones

modification_rules.name: Scales as Pitch Collections (Not Linear Drills)
modification_rules.summary: Modes and scales are to be used as harmonic resource pools — non-ordered sets of available note choices — rather than linear sequences to be played up and down.

references.quote_excerpt: The best way to think about the use of scales is 'pitch collections'

references.topic: modes as note choices, not scales
references.quote_excerpt: this doesn't mean you should play the scales like scales. You should just use them as note choices

modification_rules.name: Chromatic Approach Notes in Modal Playing
modification_rules.summary: Within Dorian or other modal contexts, occasional chromatic approach notes are permitted as expressive exceptions that do not disrupt modal identity.

references.quote_excerpt: He generally plays within the Dorian mode, with exceptions of course and occasional chromatic approach notes

systems.name: Ear-First Acquisition System (LIST Process)
systems.summary: This system describes the procedural method by which a jazz musician should learn any standard. Its members are the four sequential stages of the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer), each with defined entry criteria and outputs. Traversal rules specify the mandatory order of progression through the stages — instrument put away during Listen, undivided attention during Internalize, singing the melody alone as a pass/fail gate before Transfer, and melody learned before chords within the Transfer stage. The modification rules specify enhancements within stages: including vocal versions and learning lyrics to deepen melodic internalization, using Frank Sinatra as the reference source for straight melody, isolating bass notes to train harmonic ear, and using sheet music only as a verification check rather than a primary learning source.

members.name: Listen — Stage 1
members.summary: Instrument put away; deliberate listening to multiple recordings; no playing.

members.name: Internalize — Stage 2
members.summary: Undivided, distraction-free attention; treat music like a movie — full engagement.

members.name: Sing — Stage 3
members.summary: Sing melody both with recording and alone; pass/fail gate before touching instrument; optionally sing bass notes for harmony.

members.name: Transfer — Stage 4
members.summary: Melody first, then chords; find on instrument what can already be sung; chord learning via root identification then quality determination.

traversal_rules.name: LIST Stages Must Be Traversed in Order
traversal_rules.summary: The four stages must be completed in sequence — Listen before Internalize, Internalize before Sing, Sing before Transfer — with each stage's output serving as the prerequisite for the next.

references.quote_excerpt: Listen / Internalize / Sing / Transfer
Be sure that you can sing the melody of the standard, both along with the recording and on your own, before you learn it on your instrument

traversal_rules.name: Melody Before Chords Within Transfer Stage
traversal_rules.summary: Within the Transfer stage, learn the melody on the instrument first; only after melody is established move to learning chord progressions by ear.

references.quote_excerpt: Transfer the melody. Start learning the melody on your instrument

traversal_rules.name: Root Identification Before Quality Determination
traversal_rules.summary: When learning chords by ear, first identify the bass note (root), then determine the chord quality; these are two sequential cognitive operations, not simultaneous.

references.quote_excerpt: if you can identify that the bass player is playing a concert Eb note for the first chord, then all you have to do is figure out what quality it is

modification_rules.name: Include Vocal Recordings and Learn Lyrics
modification_rules.summary: During the Listen stage, always include at least one vocal version; learning lyrics deepens melodic memory, especially for instrumentalists who would otherwise skip this step.

references.quote_excerpt: Learning lyrics is extremely helpful for internalizing melodies

modification_rules.name: Use Frank Sinatra (or Original Artist) as Straight Melody Reference
modification_rules.summary: To establish a reliable, unembellished melody reference, use Frank Sinatra recordings (or the original composer/artist); avoid learning melody from artists known to heavily ornament.

references.quote_excerpt: Frank Sinatra is your friend. Frank almost always sings melodies straight

modification_rules.name: Extend Sing Stage to Bass Notes for Harmonic Training
modification_rules.summary: To add harmonic ear training, extend the Sing stage by singing the bass line (root motion) of the standard, not just the melody.

references.quote_excerpt: try singing the bass notes. This will help you begin to learn the harmony of the jazz standard

modification_rules.name: Sheet Music as Verification Tool Only
modification_rules.summary: Sheet music may be used to check what has been learned by ear, but must not be used as the primary learning source; ear comes first, notation confirms.

references.quote_excerpt: The idea is not to throw away sheet music but to know its place

systems.name: Improvisation Practice Toolkit System
systems.summary: This system organizes the concrete improvisation practice methods taught across all chapters into a structured toolkit. Its members are the named practice tools: guide tones, melody reference, chord tones/arpeggios, contrafact composition, motif development, transcribed licks, and composed solos. Traversal rules govern how a practitioner sequences or escalates across tools — beginning with harmonic reduction (guide tones alone, or single-note practice) before adding melodic complexity, scaffolding through the three-chorus melody exercise from straight melody to free improvisation, and transposing all learned material through all 12 keys for full internalization. Modification rules govern how each tool may be altered or combined.

members.name: Guide Tones Practice Tool
members.summary: Isolated half- or quarter-note 3rds and 7ths through the full song form; primary harmonic-melodic spine.

members.name: Melody Reference Tool
members.summary: Using the tune's written melody as a guideline during improvisation; scaffolds departure from fully straight to freely improvised.

members.name: Chord Tones / Arpeggios Tool
members.summary: Playing root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th as ordered arpeggios through changes; voice-led to nearest tone at each transition.

members.name: Contrafact Composition Tool
members.summary: A new melody composed over existing chord changes; may be simple/singable or bebop-style; must contain thematic repetition and structure.

members.name: Motif Development Tool
members.summary: Short rhythmic or melodic cells repeated and varied throughout a solo to create coherence over complex changes.

members.name: Transcribed Licks Tool
members.summary: Licks learned from recordings of great jazz musicians, internalized and transposed through 12 keys.

members.name: Composed Solos Tool
members.summary: Full solos composed at slow pace (written or memorized on instrument) to develop jazz language and phrase construction.

traversal_rules.name: Three-Chorus Melody Escalation
traversal_rules.summary: Practice escalates across three choruses: first chorus plays melody straight, second chorus embellishes it as a guideline, third chorus improvises freely while referencing melodic fragments.

references.quote_excerpt: The first chorus, just play the melody completely straight

traversal_rules.name: All Licks and Patterns Through All 12 Keys
traversal_rules.summary: Every lick, pattern, or harmonic concept must be practiced in all 12 keys to achieve full internalization and strengthen weak key areas.

references.quote_excerpt: I always suggest taking things through all 12 keys. It's a powerful practice that not only helps you internalize musical information

traversal_rules.name: Density Reduction for Harmonically Dense Passages
traversal_rules.summary: When facing confusing chord changes, reduce note density radically — down to a single note over an entire sequence — to isolate chord-tone hearing before adding complexity.

references.quote_excerpt: try playing as few notes as possible over a given chord progression. Sometimes it can even have a profound effect to play just one note over an entire sequence

references.topic: keep it simple over confusing changes
references.quote_excerpt: Whenever I come across a section within a piece of music that seems confusing

traversal_rules.name: Quarter-Note Solo Exercise (Rhythmic Reduction)
traversal_rules.summary: Practice improvisation using only quarter notes to remove rhythmic complexity, isolating harmonic navigation as the sole focus.

references.quote_excerpt: I stick to a quarter note rhythm in all of my note choices. I take out the rhythmic component from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes

modification_rules.name: Contrafact Singability and Simplicity Requirement
modification_rules.summary: A student contrafact should be simple enough to sing, should outline chord changes through chord tones, and must express a coherent theme; complexity is secondary to melodic logic.

references.quote_excerpt: I tried to keep the melody as simple as I could. My goal was to create a sing-able melody

modification_rules.name: Bebop Contrafact Mandatory Structure Rule
modification_rules.summary: Even a busy bebop-style contrafact must contain repeated sections, rhythmic clones, and deliberate structure; stream-of-consciousness is not acceptable.

references.quote_excerpt: note the themes throughout. There are repeated sections, rhythmic clones and general structure

modification_rules.name: Thematic Repetition with Variation (Space and Breathing)
modification_rules.summary: Strong improvisation includes recurring rhythmic and melodic themes with variations, and leaves deliberate space for the melody to breathe.

references.quote_excerpt: There are re-occurring rhythmic and melodic themes throughout with variations

modification_rules.name: Strong Melody Licenses Harmonic Freedom (Including Outside Playing)
modification_rules.summary: Any degree of harmonic freedom — including playing outside the chord changes — is permitted provided the improvised line is melodically strong and resolves with intention.

references.quote_excerpt: You could even play outside of the chord changes, but if your improvised melody is strong and you resolve it confidently, it's going to sound intentional

modification_rules.name: Note-Mapping Scaffold for Dense Harmonic Passages
modification_rules.summary: Over harmonically complex material, map out all available scale and chord tones as a conceptual scaffold before building melodic lines.

references.quote_excerpt: it can be beneficial to take a step back and map out the notes we have available to us

masters.name: Alan de Mause
masters.lived: active (Mel Bay catalog spans late 1970s through 2000s)
masters.biography: Alan de Mause is a Mel Bay Publications author of jazz, fingerstyle, and chord-melody method books. *Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book* (2001) is his comprehensive method for solo fingerstyle jazz guitar, sequenced from physical mechanics through harmonization interval progression to mature jazz performance language. His pedagogical signature is the conversion of a lead sheet (single-note melody + chord symbols) into a complete fingerstyle solo arrangement via a progressive harmonization hierarchy (bare → thirds → tenths → sixths → oom-pah bass) governed at every step by ear-first pragmatism. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Lead-sheet-to-solo conversion: every technical system serves the same conversion procedure
principles.summary: The book's operative procedure throughout: convert a lead sheet (single-note melody + chord symbols) into a complete solo fingerstyle guitar arrangement. Every technical system the book teaches — harmonization intervals, bass devices, voice leading, idiomatic inflection — exists in service of this conversion. The foundational source material is always the lead sheet; the complete performance is always the target. This unifying procedure is what makes the three-section arc (mechanics → harmonization hierarchy → mature jazz craft) coherent.

references.source: de Mause, Alan. Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book. Mel Bay Publications, 2001.
references.citation: Statement of Outputs Overview; reinforced across Sections 1-3 (Chapters 1-15) — every chapter contributes a technical capability to the same conversion procedure.

principles.name: Progressive harmonization hierarchy: bare → 3rd → 10th → 6th → oom-pah bass, melody primacy preserved
principles.summary: The book's central system. A single-note melody is progressively enriched by adding harmonization members in a specific sequence: bare melody, then a diatonic third below, then the third promoted to a tenth (octave-displaced functioning as bass), then a sixth below as melody shadow or bass note, and finally an oom-pah root/fifth bass underneath. Each successive member adds harmonic density while following the same chord-tone matching logic. Melody primacy must be preserved at every layer — when sixths are added, the key note stays on top; when thirds collide with the melody's own third-content, the third is omitted from the bass.

references.source: de Mause, Alan. Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 5 (thirds + oom-pah bass + bass-third omission rule pp. 77, 82-83); Chapter 6 (thirds harmonization pp. 89-92); Chapter 7 (tenths with independent rhythm p. 98); Chapter 8 (sixths with key-note-on-top p. 104, 108).

principles.name: Ear-first pragmatism: chord-tone matching is a test to pass, not a rule to follow
principles.summary: The book's animating aesthetic principle, stated explicitly in Chapter 15 and reinforced throughout Chapters 6, 8, and 10. Whatever sounds good to the ear will work, constrained only by community musical standards. Chord-tone matching is a test the arrangement must pass, never a rule the arranger follows mechanically. Non-chord-tone harmonizations are accepted if they pass the ear test; arpeggiation is abandoned when fingering demands it; voice-leading collisions can be valid solutions. This ear-first stance governs every other technical prescription in the book — without it, the harmonization hierarchy would be mechanical interval-stacking rather than a craft.

references.source: de Mause, Alan. Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 15 (animating aesthetic principle); Chapter 6 (non-chord-tone thirds and effect priority pp. 89, 92); Chapter 8 (adjustable sixths p. 104); Chapter 10 (voice-leading-collision allowance).

principles.name: Rhythmic counterpoint: when melody is busy, bass is long; when melody is sparse, bass is busy
principles.summary: de Mause's rhythmic-density rule for the melody-and-bass texture: the melody and the bass complement rather than parallel one another. When the melody is rhythmically busy and many-noted, the bass plays longer note values; when the melody is sparse, the bass becomes busier. This rhythmic counterpoint rule is what prevents the harmonization hierarchy from producing block-chord textures and instead produces independent voicing layers. It governs the tenths-as-bass rule (tenths typically have independent rhythm rather than doubling the melody) and the mixed-rhythm oom-pah rule (de-emphasize strict alternating root-fifth with quarter/half/whole note combinations).

references.source: de Mause, Alan. Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book.
references.citation: Chapter 3 (melody/bass density complement p. 56); Chapter 4 (rhythmic complementarity p. 74); Chapter 5 (mixed-rhythm bass de-emphasizes oom-pah p. 78); Chapter 7 (tenths with independent rhythm p. 98).

works.title: Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book
works.summary: de Mause's comprehensive Mel Bay method for solo fingerstyle jazz guitar, from first principles through finished performance. The three-section arc is sequenced as Section One (Chapters 1-4) establishing physical mechanics + foundational notation, Section Two (Chapters 5-8) building harmonic craft through a progressive hierarchy of harmonization intervals (third-below → tenth → sixth → oom-pah bass), and Section Three (Chapters 9-15) addressing mature jazz concerns: harmonic theory, self-accompaniment devices, accompanist technique, expressive language, voice leading, and structural conventions. The operative procedure throughout is lead-sheet-to-solo conversion: every technical system serves the conversion of a single-note melody plus chord symbols into a complete solo fingerstyle arrangement. The animating aesthetic principle, stated explicitly in Chapter 15, is that whatever sounds good to the ear will work — chord-tone matching is a test to pass, not a rule to follow mechanically. Three systems were derived: the Melody Harmonization Hierarchy (central), Jazz Harmonic Movement, and Self-Accompaniment Texture.

references.source: de Mause, Alan. Mel Bay's Complete Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar Book. Mel Bay Publications, 2001.
references.citation: Primary source. PDF salvaged from a #360 mismap originally filed under Bill Carter; OCR was correct, only the master_id was wrong.

systems.name: Melody Harmonization Hierarchy System
systems.summary: The book's central system is a progressive hierarchy of harmonization devices applied to a single-note melody sourced from a lead sheet. The members are the harmonization intervals — unison/octave (bare melody), thirds below, tenths (octave-displaced thirds functioning as bass), and sixths below — each successive member adding harmonic density while following shared chord-tone matching logic. The system governs how a player traverses from one harmonization layer to the next (adding or swapping intervals) and how each member is modified (adjusting non-chord tones, varying rhythm, handling altered extensions). The unifying rule across all members is that melody primacy must be preserved and that the underlying chord symbol is the arbiter of acceptability, with the ear as the final court of appeal.

members.name: Bare Single-Line Melody
members.summary: The unharmonized melody derived from a lead sheet; the entry-point member of the hierarchy.

members.name: Thirds Below (Parallel Thirds Harmonization)
members.summary: A harmony note a diatonic third below each melody note, played with free stroke on adjacent strings.

members.name: Tenths (Octave-Displaced Third as Bass)
members.summary: The third dropped an octave lower so it functions as an independent bass note beneath the melody.

members.name: Sixths Below (Diatonic Sixth Harmonization)
members.summary: A harmony note a diatonic sixth below the melody, functioning as a melody shadow or bass note, with the key note kept on top.

members.name: Root/Fifth Oom-Pah Bass
members.summary: Alternating root and fifth of each chord in the bass as the foundational accompaniment underneath the melody.

traversal_rules.name: Add Bass to Complete Three-Part Texture
traversal_rules.summary: When thirds harmonization is present, add root/fifth bass to complete the melody-harmony-bass three-voice arrangement.

references.quote_excerpt: Put the bass back in. Even a simple root/root and root/fifth bass can turn the melody with thirds into a full three-part arrangement

traversal_rules.name: Promote Thirds to Tenths with Independent Bass Rhythm
traversal_rules.summary: When moving from parallel thirds to tenths, assign the tenth its own independent rhythmic pattern rather than doubling the melody rhythm.

references.topic: Parallel tenths vs independent bass rhythm
references.quote_excerpt: the tenth does often function as a bass note, but only infrequently does it move parallel to the melodic rhythm. More often it has its own independent rhythm

traversal_rules.name: Rhythmic Counterpoint Between Melody Density and Bass Density
traversal_rules.summary: When the melody is rhythmically busy, use longer bass note values; when the melody is sparse, use a busier bass part.

references.topic: Bass rhythm vs melody density rule
references.quote_excerpt: If the rhythm of the melody is busy and many-noted, you can complement it by playing longer notes in the bass
Changing the bass accompaniment rhythm can change the mood or character of a piece

traversal_rules.name: Abandon Arpeggiation When Melody Leaps Unpredictably
traversal_rules.summary: Switch away from arpeggiation texture to another accompaniment technique when the melody continually leaps wildly.

references.quote_excerpt: If you notice that the melody of the tune you wish to arrange continually leaps up and down wildly, abandon arpeggiation for that particular tune

traversal_rules.name: Keep Key Note on Top When Traversing to Sixths
traversal_rules.summary: When applying sixth-below harmonization, the key note must remain on top to preserve major tonality.

references.topic: Table of Sixths key note on top rule
references.quote_excerpt: scales harmonized in sixths have the key note of each scale on top, with the harmony note a sixth below. If the key note were in the bottom position the ear would perceive the scale as being in a minor key

modification_rules.name: Omit Bass Third When Melody Already Supplies It
modification_rules.summary: When the melody note is itself a major or minor third of the chord, do not add a redundant third in the bass; use root or fifth instead.

references.quote_excerpt: when the melody is a major or minor third, the bass note is either a root or fifth, and when the melody is other than a third, the third is used in the bass to harmonically identify the chord
If the melody in that measure already contains the major or minor third of the major or minor chord indicated above, no additional third is required

modification_rules.name: Accept Non-Chord-Tone Thirds That Pass the Ear Test
modification_rules.summary: When a third-below harmonization lands on a non-chord tone, accept it if it passes an ear test; adjust to a nearby chord tone only if needed.

references.topic: non-chord tone thirds adjust or not
references.quote_excerpt: Non-chord tones make for the interesting spice in an otherwise dull chord-tone-only dish. It's your ear and your decision

modification_rules.name: Adjust Sixths That Imply Unwanted Harmonic Extensions
modification_rules.summary: When a diatonic sixth implies an extension (seventh, ninth) not intended by the chord symbol, optionally adjust the sixth to a strict chord tone.

references.topic: Melody on fifth sixth below is minor seventh
references.quote_excerpt: The minor seventh interval is contained in the C scale, so it is not a clash. You can change the C to D if you wish to keep the sound to a simple Dm chord

modification_rules.name: Sharpen or Flatten Fifth for Altered Chord Symbols
modification_rules.summary: When the chord symbol specifies an altered fifth (#5 or b5), raise or lower the bass fifth accordingly rather than deriving it from the plain major scale.

references.quote_excerpt: If the chord is c7#s, raise the fifth a half step, and if it is C7>5, lower the fifth a half step

modification_rules.name: Use Mixed-Rhythm Bass to De-Emphasize Strict Oom-Pah
modification_rules.summary: Replace strict alternating root/fifth with quarter, half, or whole notes in mixed combinations for rhythmic variety.

references.quote_excerpt: Another way of de-emphasizing the oom-pah accompaniment while still using only root/fifth is to have bass notes with mixed rhythm moving in quarter, half or whole notes

modification_rules.name: Prioritize Musical Effect Over Sustain Perfection
modification_rules.summary: Accept shorter note durations in arpeggiated harmonization when fingering constraints prevent full sustain; overall effect supersedes mechanical completeness.

references.quote_excerpt: Go for the overall effect, and don't get crazy over the shorter duration of a single note

modification_rules.name: More-Is-Less Arranging Restraint
modification_rules.summary: Prefer slow-moving bass, call-and-answer sequences, and selective harmonic devices over dense layering of all available techniques simultaneously.

references.quote_excerpt: here is a tune that isn't cluttered with every arranging device, but is still totally whole, missing nothing
In all of the tunes here I like to imply more with less. Chords, for instance, are most often only two or three notes, and the melody is all-important

systems.name: Jazz Harmonic Movement System
systems.summary: This system governs how harmonic motion is constructed and navigated in fingerstyle jazz arrangements. Its members are the three primary harmonic movement types the book identifies: diatonic (scale-step), cycle-of-fifths (descending perfect fifths), and chromatic (half-step root movement), plus the structural deployment contexts (intro, ending, turnaround, tag, modulation). Traversal rules describe how to move between chords using these types — particularly voice-leading by half or whole step rather than parallel root motion. Modification rules govern how individual chord members are altered at structural moments: intro gateway requirements, tag substitution, modulation methods, and the substitution of II–V7 for direct dominant motion. The system treats harmonic movement as several simultaneously moving voices rather than block-chord substitution.

members.name: Diatonic Harmony (Scale-Step Movement)
members.summary: Harmonic movement by whole and half steps within a single key.

members.name: Cycle of Fifths (Descending Perfect Fifth Movement)
members.summary: Harmonic movement descending by perfect fifths (or ascending fourths), the most basic harmonic movement of jazz.

members.name: Chromatic Root Movement (Half-Step Progression)
members.summary: Harmonic movement in which chord roots move by half steps, with diminished seventh as the standard connector chord for ascending sequences.

members.name: Structural Deployment Contexts
members.summary: Formal positions within a performance — intro, ending, turnaround, tag, and modulation — each with specific harmonic requirements.

members.name: Seventh Chord (Minimum Jazz Harmonic Unit)
members.summary: Four-note chords built by stacking thirds (1-3-5-7); the minimum harmonic unit of jazz harmony.

traversal_rules.name: Voice-Lead by Step Rather Than Root-Position Block Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer moving individual voices by half-step, whole-step, or mixed intervals over always placing the root in the bass; think of harmonic movement as several moving voices.

references.quote_excerpt: Always playing chords with the root in the bass works, but may not be as smooth or interesting as moving by half-step or whole-step intervals or mixed movement
If you think of harmonic movement as several moving voices rather than a glob of dots on a chord box grid, your arrangements and improvisations will be more airy, more interesting and less clunky

traversal_rules.name: Contrary Motion for V7–I Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: In V7–I progressions, accompany a descending top voice with an opposing (contrary) bass movement rather than parallel motion.

references.quote_excerpt: As the top note descends by half step the root note has its own opposing pattern, not parallel to the top

traversal_rules.name: Tritone Pull Drives V7–I Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: The tritone interval within a dominant seventh chord (b7 and 3) creates tension that resolves by half step to the tonic chord tones.

references.quote_excerpt: you can just feel the tug of the unresolved B' and E (the tritone interval of a-5) being pulled by the A and F in the resolved F chord

traversal_rules.name: Spread Texture: Bass Alternates Up a Fourth / Down a Fifth
traversal_rules.summary: Create a spread texture by alternating bass notes up a fourth and then down a fifth, producing a tenth interval from the melody above.

references.quote_excerpt: The root bass notes alternate in moving up a fourth and then down a fifth. Each new bass note is a tenth interval from the melody note above

traversal_rules.name: Use Chromatic Bass-Note Choice to Maintain Chromatic Continuity
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords of different qualities, select the bass voicing note (root, third, fifth) that continues a chromatic line rather than jumping to the root.

references.quote_excerpt: the lowest note of Gmaj7 in the first measure is D, the fifth, and the lowest note of G7(5) is b5. This was done to continue the effect of chromatic movement from Gmaj7 to G7(5) to Cmaj7

traversal_rules.name: Use Diminished Seventh as Connector for Ascending Chromatic Movement
traversal_rules.summary: When root movement ascends by half steps, insert a diminished seventh chord as the in-between connector chord.

references.quote_excerpt: In many cases the diminished seventh chord is the in-between, the hamburger helper of ascending harmonic movement

modification_rules.name: Intro Gateway: Final Chord Must Be V7 or Substitute
modification_rules.summary: Any intro must close on a V7 or V7 substitute chord to create the harmonic gateway into the first chord of the tune.

references.quote_excerpt: The last chord of the intro must be a V7 or V7 substitute in order to move into the first chord of the tune itself

modification_rules.name: Tag Initiation: Substitute V7 for Final I Chord
modification_rules.summary: To initiate a tag/coda, replace the final I chord with a V7 (or substitute) chord, extending the harmonic motion.

references.quote_excerpt: the replacement of the usual final I chord—E, here—with a V7 (or other substitute) chord is required

modification_rules.name: II–V7–I as Most Idiomatic Modulation Bridge
modification_rules.summary: When modulating between keys, use a II–V7–I of the new key as the smoothest and most idiomatic jazz bridge; this is preferred over bare V7 substitution or back-cycling alone.

references.quote_excerpt: the most common jazz progression unit is II-V7-I. Use it here then, by putting a II of the upcoming new key in front of the new key V-7

modification_rules.name: Reuse Song's Final Measures as Instant Intro
modification_rules.summary: The last four or eight measures of the tune itself, including its melody, can directly serve as an intro, reducing preparation overhead.

references.quote_excerpt: the last four- or eight measures of the tune itself can often be used, including the melody

modification_rules.name: Drop Root/Fifth for Rootless Tritone/Fourth Voicing in Ensemble
modification_rules.summary: In ensemble settings where the bassist supplies root and fifth, voice chords as alternating pairs of fourths and tritones (major/minor seventh to major third) without explicitly stating the root.

references.quote_excerpt: There is no explicitly stated root or fifth of the chord. If you play in a group, the bassist will cover roots and fifths

modification_rules.name: Two Additional Notes Suffice for a Full-Sounding Arrangement
modification_rules.summary: A melody accompanied by only two inner-voice notes produces a full arrangement; do not add density beyond what the ear requires.

references.quote_excerpt: A melody accompanied by two additional notes is often all you need to make an arrangement sound full, whether you are playing by yourself or with a group

modification_rules.name: Deceptive Cadence bVI Substitution for Modulation
modification_rules.summary: Insert a bVI chord after diatonic cycle movement to open alternative harmonic paths as a modulation device.

references.quote_excerpt: a VI (A-6) of the C scale is inserted. This deceptive cadence opens up several possibilities

systems.name: Self-Accompaniment Texture System
systems.summary: This system organizes the toolkit of self-accompaniment textures available to a solo fingerstyle jazz guitarist. Its members are the distinct bass-and-chord textures arrayed from simplest to most complex: root-movement bass, doubled bass, root/fifth alternation, walking bass with chromatic approach notes, arpeggiation, two-note inner voices, moving tenths, and bass-first walking-with-chord. Traversal rules govern when and how to move from one texture to another — primarily based on ensemble context (solo vs. group), melodic density, and stylistic period. Modification rules govern how individual textures are altered: applying anticipation/delay to any bass line, adjusting voicing register for ensemble, and choosing bass-first vs. chord-first conception. The system's governing aesthetic is that musical effect and coherence outrank mechanical interval adherence.

members.name: Root-Movement Bass
members.summary: Simplest accompaniment: bass notes match chord root names directly.

members.name: Doubled Bass Notes
members.summary: Two same bass notes per measure; swing-era upright bass style.

members.name: Root/Fifth Alternation
members.summary: Bass alternates between root and fifth of each chord; folk, country, and jazz oom-pah idiom.

members.name: Walking Bass with Chromatic Approach Notes
members.summary: Jazz walking bass line using chromatic approach notes to target each chord.

members.name: Arpeggiation
members.summary: Broken-chord texture appropriate for slower tunes; p plays lower strings, i/m play higher strings with free stroke.

members.name: Two-Note Inner-Voice Voicing
members.summary: Sparse two-note accompaniment producing full texture with minimal voice count.

members.name: Moving Tenths (Stride Piano Device)
members.summary: Sustained half-note or quarter-note tenths in the bass, adapted from swing-era piano practice.

members.name: Bass-First Walking-Bass-Plus-Chord
members.summary: Offset bass note preceding the chord by a quarter or eighth note; conceived bass-first for maximum harmonic flexibility.

traversal_rules.name: Conceive Bass Line Before Chord in Walking-Bass Texture
traversal_rules.summary: When building a walking-bass-plus-chord texture, plan or improvise the bass line first and add chords afterward; committing to chord fingering first severely restricts bass choices.

references.quote_excerpt: Think bass-plus-chord, not chord-plus-bass. It's best to conceive of a bass line first—planned or improvised—and then maybe add a chord

traversal_rules.name: Select Arpeggiation Texture for Slower-Moving Tunes
traversal_rules.summary: Choose arpeggiation as the accompaniment texture when the tune moves slowly; project the melody note clearly within the arpeggio.

references.quote_excerpt: For slower moving tunes, arpeggiation is a natural for fingerstyle playing. Don't forget to emphasize the melody note so that it won't get lost

traversal_rules.name: Voice High on Top Four/Five Strings When Electric Bass Present
traversal_rules.summary: In ensemble with electric bass, move all chord voicings to the upper four or five strings to avoid muddiness in the low register.

references.quote_excerpt: play your rhythm part, whether constant or comping, higher on the board on the top four or at most five strings

traversal_rules.name: Comping Density: More in Holes, Less When Soloist Is Active
traversal_rules.summary: When accompanying a soloist, increase harmonic/rhythmic density during the soloist's rests and reduce it when the soloist plays busily.

references.quote_excerpt: play more when the soloist is not playing, and play less when he is. Play more when there are 'holes' to be filled

modification_rules.name: Apply Anticipation or Delay to Bass Line for Rhythmic Variety
modification_rules.summary: Lead the bass note ahead of the beat (anticipation) or delay it behind the beat to avoid four-on-the-floor heaviness.

references.quote_excerpt: Leading with a bass note is a way to give variety to accompaniment instead of just chugging along with four bass notes to a measure
The opposite of leading with the lower line is trailing or delaying it. This too steers away from heaviness

modification_rules.name: Prioritize Effect Over Strict Interval Pattern
modification_rules.summary: When varying alternating bass patterns, maintaining the low-to-high alternation effect matters more than preserving the exact interval (root/fifth, root/seventh, etc.).

references.quote_excerpt: Keeping a strict pattern of root/seventh is not as important as long as the effect (low to high alternation) is there

modification_rules.name: Reframe Melody/Bass Overlap as Valid Arrangement Solution
modification_rules.summary: When the melody note merges with the top voice of a two-note bass accompaniment, treat this as a valid arrangement solution rather than a voice-leading problem to fix.

references.quote_excerpt: Often the melody may merge with and become the top part of the two note bass accompaniment. It's not a problem, it's a solution

modification_rules.name: Avoid Constant Bass Note Doubling in Modern Style
modification_rules.summary: Unless recreating vintage jazz, do not double bass notes constantly (two same notes per measure); modern style favors more variety.

references.quote_excerpt: Today the bass doesn't often play four quarter notes to the bar, especially during the first and last chorus. Unless you, as the guitarist/bassist, want to recreate vintage jazz, avoid constant doubling

masters.name: Barry Galbraith
masters.biography: Barry Galbraith (1919-1983) was an American jazz guitarist whose long studio career in New York from the 1940s through the 1970s made him one of the most-recorded jazz guitarists of his generation. His published work includes the *Jazz Solo Guitar* anthology — 42 fully notated chord-melody arrangements that teach by demonstration rather than by prose. Galbraith's approach is widely cited as foundational to the New York chord-melody tradition. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review.

principles.name: Arrangement-as-curriculum: the notated arrangement is itself the pedagogical content
principles.summary: Galbraith's *Jazz Solo Guitar* anthology teaches by demonstration rather than by prose. There is no narrative chapter structure, no labeled methodology, no glossary, and no explicit rules — yet the 42 arrangements constitute a complete pedagogical statement. Voicing choices, voice leading, chord substitutions, melodic harmonization, and bass-melody-harmony coordination are all encoded in the written notation. The implicit method must be inferred by attentive analysis of the music itself; the book offers no shortcuts. This is itself a principle: the arrangement IS the lesson, and the student is expected to absorb the principles through repeated playing, study, and comparison rather than through explanation.

references.source: Galbraith, Barry. Jazz Solo Guitar.
references.citation: Book-level structure: 42 notated arrangements without prose commentary, glossary, or stated methodology. The arrangements ARE the curriculum (STATEMENT.md What this book is + What it teaches).

principles.name: Notation-only corpus: the book is analytic source material, not a rule-bearing method
principles.summary: Galbraith's *Jazz Solo Guitar* is an analytic corpus — input to other masters' rule sets — rather than a rule-bearing master text in its own right. Its place in the catalog is honest: a notation-only anthology whose pedagogical system is implicit in the music. The chapter-loop registers a single `_pending:notation-only-implicit-system` placeholder rather than inventing rule references, acknowledging that the system is present in the music but cannot be structured from text alone. Resolving the placeholder would require notation-level analysis (voicing extraction, voice-leading graph construction, substitution lattice inference) across the 42 arrangements, or a curatorial decision to treat this work specifically as analytic corpus.

references.source: Galbraith, Barry. Jazz Solo Guitar.
references.citation: STATEMENT.md Pending Work + Provenance Notes: notation-only book, _pending:notation-only-implicit-system shell registered honestly, no rules invented.

works.title: Jazz Solo Guitar — 42 Chord Melody Arrangements
works.summary: Galbraith's performance anthology of 42 fully notated chord-melody arrangements of jazz standards for solo guitar — among them A Certain Smile, Alone Together, Autumn in New York, As Time Goes By, Have You Met Miss Jones?, I Cover the Waterfront, Embraceable You, The Christmas Song, Dancing on the Ceiling, Darn That Dream, and Dolphin Dance. There is no prose commentary, no labeled chapter structure, no glossary of techniques, and no stated methodology. Galbraith teaches by demonstration: the arrangements themselves are the curriculum. The pedagogical content — voicing choices, voice leading, chord substitutions, melodic harmonization, bass-melody-harmony coordination — is encoded entirely in the written notation. Whatever principles govern Galbraith's approach can only be inferred by sufficiently attentive analysis of the notated music itself. The chapter-loop produced a single placeholder system (`_pending:notation-only-implicit-system`) registering this honestly with empty members and rules — no rules were invented to fill the schema. Resolving the pending system would require notation-level analysis or a curatorial decision to treat this work as analytic corpus rather than rule-bearing master text.

references.source: Galbraith, Barry. Jazz Solo Guitar — 42 Chord Melody Arrangements. (date placeholder)
references.citation: Primary source.
