systems.name: Implicit Chord-Melody Arranging System (notation-only)
systems.summary: Galbraith's 42 Chord Melody Arrangements for Solo Guitar is a notation-only anthology — no prose, no labeled rules, no stated taxonomy. The pedagogical system it teaches is entirely implicit in the written arrangements: voice-leading choices, voicing densities, chord substitutions, inner-voice motion, and bass-melody-harmony coordination across 42 standards. Members, traversal rules, and modification rules cannot be extracted from text alone; they require direct musical analysis of the notation, which is out of scope for the prose-driven distillation pipeline. This system is recorded as a placeholder so downstream review can decide whether to commission notation-level analysis or treat this work as an analytic corpus rather than a rule-bearing master text.

masters.name: Gene Bertoncini
masters.biography: Gene Bertoncini (1937-) is an American jazz guitarist whose nylon-string chord-melody approach — heard on his long discography and in particular the duo recordings with bassist Michael Moore — is widely cited as foundational to the New York chord-melody tradition. *Arrangements for Solo Guitar*, transcribed by Jason Ennis and Chris Ullrich from his *Body and Soul* and *Quiet Now* CDs, documents 24 of his solo arrangements alongside a glossary of his characteristic open-string voicings. The book teaches by demonstration; its only prose appears in the Preface and the Transcribers' Notes. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review.

principles.name: Arrangement-as-curriculum: the notated arrangement is itself the pedagogical content
principles.summary: Bertoncini's *Arrangements for Solo Guitar* teaches by demonstration. There is no narrative chapter structure beyond the Preface and the Transcribers' Notes, no stated methodology, no labeled rules — yet the 24 arrangements + the Open-String Voicing Glossary constitute a complete pedagogical statement. Voicing choices, voice leading, textural orchestration, and use of unfretted strings as harmonic resource are all encoded in the written notation, transcribed faithfully from Bertoncini's own recordings. The implicit method must be inferred by careful study of the music; the book offers no shortcuts.

references.source: Bertoncini, Gene. Arrangements for Solo Guitar.
references.citation: 24 notated arrangements + 25th chapter Open-String Voicing Glossary; prose limited to Preface and Transcribers' Notes (STATEMENT.md What this book is).

principles.name: Open strings as orchestrational resource: unfretted strings are treated as independent harmonic voices
principles.summary: Bertoncini's nylon-string chord-melody approach treats open strings — particularly the open G and open B on standard tuning — as orchestrational resources, not just as convenience-of-fingering. The Open-String Voicing Glossary in the final chapter (ch25) catalogs 22 named voicings that exploit open strings as sustained harmonic textures, sympathetic resonance, register-extension devices, and contrapuntal counter-voices below or above the fretted melodic line. This is the most identifiable surface marker of his style and the most concrete pedagogical takeaway from the book.

references.source: Bertoncini, Gene. Arrangements for Solo Guitar.
references.citation: Chapter 25 (Glossary of Open-String Voicings, 22 cataloged voicings); applications throughout the 24 arrangements (STATEMENT.md Systems extracted).

works.title: Arrangements for Solo Guitar (from the Ambient Records CDs: Body and Soul and Quiet Now)
works.summary: Bertoncini's 24-arrangement notation folio + open-string-voicing glossary, transcribed by Jason Ennis and Chris Ullrich from Bertoncini's *Body and Soul* and *Quiet Now* CDs on Ambient Records. 81 pages: 24 fully notated chord-melody arrangements (chs 1-24) plus a 25-chapter (Glossary of Open-String Voicings) cataloguing 22 named voicings that exploit unfretted strings as harmonic resources. The book is a notation-only performance document with the only prose appearing in the Preface and the Notes about the Transcriptions and Notation by Jason Ennis. The chapter-loop produced two systems as MEMBER-ONLY taxonomies — Open-String Voicing Glossary (22 voicings) and Little-Orchestra Textural Roles (7 roles: melody, bass, harmony, counterpoint, register-shift, etc.) — registered honestly with empty traversal_rules and modification_rules because no source-quoted rules exist to anchor rule references.

references.source: Bertoncini, Gene. Arrangements for Solo Guitar. Transcribed by Jason Ennis, Chris Ullrich, Gene Bertoncini.
references.citation: Primary source. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-bertoncini-arrangements-solo-guitar; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/gene-bertoncini/arrangements-for-solo-guitar/. Notation-only folio; member-only taxonomic systems, no rules invented.

systems.name: Open-String Voicing Glossary
systems.summary: The book's most explicit organizing system, located in Chapter 25 (the Glossary of Open-String Voicings, pages 77-81). Bertoncini's distinctive practice is to incorporate ringing open strings as harmonic color inside chord voicings rather than stopping every note. The editors extracted these voicings from across the 24 arrangements and indexed them by chord label and source measure (e.g., The Shadow of Your Smile m.9 Em9; Body and Soul m.7 Dbmaj7(#11); 'Round Midnight m.3 Abm9; Edelweiss m.35 Ebmaj9; But Beautiful m.13 G13). Members below enumerate the cataloged voicings as documented by the Glossary index entries visible in the chapter-25 transcript. No prose passages backing traversal or modification rules exist in this folio, so the system is presented as a taxonomy only; rule extraction is deferred until either (a) a future pass works directly from notation or (b) external prose sources document the underlying voice-leading and substitution logic.

members.name: The Shadow of Your Smile — Em9 (m. 9)
The Shadow of Your Smile — A13 (mm. 19-20)
The Shadow of Your Smile — Bm9 (m. 39)
How Are Things in Glocca Morra — Em11 (mm. 31-32)
How Are Things in Glocca Morra — A13 (mm. 31-32)

systems.name: Little-Orchestra Textural Roles
systems.summary: Bertoncini's framing premise (stated in the book's Preface, attributed to Berlioz's description of the guitar as a 'little orchestra') treats the solo guitar as a polyphonic ensemble distributing musical material across distinct textural roles. The Preface names the roles and techniques explicitly: melody can be stated in the soprano voice, given to the bass voice, or nestled within the voicing; surrounding material uses counterpoint, cluster voicings, open-string voicings, and the special color of each individual string. The 24 arrangements (chapters 1-24) realize this taxonomy in notation. Because the Preface prose was not extracted as a numbered chapter (the TOC begins at the first arrangement), no per-chapter quotes back this system, and no traversal or modification rules are asserted here. The members below enumerate the textural roles the Preface names.

members.name: Melody stated in the soprano voice
members.summary: Default placement; melody on top of the voicing.

members.name: Melody given to the bass voice
members.summary: Mood/message-driven inversion of the default.

members.name: Melody nestled within an inner voice of the voicing
members.summary: Inner-voice melody, requiring the listener to track a middle line through the chord.

members.name: Per-string timbral color (the special color of each string)

masters.name: George Benson
masters.biography: Jazz guitarist and vocalist whose recording career spans from the Jack McDuff trio (early 1960s) through the soul-jazz crossover era of Breezin' (1976) to the vocal-pop work that defined his commercial peak. Originally trained as a bebop single-line player, Benson's harmonic vocabulary draws on tertian chord construction, the modal-substitution catalog of the natural-major harmonic field, and the chromatic color scales (harmonic minor, melodic minor, harmonic major) layered over diatonic progressions. The seven-volume George Benson Method (Jupiter Musical Group, 2019, co-authored with Peter Farrell) is the canonical written distillation of his playing approach. Vol. 1 (Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony) is the foundation; Vols. 2-7 build on it through advanced harmony, technique, approach tones, melodic-minor world, the Giant Lines, and the blues.

works.title: The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 — Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony
works.summary: Vol. 1 is a strict bottom-up curriculum that moves from notation literacy to a working theory of diatonic harmony on the guitar. Chapters 1-3 establish notational vocabulary, the diatonic/chromatic interval system, and the Circle of Fourths/Fifths as the system of key relationships. Chapter 4 — the densest chapter — constructs the chord universe by tertian stacking (Triads, Tetrads, Extended Chords). Chapters 5-6 organize those chords into harmonic fields and the seven modes and prescribe how to practice them. Chapter 7 extends the field idea to Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major as coloristic devices layered over existing chords rather than as progression engines. The book's load-bearing thesis: the fretboard is a matrix to be visualized chord-first, organized into four root-string zones (6th/5th/4th/3rd-strings, the last only for triads), anchored by bass-note Guide Notes.

references.source: Benson, George and Peter Farrell. The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 — Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony. Jupiter Musical Group Ltd., 2019.
references.citation: 319 PDF pages; book pages 1-292. Vol. 1 of a 7-volume series.

systems.name: Fretboard-as-Matrix Visualization System
systems.summary: Benson's load-bearing organizing system: the fretboard is a matrix to be navigated chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases derived from chord knowledge rather than the reverse. The Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the layout repeats at the 12th fret, and two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. Chord shapes are organized into four root-string zones (6th, 5th, 4th, and 3rd-string for triads only). A foundational methodological commitment runs through Chapters 6 and 7: locate the bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings first, and defer 4th-string voicings until those are mastered. The book also prescribes a Study Devices practice protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string ones) as a practice prescription that surrounds the system rather than functioning as a voicing-engine rule.

members.name: 6th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the low-E (6th) string.

members.name: 5th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the A (5th) string.

members.name: 4th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the D (4th) string; deferred until 6th/5th-string fluency.

members.name: 3rd-String Root Zone (triads only)
members.summary: Triad-only zone anchored on the G string.

members.name: Guide Notes (5th and 6th strings)
members.summary: Memorized natural-note anchors that serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape.

members.name: 12th-Fret Octave Mirror
members.summary: The fretboard layout repeats at the 12th fret, allowing octave-equivalent transfer of any shape.

traversal_rules.name: Visualization via Bass Note
traversal_rules.summary: Anchor chord study to roots on the 5th and 6th strings rather than to scale shapes; move between zones by relocating the bass root.

references.quote_excerpt: anchors chord study to roots on the 5th and 6th strings
locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings only

traversal_rules.name: Defer 4th-String Voicings Until 6th/5th Mastery
traversal_rules.summary: Master 6th- and 5th-string voicings before moving traversal into the 4th-string zone.

references.quote_excerpt: 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string ones
4th-string chords deferred until comfort

traversal_rules.name: 12th-Fret Octave Mirror
traversal_rules.summary: Any shape can be relocated by the 12-fret octave repeat as a position shift.

references.quote_excerpt: the fretboard repeats at the 12th fret

traversal_rules.name: Proximity-Based Chord Selection
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords, prefer voicings close to one another on the fretboard.

references.quote_excerpt: choosing chords close to one another for proximity

modification_rules.name: Learn Shapes and Intervals Together
modification_rules.summary: Shapes and intervals must be learned as a single unit, anchored by the two Guide Notes that serve as the Root reference.

references.quote_excerpt: shapes and intervals must be learned together

systems.name: Chord-Family System (Triads, Tetrads, Extended)
systems.summary: Chapter 4 partitions the chord universe into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). The signature device is the Chord Skeleton: pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants. Voicings are continually shaped by a load-bearing instrument constraint: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes.

members.name: Triads
members.summary: Four qualities (major, minor, diminished, augmented); three inversion positions (root, first, second).

members.name: Tetrads
members.summary: Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework.

members.name: Extended Chords
members.summary: 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions.

members.name: Suspended Chords
members.summary: sus2/sus4 defined strictly as 3rd-replacement, visualized by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes.

members.name: Chord Skeletons
members.summary: Pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants.

traversal_rules.name: Smallest-Movement Voice Leading
traversal_rules.summary: Between adjacent chords, prefer the voicing that moves each voice the smallest distance.

references.quote_excerpt: voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule

traversal_rules.name: Sus Chords Anchored to Dominant-7 Shapes
traversal_rules.summary: Move into a suspended voicing by replacing the 3rd within a known dominant-7 shape.

references.quote_excerpt: visualizing them by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes

traversal_rules.name: 4th-String Extended Voicings Derived From 6th/5th
traversal_rules.summary: Map 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes, typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord.

references.quote_excerpt: derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes

modification_rules.name: Drop 2 / Drop 3 Restricted to Tetrads
modification_rules.summary: Drop 2 and Drop 3 voicing transformations are restricted to tetrads.

references.quote_excerpt: Drop 2 / Drop 3 voicing transformations restricted to tetrads

modification_rules.name: No Inversions for Symmetric Chords
modification_rules.summary: Diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric.

references.quote_excerpt: diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric

modification_rules.name: Omit the 5th in 13th Chords
modification_rules.summary: In 13th-chord voicings the 5th is almost always omitted.

references.quote_excerpt: the 5th is almost always omitted in 13th chords

modification_rules.name: No b9 Between Bass and Top on Major/Minor
modification_rules.summary: On major and minor chords, avoid a flat-9 interval between the bass note and the top note of the voicing.

references.quote_excerpt: avoiding a flat-9 between bass and top note on major/minor chords

modification_rules.name: #11 vs Natural 11 Rule
modification_rules.summary: Use #11 on major and dominant 7 chords to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely.

references.quote_excerpt: use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd

modification_rules.name: Sus Is 3rd-Replacement Only
modification_rules.summary: Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2 or sus4), never as additions.

references.quote_excerpt: defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4)

modification_rules.name: Guitar Must Edit and Omit Notes
modification_rules.summary: Unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes; this constraint drives nearly every voicing choice.

references.quote_excerpt: the guitar must edit and omit notes

modification_rules.name: Unmarked 7 Is Minor (Maj7 Must Be Marked)
modification_rules.summary: By notation convention, an unmarked 7 means minor 7; a major 7th must be explicitly indicated.

references.quote_excerpt: an unmarked 7 is minor (major 7 must be indicated)

systems.name: Harmonic Field System (Diatonic Modes and Substitutions)
systems.summary: Chapter 5 builds the harmonic field by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords in Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm) and pairing each degree with one of the seven modes (Ionian through Locrian). Scale notes are classified as Chord Notes, Extended Notes, Avoid Notes, and Characteristic Notes. Chapter 6 then prescribes a degree-by-degree substitution catalog grounded in modal character; listed extensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components.

traversal_rules.name: V Dominant-to-Sus Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: On the V degree, substitute G7 with Gsus9/Gsus13 (Dm/G as mixolydian) when a suspended-dominant color is desired.

references.quote_excerpt: G7 to Gsus9/Gsus13, Dm/G as mixolydian

traversal_rules.name: IIIm Phrygian Slash-Chord Recasting
traversal_rules.summary: On the IIIm degree, recast as slash voicings like Cmaj7/E, C/E, and Esus(b9) including the Jobim/Villa-Lobos Em7(b9).

references.quote_excerpt: Em7(b6), Cmaj7/E, C/E, Esus(b9) and the Jobim/Villa-Lobos Em7(b9)

traversal_rules.name: VIIm Locrian Recast as G7(9)/B
traversal_rules.summary: Recast the VIIm Bo as G7(9)/B as the locrian-compatible substitute.

references.quote_excerpt: recasting Bo as G7(9)/B

traversal_rules.name: I Degree C6/Cmaj7 Interchange
traversal_rules.summary: On the I degree, freely interchange C6 and Cmaj7, with a b9-conflict exception when the melody is the octave.

references.quote_excerpt: C6/Cmaj7 interchange with a b9-conflict exception

modification_rules.name: Modes Drive Both Harmony and Melody
modification_rules.summary: Each chord's correct scale choice and available tensions are determined by the mode paired with its degree.

references.quote_excerpt: modes drive both harmony and melody by revealing each chord's tensions

modification_rules.name: Avoid Notes Usable Mid-Phrase, Never as Endings
modification_rules.summary: Avoid Notes are usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones.

references.quote_excerpt: usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones

modification_rules.name: Listed Extensions Are Melodic Options, Not Voicing Requirements
modification_rules.summary: Extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

references.quote_excerpt: listed extended notes are melodic passing-tone options, not mandatory voicing components
extended notes need not sit in the voicing

modification_rules.name: Characteristic Notes Define Modal Identity
modification_rules.summary: Each mode is identified by characteristic notes that should be present (e.g., Lydian's #11, Mixolydian's b7).

references.quote_excerpt: those that give the mode its identity

modification_rules.name: 9th Forbidden on the VIIm Locrian Chord
modification_rules.summary: Forbid the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note against the root.

references.quote_excerpt: forbidding the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note

modification_rules.name: IIm Dorian Extensions With m6 Deferred
modification_rules.summary: On the IIm dorian degree, add Dm7(9)/(11)/(13) but defer m6.

references.quote_excerpt: Dm7(9)/(11)/(13) with m6 deferred

modification_rules.name: VIm Aeolian Extensions and Slash Voicings
modification_rules.summary: On the VIm aeolian degree, allow Am7(9)/(11)/(b6) plus Dm7/A and Fmaj7/A slash voicings.

references.quote_excerpt: Am7(9)/(11)/(b6) plus Dm7/A and Fmaj7/A slash voicings

systems.name: Chromatic Color-Scale System (Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, Harmonic Major)
systems.summary: Chapter 7 builds its framework on the functional definition of the leading tone as the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale - the half-step tension the Natural Minor lacks. This absence motivated raising the natural minor's VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, then the Melodic Minor Scale (treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form), and finally Rimsky-Korsakov's Harmonic Major Scale. These parent families each generate seven scale modes - deliberately called scale modes rather than harmonic fields - because their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices layered over existing chords.

members.name: Harmonic Minor Scale
members.summary: Im with min 3rd / min 6th / maj 7th; restores the leading tone to the natural minor.

members.name: Melodic Minor Scale (Bach Scale form)
members.summary: Im with min 3rd / maj 6th / maj 7th, treated as one symmetric form.

members.name: Harmonic Major Scale
members.summary: Rimsky-Korsakov's mid-20th-century scale: major scale with b6.

members.name: VIIm Locrian bb7 (from Harmonic Major)
members.summary: Sources o7(b13) and (11) colors.

traversal_rules.name: Color Scales Layer Over Existing Chords
traversal_rules.summary: Derived chord families are not progression-guiding; introduce them by layering coloristic tension over an existing diatonic chord, not by substituting the chord's function.

references.quote_excerpt: coloristic devices that add tension on top of existing chords

modification_rules.name: Leading-Tone Restoration Generates Color Scales
modification_rules.summary: Raising the natural minor's VII restores the leading tone and motivates the Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major families as color sources.

references.quote_excerpt: raising its VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale

modification_rules.name: Each Derived Mode Specified by Avoid and Characteristic Notes
modification_rules.summary: Each derived scale mode is specified by its avoid notes and characteristic notes (e.g., Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th).

references.quote_excerpt: Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th

modification_rules.name: Extended Notes as Passing Tones, Not Voicing Requirements
modification_rules.summary: VERY IMPORTANT rule: extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

references.quote_excerpt: many belong only as passing tones in the melody

masters.name: Jimmy Bruno
masters.biography: Philadelphia-born jazz guitarist trained in the bebop tradition; sideman with Buddy Rich and Lionel Hampton in the 1970s before establishing his own quartet and a long pedagogical career. Bruno's published method centers on right-hand technique as a habit-formation problem: a three-rule directional picking system (down to higher string, up to lower, alternate on same string), justified by economy of motion, replaces strict alternate picking as the basis for jazz line playing. The Art of Picking (2002) is the canonical written distillation of this approach; companion volumes (notably Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist) supply the scale/fingering substrate the picking method is applied to.

principles.name: Three-rule directional picking — down to higher, up to lower, alternate same string
principles.summary: Bruno's central system. Three rules govern every pick stroke: down-stroke when crossing to a higher string, up-stroke when crossing to a lower string, alternate strokes when staying on one string. The justification is economy of motion — the pick is always positioned where it next needs to be. Bruno frames adoption as a deliberate break from the entrenched strict-alternate-picking habit and warns the student that the new motion feels awkward before paying off. The same rule extends without modification to non-adjacent (skipped) strings, all six major-scale fingerings, and arpeggios.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (core rules + economy-of-motion justification); Chapters 2-6 (uniform application across strokes, scales, arpeggios, string skipping).

principles.name: Fingerboard position dictates picking pattern (second-tier load-bearing rule)
principles.summary: Relocating an identical phrase to a different position changes which notes land on which strings, and the pick sequence must be redrawn accordingly. The picking pattern is not a property of the line; it is a property of the line-in-this-position. Combined with the directional rule, this means the student must internalize the rule deeply enough to re-derive picking on the fly when transposing position. It also means fingering choices (which position) have consequences for picking — fingering enables speed, not the reverse.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: Chapter 7 develops the principle explicitly with the 7th-position example showing the same phrase requires a different pick sequence.

principles.name: No-anchor, elbow-driven, relaxed right-hand mechanics
principles.summary: Mechanical preconditions for the directional system: the right hand has no anchor — fingers must not touch the strings, pick guard, or any other part of the guitar. Motion is driven from the elbow, not the wrist or fingers, and the wrist is held relaxed. These are not aesthetic preferences but the physical substrate that makes the directional rule executable; the system fails if the hand is anchored or the wrist is rigid. Bruno treats this as equal in importance to the directional rules themselves.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: Chapters 1-2 specify the no-anchor + elbow-driven + relaxed-wrist preconditions; reinforced throughout subsequent chapters.

principles.name: Music drives picking — sanctioned exceptions for musical intent
principles.summary: The aesthetic axiom: the picking mechanism serves musical intent, not fingerboard convenience. Bruno explicitly licenses rule-breaking under stylistic and rhythmic conditions: big-band accents permit consecutive down-strokes (or slurs as equivalent); rhythmic breaks license two consecutive downs; bebop prefers slurred over picked triplets ('more horn-like'); rests permit either starting stroke; the triplet drill exception (three down then three up) is confined to drill, not real phrases; Bruno himself prefers strict alternate down-up for the hardest skip+accent+in-between combination. The exceptions are not loopholes — they are the framework by which musical taste overrides mechanical convenience.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: Chapter 5 (music-drives-picking axiom); Chapters 3, 6, 7, 8, 9 (the catalog of sanctioned exceptions, each tied to a specific musical context).

works.title: The Art of Picking
works.summary: A compact picking-hand method built around three directional stroke rules — down when crossing to a higher string, up when crossing to a lower, alternate on the same string — justified by economy of motion. Chapters 1-2 establish the rules, the stroke-type vocabulary (down-up, up-down, in-between), and the no-anchor right-hand mechanics (elbow-driven motion, relaxed wrist, fingers off the strings/pick guard/body). Chapter 3 adds consecutive same-direction strokes as jazz vocabulary with a strict no-ringing constraint. Chapters 4-6 generalize the rule across textures: the six basic major-scale fingerings (V/H taxonomy: 6V2, 5V2, 6V4, 5V4, 6H2, 5H2), arpeggios (including Bruno's anti-sweep-picking polemic), and string skipping. Chapters 7-8 turn the mechanism toward musical material (bebop phrases, slurred-triplet articulation) and Chapter 9 codifies the sanctioned rule-breaks (big-band consecutive downs, rhythmic-break exceptions, rest-start stroke freedom). Chapter 10 closes with the meta-rule that picking is habit-formation, with repetition prescribed over analysis. The book's aesthetic axiom (Ch. 5): music drives picking, not the fretboard.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: 51-page method book; 10 chapters covering directional picking and its application to scales, arpeggios, string skipping, bebop phrasing, and articulations.

systems.name: Directional Picking
systems.summary: Bruno's central system: three directional stroke rules (down to higher string, up to lower string, alternate on same string) applied across all textures (scales, arpeggios, skips, bebop). Justified by economy of motion. The system enumerates the stroke-type vocabulary as members and codifies both the core rules and the sanctioned exceptions (rest start, triplet drill, big-band emphasis, rhythmic break, hard skip+accent override).

members.name: Down-up stroke
members.summary: Default stroke; trains downbeat accent.

members.name: Up-down stroke
members.summary: Inverted-accent stroke; upstroke carries the downbeat.

members.name: Mixed strokes
members.summary: Alternates accents between down and up.

members.name: In-between stroke
members.summary: Hardest stroke; pick moves between two strings (adjacent or skipped).

members.name: Consecutive down-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive down-strokes.

members.name: Consecutive up-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive up-strokes.

members.name: Same-string sixteenth reversal
members.summary: Direction reversal occurring on a single string with repeated notes.

traversal_rules.name: Down-stroke when crossing to a higher string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a higher string, use a down-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: When going to a higher string, use a down-stroke

traversal_rules.name: Up-stroke when crossing to a lower string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a lower string, use an up-stroke.

references.quote_excerpt: When going to a lower string, use an up-stroke

traversal_rules.name: Alternate strokes on the same string
traversal_rules.summary: When two consecutive notes lie on the same string, alternate down and up.

references.quote_excerpt: When playing on one string, use alternate strokes

traversal_rules.name: Economy of motion governs traversal
traversal_rules.summary: The pick lands where it next needs to be; minimize travel between notes on different strings.

traversal_rules.name: String-skip directional rule
traversal_rules.summary: Directional rule applies identically when skipping non-adjacent strings: down to higher, up to lower.

references.quote_excerpt: When skipping strings the same rule applies

traversal_rules.name: Directional rule applies across all six major-scale fingerings
traversal_rules.summary: Down-on-higher / up-on-lower governs every string crossing in the 6V2/5V2/6V4/5V4/6H2/5H2 fingerings.

references.quote_excerpt: six basic fingerings for the major scales

traversal_rules.name: Arpeggio traversal is the same directional rule
traversal_rules.summary: Sweep-style arpeggios are not a separate technique; they are the higher/lower rule on arpeggio notes.

picking for single lines is different than picking for arpeggios

traversal_rules.name: Fingerboard position dictates picking pattern
traversal_rules.summary: Relocating an identical phrase to a different position changes which notes land on which strings, thus changing the stroke sequence.

modification_rules.name: Triplet exception: three down then three up
modification_rules.summary: For repeating triplet arpeggio patterns across three adjacent strings (drill only), suspend alternation and use three consecutive downs then three consecutive ups.

three down-strokes followed by three up-strokes

modification_rules.summary: When a rest separates phrases, the next phrase can begin with either a down- or an up-stroke.

modification_rules.summary: To emulate big-band horn-section accents, use consecutive down-strokes to emphasize specific notes.

modification_rules.summary: The same big-band phrase may be articulated with slurs instead of consecutive down-strokes.

modification_rules.summary: When a rhythmic break separates notes, two consecutive down-strokes may be used instead of strict alternation.

masters.name: Peter Bernstein
masters.biography: Brooklyn-born jazz guitarist whose playing and teaching extend the post-bop lineage of Wes Montgomery, Grant Green, and Jim Hall. Sideman recordings with Lou Donaldson, Sonny Rollins, Diana Krall, and Larry Goldings situate his aesthetic in a melody-primary, narrative-driven approach to improvisation that treats jazz as a language rather than a vocabulary of scales. Bernstein teaches privately and at NYU; the 2023 master's thesis by Nico Moreno (also at NYU) is the canonical written distillation of his teaching, transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings. The masters.json entry is sourced from the Moreno thesis, not from a Bernstein-authored method.

principles.name: Jazz as language — narrative outranks correctness
principles.summary: Bernstein's load-bearing conceptual frame: improvisation is language use, not scale deployment. Every concept is anchored to a specific tune rather than presented as abstract harmonic theory — the standard is a poem and the melody is its story. Vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak (listening and imitation); the unit of practice is the small phrase, not the transcribed chorus. The evaluative criterion is whether the line tells a story, not whether the notes are 'correct.' Contrast, silence, and rhymes are the narrative grammar; target notes are the structural landing points. This inverts academic chord-scale-drill pedagogy, which Bernstein names as the failure the method exists to correct.

references.source: Moreno, Eric. *An Approach to Jazz Improvisation: A Method by Peter Bernstein*. NYU MM thesis, 2023.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (oral tradition / no formal system); Chapter 2 (jazz-as-language analogy); Chapter 8 (narrative-outranks-correctness aesthetic stated explicitly); Chapter 9 (contrast / silence / rhyme as narrative grammar).

principles.name: Melody is primary — Step 1 before Step 12
principles.summary: Bernstein's load-bearing pedagogical rule: melody is primary and harmony exists to serve it. The first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument — attending to lyric syllables as articulation cues — BEFORE any harmonic embellishment. A player must not rush to 'step 12' (harmonic dressing) before completing 'step 1' (melody mastery). Target notes operationally link melody and harmony: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone, the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice. Worked example: Dm6 vs Dm7 — if the melody lands on b7, the b7 overrides the default minor-tonic Dm6. The 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel.

references.source: Moreno, Eric. *An Approach to Jazz Improvisation: A Method by Peter Bernstein*. NYU MM thesis, 2023.
references.citation: Chapter 4 codifies the rule and the Dm6/Dm7 worked example; reasserted across Chapters 5, 8.

principles.name: Dressing the notes — root-3-7 first, extensions only after the relationship is heard
principles.summary: The melody-first hierarchy applied to accompaniment: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important. Concrete practice rule: begin with root-3-7 shell voicings only, internalize their relationship to the melody, add extensions only after that relationship is heard. Two-phase comping-construction procedure: (1) design a guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody, then (2) fill inner voices to dress those guide tones. Tension and resolution are generated even inside a single sustained chord. Rhythm in comping is elevated to equal status with voicing choice. Two contrarian harmonic moves licensed: under-resolving the I chord and tritone-distance ii-V over a resolving dominant — both treated as forward-motion devices.

references.source: Moreno, Eric. *An Approach to Jazz Improvisation: A Method by Peter Bernstein*. NYU MM thesis, 2023.
references.citation: Chapter 5 (dressing-the-notes metaphor + root-3-7 → extensions order); Chapter 6 (chords as tools, not destinations); Chapter 8 (tritone ii-V over resolving dominant).

principles.name: Vocabulary acquisition by listening + imitation; practice away from the instrument
principles.summary: Improvisation is recombination of known phrases under a syntax, not real-time token generation. Vocabulary is built the way children learn to speak — by listening and imitation. Practice happens away from the instrument so imagination leads and the fingers follow; lessons are taught orally without lead sheets. Tritone ii-V substitution and three named variation methods are taught as recombination operators on existing phrases, not as exercises in isolation. Target-note practice anchors the player's recombination to the tune's structural landings. Permissive chromatic stance: any note can be made to work if it is delivered with intention and arrival.

references.source: Moreno, Eric. *An Approach to Jazz Improvisation: A Method by Peter Bernstein*. NYU MM thesis, 2023.
references.citation: Chapter 7 (tritone ii-V + variation methods + target-note practice + permissive chromatic stance); Chapter 3 (tunes outranking solos, oral transmission).

works.title: An approach to Jazz Improvisation: A method by Peter Bernstein (as documented by Nico Moreno, NYU 2023)
works.summary: A master's-thesis-shaped distillation of Bernstein's private and NYU lessons, transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings. The document organizes Bernstein's teaching into eight conceptual themes: jazz as a language and the importance of narrative; how to learn a tune; the primacy of the melody; the relation between chords and melody; language, rhythm, and meaning; comping and supporting the melody; harmonic movement, tension, and substitutions; and expanding the vocabulary (creating ideas around target notes, creating ideas from a shape, the narrative of the solo). The thesis is explicit that Bernstein's own teaching lacks a strict methodical structure and that every concept is contextualized in specific musical material rather than presented as standalone theory — the document supplies the structure that Bernstein's informal pedagogy intentionally does not.

references.source: Moreno, Nico. "An approach to Jazz Improvisation: A method by Peter Bernstein." Master's Thesis, NYU Jazz Studies Program, 2023. Supervisor: Dr. David Schroeder.
references.citation: 30-page master's thesis transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings; covers eight conceptual themes plus closing remarks on Bernstein's aesthetic path.

systems.name: Melodic Narrative System (Jazz as Language)
systems.summary: Bernstein's load-bearing conceptual system: improvisation is language use, not scale deployment. Every concept is anchored to a specific tune rather than to an abstract harmonic exercise; the standard is a poem and the melody is its story. The system's central aesthetic - connected narrative outranks correctness-of-notes - unifies vocabulary acquisition (by listening and imitation), the practice unit (small phrases, not transcribed choruses), and the evaluative criterion for any played line (does it tell a story?). Contrast, silence, and rhymes are the narrative grammar; target notes are the structural landing points around which lines are built.

members.name: Tune-as-Poem

members.name: Melody-as-Story

members.name: Contrast

members.name: Silence
members.summary: Spaces between notes serve as punctuation between ideas.

members.name: Rhyme
members.summary: Motifs connected across time via call-and-response or parallel tension-resolution.

The order-of-operations is explicit - the first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument, with attention to lyric syllables as a guide to articulation, before any harmonic embellishment. Target notes are the operational link between the melody and the harmony: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone, the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice. The 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel.

systems.name: Comping Aesthetic System (Dressing the Notes)
systems.summary: Applies the melody-first hierarchy to accompaniment via the signature 'dressing the notes' metaphor: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important. The concrete practice rule is to begin with root-3-7 voicings only, internalize their relationship to the melody, and add extensions only after that relationship is heard. The chapter prescribes a two-phase comping-construction procedure - design a guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody, then fill the inner voices to dress those guide tones. Tension and resolution are generated even inside a single sustained chord, and rhythm in comping is elevated to equal status with voicing choice.

systems.name: Vocabulary Development System (Recursive Variation and Target-Note Practice)
systems.summary: The apparatus by which a player expands the internalized library of phrases that improvisation recombines. Vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak - by listening and imitation - and creativity is defined operationally as recursive variation: ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those. The chapter enumerates three named variation methods (ear-led free variation, phrases built around a single target note, phrases around two simultaneous target notes) and pairs them with a permissive chromatic stance under the 'party for the target' analogy: diatonic notes are family, chromatic notes are friends and neighbors. The system closes with two practice meta-rules - practice away from the instrument so imagination leads the fingers, and learn classic lines first then progressively abstract them.

members.name: Ear-Led Free Variation

masters.name: Mickey Baker
masters.biography: Kentucky-born guitarist whose recording career bridged the 1950s R&B/early-rock-and-roll session world (Mickey & Sylvia's "Love Is Strange", Atlantic and Savoy sessions for Ray Charles, Big Joe Turner, Ruth Brown, LaVern Baker) and the Paris-based jazz expat community where he lived from the 1960s onward. The Complete Course in Jazz Guitar (Lewis Music Publishing Co., 1955) — universally known as Mickey Baker's Vol. 1 — is among the most-cited and most-used jazz-guitar method books ever written; its 26 lessons introduce the working-musician's chord vocabulary (the "Baker chords"), substitution lattice, comping and rhythm patterns, and single-note line vocabulary as a unified practical system rather than a theoretical exposition. A Volume 2 followed.

principles.name: Numbered, finite chord vocabulary + universal transposition mandate
principles.summary: Baker prunes the guitar's chord universe to 26 essential shapes (Chapter 1), later extended to 30 (Chapter 5), and references every voicing by number rather than by Roman-numeral function. The mechanical definition of mastery is transposition: every voicing must be physically rewritten — by hand — into a manuscript notebook in every key, with practical target lists (seven keys for vamps, six for intros, five horn-friendly keys for blues runs) contracting the universal mandate to working-guitarist reality. Drill is chromatic up-and-down-the-neck, with two governing positions per key (additional 3rd-fret position for Ab/Bb/C/Db/Eb).

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar Vol. 1. Lewis Music Publishing, 1955.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (26-chord catalog + transposition mandate); Chapter 4 (two-positions-per-key); Chapter 5 (Chords 27-30, key-specific); Chapter 12 (additional 3rd position).

principles.name: Substitution lattice: two-names + relative-minor + tritone-equivalent + dominant-as-connector
principles.summary: Four named substitution principles co-operate as a lattice. (1) Two-names: one shape carries two functional identities (Gma6 = Emi7, Ama6 = Fmi7), internalized under both names in all keys. (2) Dominant-to-relative-minor: any dominant-7 can be replaced by the minor a fifth above it (G7 by Dmi7), drilled as a 12-row written chart C7→B7. (3) Root-omitted tritone-equivalent voicing: G7#5 = Db9b5 — same shape, different root. (4) Dominant-as-leading-chord: every chord-to-chord connection can be built by inserting the appropriate dominant between them, organized into three categories (dominant-to-dominant, dominant-to-minor, dominant-to-major). Load-bearing constraint: substitutions must serve voice-leading and an actual progression, never be 'thrown in' for color.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar Vol. 1. Lewis Music Publishing, 1955.
references.citation: Chapter 4 (relative-minor chart); Chapter 7 (two-names + tritone-equivalent); Chapter 5 + Lesson 16 (dominant-as-leading-chord, flagged as 'the key to all of the other lessons').

principles.name: Adaptable voicings and moving textures displace strumming
principles.summary: The book's central architectural polemic: static two-bar rhythm strumming is 'corny'; what makes the music jazz is motion through adaptable voicings. Two textures replace strumming as the comping default — arpeggio comping and walking-string-bass — and intros, vamps, and fill-ins are taught explicitly as motion devices that prevent the texture from stalling. Open-string-root horn-backing voicings (Chords 27-30) are the vamp-shape vocabulary; the two-line standard-vs-new format (original changes above, Baker's reharmonization below) is the visual signature of the substitution-driven motion-first philosophy.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar Vol. 1. Lewis Music Publishing, 1955.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (no-strumming polemic); Chapter 2 (arpeggio + walking-string-bass textures); Chapter 4 (vamps, fill-ins); Chapter 5 (Chords 27-30 horn-backing voicings).

principles.name: Relative-run substitution unifies chord study with solo study
principles.summary: Part Two extends the relative-substitution principle from chords into single-note runs: an Ami7 run can be played over D7, a Gmi7 run over C7. The same lattice (chord = relative chord; minor a fifth above the dominant) that licenses chord substitution in Part One also licenses run substitution in Part Two — so the player's vocabulary doubles in coverage without doubling in memorization cost. Riffs are introduced as 'ideas put together and forced against the chord' — structurally justified for vamp changes that move too fast for chord-tone runs. Solo construction (Chapter 13) closes with two methods: chords run around the implied melody under a 7th-11th-fret position lock, and the solo pattern as a per-course rhythmic frame.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar Vol. 1. Lewis Music Publishing, 1955.
references.citation: Chapter 8 (alternate picking + 28-exercise mechanical foundation); Chapters 9-10 (scale/minor/dominant/diminished runs + relative-substitution extended to runs); Chapter 11 (riffs); Chapter 13 (solo construction methods).

works.title: Mickey Baker's Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Volume 1
works.summary: 26-lesson method book that introduces the jazz-guitar working-musician vocabulary as a practical system. The book is famous for its by-ear-first, voicing-first approach: Lessons 1-7 establish the core chord vocabulary (the "Baker chords" — major 6/major 7, minor 6/minor 7, dominant 7 + extensions 9, 13, +5, -5, sus); Lessons 8-12 build the harmonic-substitution lattice (relative-name pairings like Gma6 = Emi7, tritone and diminished subs, walking-bass lines); Lessons 13-18 develop comping rhythm patterns and vamp textures; Lessons 19-26 cover single-note line vocabulary, riff construction, and the deliberate use of lines that are 'foreign' to the underlying changes. The book's aesthetic axiom is practical-over-theoretical: sound-driven content selection, working-musician-tested voicings, and explicit rejection of formula-derived harmonic abstraction. A Volume 2 followed and could be added as a sibling work in a later run.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Volume 1. Lewis Music Publishing Co. (Amsco / Music Sales), 1955.
references.citation: 65-page method book; 26 numbered lessons. OCR-sourced via the cyberpower OCR branch (#315/#316/#318); transcripts retain tesseract+qwen2.5vl artifacts that survive into chapter quotes (see chapter-file frontmatter).

systems.name: Numbered Chord Vocabulary System (the 26-to-30 Catalog)
systems.summary: Baker's load-bearing taxonomic system: the guitar's chord universe is pruned to 26 essential shapes (Chapter 1), later extended to 30 by the addition of open-string-root horn-backing voicings (Chapter 5, Chords 27-30). Chord shapes are referenced by number (No. 1 through No. 30) rather than by Roman-numeral function, drilled chromatically up and down the neck so the same shape is internalized in every key, and cross-referenced via circled-number annotations over every progression diagram from Lesson 1 onward. The first six chords (No. 1-6) are restricted to the four foundational keys (F, G, Ab, Bb) and form the rhythm palette of Part One; the 9-12 group opens the second three-key cluster (C, Db, Eb) and installs the substitution menu (ma7/6/9, mi6/7/9, 13, 9, 7#5+9, 13#5+9, 7b5, 11, 13b9); and Chords 27-30 are open-string-root vamp shapes flagged as key-specific exceptions to the otherwise-universal transposition mandate. The system's operating principle is transposition-as-mastery: every voicing must be physically rewritten into the student's manuscript notebook in every key, with practical target lists (seven keys for vamps, six for intros, five horn-friendly keys for blues runs) contracting the universal mandate to working-guitarist reality.

systems.name: Harmonic Substitution Lattice (Two-Names, Relative-Minor, Tritone, Dominant-as-Connector)
systems.summary: Baker's harmonic engine: a small set of named substitution principles that drive every reharmonization in the book and license the relative-run substitutions that unify chord study with solo study in Part Two. Four principles co-operate as a lattice. The two-names principle: one shape carries two functional identities (Gma6 = Emi7, Ama6 = Fmi7), internalized under both names in all keys (Chapter 7). The dominant-to-relative-minor substitution: any dominant-7 can be replaced by the minor a fifth above it (G7 by Dmi7, D7 by Ami), drilled as a twelve-row written chart from C7 through B7 (Chapter 4). The root-omitted tritone-equivalent voicing: G7#5 and Db9b5 are the same shape with different roots - the structural mechanism behind Baker's b5 substitutions (Chapter 7). The dominant-as-leading-chord principle: every chord-to-chord connection can be built by inserting the appropriate dominant between them, organized into the three categories dominant-to-dominant, dominant-to-minor, and dominant-to-major (Chapters 5, 6). Lesson 16 is flagged by Baker as 'the key to all of the other lessons.' The lattice carries a load-bearing constraint - substitutions must serve voice-leading and an actual progression, never be 'thrown in' for color (Chapter 3) - and a load-bearing aesthetic: static two-bar strumming is 'corny' and motion through adaptable voicings is what makes the music jazz.

systems.name: Comping and Vamp Texture System (Arpeggio, Walking Bass, Fill, Intro)
systems.summary: Baker's accompaniment system: rhythm strumming is obsolete and 'corny,' and must be replaced by motion through adaptable voicings (Chapter 3). Two characteristic textures are elevated above strumming - arpeggio and walking string-bass (Chapter 2) - and three short-form units are codified as the working accompaniment library: intros, vamps, and fill-ins. The system's operating principle is combinatorial creativity: a small fixed chord set generates unlimited intros if the student varies rhythm (root-then-chord, arpeggiated, recombined). Vamps use the open-string-root horn-backing voicings (Chords 27-30) and key-shift the practical study from C to G. Fill-ins are dispatched on labeled run-types so the student scaffolds improvisation by selecting from the named run vocabulary. The texture system is the rhythmic engine that lets the substitution lattice actually move on the instrument.

systems.name: Single-Note Line and Riff System (Position-Locked Runs, Relative-Run Substitution, Forced Riff)
systems.summary: Baker's improvisational system: single-note technique is built directly out of the exercise vocabulary, so practice and performance share a single substrate. Chapter 8 installs alternate picking and a 28-exercise mechanical foundation; Chapters 9-10 drill scale, minor, dominant, and diminished runs and narrow the blues to five horn-friendly keys (F/G/Ab/Bb/C). The system carries a load-bearing improvisational license - the relative-run substitution: a minor-7 run substitutes freely for its relative major chord because the same relative rule that applies to chords applies to runs (Ami7 run over D7, Gmi7 run over C7). Chapter 11 introduces a parallel melodic unit, the riff, defined as 'ideas put together and forced against the chord' - structurally justified for fast-moving vamp changes where chord-tone runs cannot keep up. Chapter 12 codifies the groove riff: same riff repeated, first and last courses matching, executed without leaving 3rd position. Chapter 13 closes with two solo-construction methods - the implied-melody method (run chords around the melody under a 7th-11th-fret position lock so the listener still hears the tune even when it is not literally present) and the solo pattern (a per-course rhythmic frame where the first measure sets the timing for everything that follows). Internalization is operationalized through the sing-against-the-chord rule: riffs and runs are sung until they are heard before they are played.

works.title: Mickey Baker's Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Book 2
works.summary: Cumulative harmonic curriculum that carries the guitarist from discrete voicing shapes to integrated chord-melody performance. Four ascending phases: vocabulary (Upper Partial chord forms, moving bass line, passing tones), harmonic syntax (II-V-I as gravitational center, chord cycle resolutions, relative-chord substitution, Forced Harmony), Symmetric Harmony (Symmetric Cycles, Augmented Cycles, Pedal Point, Cadence Break as a parallel harmonic universe free of diatonic key centers), and chord-melody integration (chord analysis, diminished/augmented substitutions, Counterpoint). The final three chapters are notated chord-melody solos demonstrating the integrated method in performance.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Book 2. Lewis Music Publishing, 1959.
references.citation: Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-baker-vol-2; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/mickey-baker/complete-course-vol-2/.

systems.name: Functional Diatonic Harmony System
systems.summary: The central harmonic system of the method, organized around the II-V-I resolution chain. Chord forms (Upper Partial voicings) are the members; traversal is governed by dominant-to-tonic pull and the insertion of relative/substitute chords that must always arrive at V before resolving to I. Modification rules expand the dominant and II chord through upper partials (7th, 9th, 11th, aug. 11th, 13th, flat-5, flat-9), permit diminished and augmented chords as dominant substitutes, and license Forced Harmony as a device for harmonic activity. The system culminates in chord-melody arranging, where melody priority can override harmonic completeness.

members.name: Tonic (I) Chord
members.summary: Harmonic destination and point of rest; the ear is satisfied once I is reached.

members.name: Subdominant (IV) Chord
members.summary: Intermediate chord in the I-IV-V-I cycle; demands temporary resolution toward V.

members.name: Dominant (V) Chord
members.summary: Most active chord of the tonality; all cycle motion gravitates toward V before resolving to I.

members.name: Supertonic (II) Chord
members.summary: Prepares the dominant; can substitute for V; carries its own upper partials.

members.name: Upper Partial Voicings (Chord Forms)
members.summary: Extended voicing shapes: 9th, 11th, aug. 11th, 13th, flat-9 dominants, 6th and 9th tonic forms.

members.name: Diminished 7th as Dominant Substitute
members.summary: Root-omitted flat-9 dominant reframed as diminished; resolves to tonic in place of V.

members.name: Augmented Chord as Dominant Substitute
members.summary: Augmented triad/7th functioning as altered dominant resolving to tonic.

members.name: Passing Tone
members.summary: Voice-leading mechanism (R/P notation) connecting 9th to 6th chord forms.

traversal_rules.name: Dominant-to-Tonic Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: All chord cycles, however elaborated, must eventually reach V and resolve to I.

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Fingerboard Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Every exercise must be transposed chromatically up and down the full neck in both major and minor modes.

references.quote_excerpt: practice them chromatically, moving up and down the finger board

references.topic: V to I cadence (dominant to tonic)
references.quote_excerpt: Practice this chromatically up and down the fingerboard.

modification_rules.name: Upper Partials Dominant Alteration
modification_rules.summary: The dominant chord may be extended to 7th/9th/11th/aug.11th/13th; the 5th or 9th may be flatted; all remain Upper Partials and resolve to the tonic.

references.quote_excerpt: The dominant chord can be the dominant 7th, 9th, 11th, aug. 11th or 13th. The 5th or the 9th can be Flatted

modification_rules.name: Diminished 7th as Dominant Substitute
modification_rules.summary: A diminished 7th chord functions as the upper structure of a flat-9 dominant and may replace V in V-I resolution.

references.quote_excerpt: They are the Upper tones to a diminished or flatted 9th chord.
the diminished chord instead of the dominant, resolves into the tonic.

modification_rules.name: Forced Harmony Expansion
modification_rules.summary: Additional chord progressions that move away from and back to the tonic are inserted to keep the music active.

references.quote_excerpt: Forced Harmony (adding chords via progressions that move away from, then lead back into the tonic.)

modification_rules.name: Melody-Priority Chord Omission
modification_rules.summary: In chord-melody arranging, chords must be omitted when they conflict with the melodic line.

references.quote_excerpt: chords are forced against the melody and, at some points, even dropped for a better melodic line.

modification_rules.name: Player Ear as Substitution Authority
modification_rules.summary: The player's ear governs substitution choices; jazz arrangements characteristically diverge from original progressions.

references.quote_excerpt: musical sounds that sound best to you rather than the ideas and chord cycles that were originally written
The original chord progressions of the song are routine and much is left to your imagination.

modification_rules.name: Non-Standard Voicing Creation for Melody Fit
modification_rules.summary: When no familiar shape harmonizes the melody, invent a voicing that fits the line and blends with the surrounding cycle.

references.quote_excerpt: it becomes necessary to create chords that will fit the melody and also blend well musically with the chord cycles

systems.name: Symmetric Harmony System
systems.summary: A parallel harmonic universe (Chapter 13) that operates without a diatonic key center. Every chord functions as a tonic; harmonic motion distributes equally across the 12-tone scale via minor-third (T1-T4) or major-third (Augmented Cycle T1-T3) division. The diminished 7th chord is the emblematic vehicle. Symmetric Harmony is applied at cadences, phrase endings (Cadence Breaks), and introductions. Traversal rules govern direction and interval of fingerboard transposition; modification rules permit partial chord fragments, retrograde motion, and tonic-count expansion to 6 or 12 by inserting intermediate chords.

members.name: Diminished 7th Chord (4-tonic symmetric vehicle)
members.summary: Built on successive minor thirds; all four tones are enharmonically equivalent tonics.

members.name: Augmented Triad / Augmented 7th (3-tonic symmetric vehicle)
members.summary: Built on major thirds; major-third transposition reproduces the same pitch-class set.

members.name: Symmetric Cycle Position (T1-T4)
members.summary: One of four equal fingerboard positions reached by minor thirds.

members.name: Augmented Cycle Position (T1-T3)
members.summary: One of three equal positions reached by major thirds.

members.name: Chord Fragment
members.summary: Partial voicing executing the minor-third glissando in place of the full chord.

members.name: Cadence Break Slot
members.summary: Structural one-to-two-bar pause at phrase endings where Symmetric Harmony is deployed.

traversal_rules.name: Minor-Third Symmetric Cycle Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Any chord shape is transposed up or down the fingerboard in minor thirds (T1-T4), maintaining the same left-hand shape.

references.quote_excerpt: Only the first chord will be diagrammed and those to follow ... are a minor third higher.
a diminished 7th chord which moves freely up and down the finger board in minor thirds.

traversal_rules.name: Major-Third Augmented Cycle Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Augmented chord shapes are transposed in major thirds, dividing the 12-tone scale into three equal parts.

references.quote_excerpt: Divide the 12 tone scale in three equal parts and you get three tonics, T. 1, 2, and 3.
When this chord is moved up a major third on the finger board, the same tones appear.

traversal_rules.name: Directional Glissando Rule (High-Down, Low-Up)
traversal_rules.summary: If a phrase ends high on the fingerboard move the cycle downward in minor thirds; if it ends low, move it upward.

references.topic: Applying Symmetric Cycle to existing material
references.quote_excerpt: If the last chord ends high on the finger board, move it down in minor thirds, if it ends low, move it up.

traversal_rules.name: Retrograde Cycle Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Symmetric Cycle exercises are practiced backwards (T4-T3-T2-T1) to build descending facility.

references.quote_excerpt: Play the exercises below backwards, starting with T1 then 4, 3, 2 and back to the 1.

traversal_rules.name: Parallel Motion / Endpoint-Only Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Only the start and end points of the motion matter; harmony moves strictly in parallel up or down.

references.quote_excerpt: Harmony is parallel either up or down and you can have as many as 12 tonics

modification_rules.name: Tonic Count Expansion (6 or 12 Tonics)
modification_rules.summary: The 3-tonic Augmented Cycle expands to 6 tonics by inserting intermediate chords, or up to 12 via full Symmetric Harmony.

references.quote_excerpt: now we have six tonics, the additional tonics having been forced into the cycle.

modification_rules.name: Chord Fragment Substitution in Symmetric Cycle
modification_rules.summary: Partial chord voicings may execute the minor-third glissando in place of the full chord.

references.quote_excerpt: It is possible to use only fragments of the chord rather than its entirety

modification_rules.name: Non-Diatonic Color Chord Insertion
modification_rules.summary: Chords absent from a song's original structure may be inserted to add color and originality.

references.quote_excerpt: many chords are used that don't appear in the original chord structure of a song. They are used to add color and originality.

modification_rules.name: Cadence Break Harmonization
modification_rules.summary: The Cadence Break is filled with Symmetric Cycles, Pedal Point, or original chord progressions.

references.quote_excerpt: Symetric Cycles, Pedal Point and other types of original chord progressions may be employed at these points.

systems.name: Counterpoint and Chord-Melody System
systems.summary: The culminating system, framing prior learning as harmonic support for independent melodic lines. Counterpoint is the movement of two or more independent melodies — Cantus Firmus and Counter melody — rather than chord harmony. Members include the two melodic voices, arpeggiated bass voice, and Pedal Point. Traversal rules govern rhythmic contrast between voices and the requirement that chordal motion avoids bad harmonic movement. Modification rules govern arpeggiated bass extraction as counter-melody, Pedal Point distribution across ensemble, and chord omission for melody priority.

members.name: Cantus Firmus (Original Melody)
members.summary: Primary melodic voice against which the counter melody moves.

members.name: Counter Melody (Added Voice)
members.summary: Second independent melodic line; may be extracted as the first note of each arpeggiated chord.

members.name: Arpeggiated Bass Voice
members.summary: Lowest note of each arpeggiated chord, treated as independent melodic line.

members.name: Pedal Point
members.summary: Sustained single pitch (canonically the fifth of the scale) held while harmonies shift above it.

traversal_rules.name: Rhythmic Contrast Between Voices
traversal_rules.summary: One voice sustains (up to four beats) while the other moves in quarter or eighth notes.

references.quote_excerpt: one melody sustains itself for as many as 4 beats, while the other line moves along in quarter or eighth notes.

traversal_rules.name: Harmonic Integrity Constraint
traversal_rules.summary: Melodic independence must never compromise the harmonic foundation; examples avoid bad harmonic movement.

references.quote_excerpt: there is never a sign or sound of bad harmonic movement.

traversal_rules.name: Pedal Point Ensemble Distribution
traversal_rules.summary: Bass plays the pedal tone; guitar or piano executes chord variations above; a melody instrument carries the song's line.

references.quote_excerpt: the bass plays the Pedal Point while the piano or guitar plays the chord variations.
bass playing Pedal Point, the guitar playing the chord variations and the voice or solo instrument carrying the melody

modification_rules.name: Arpeggiated Bass Counter-Melody Extraction
modification_rules.summary: Only the first (lowest) note of each arpeggiated chord is retained to form a sustaining counter-melody line.

references.quote_excerpt: only the first note in each chord of the counter melody is used. This is a sustaining type of counter melody
the first note of each chord is the most important. It forms a melodic line at the base of the chord

modification_rules.name: Pedal Point Fifth-of-Scale Canonical Choice
modification_rules.summary: The fifth of the scale is the canonical sustained pedal tone.

references.quote_excerpt: The most popular note in Pedal Point is the fifth of the scale.
Pedal Point on D, the V of Gmaj.

modification_rules.name: Chord Omission for Melody Priority
modification_rules.summary: In chord-melody arranging, chords conflicting with the melodic line must be dropped.

references.quote_excerpt: chords are forced against the melody and, at some points, even dropped for a better melodic line.
